chapters.summary: Linda Lee welcomes attendees to the fourth day of FY27 Executive Budget Hearings, introducing the joint hearing on DOT, DCWP, and EDC budgets, and recognizing colleagues and Commissioner Mike Flynn.
chapters.title: Chair Lee opens FY27 Executive Budget Hearing for DOT

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: At this time, no food or beverages are allowed in this room, and please do not approach the dais.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Thank you for your cooperation.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Chairs, you may begin.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Great, thank you.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Oh, I always forget to do that.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Good morning and welcome to the fourth day of the FY27 Executive Budget Hearings.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: I'm Councilmember Linda Lee and today's hearings will begin with the Department of Transportation followed by Department of Consumer Protection and the New York City Economic Development Corporation.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So we have a very fun day ahead of us.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: I'm pleased to be joined by my colleagues who are here with us today and also especially our Majority Leader Shaun-Abreu who is the Chair of the Committee of Transportation.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And we have been joined by Council Members Wan, Ressler, Lewis, and Wong.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: I think that's it for now.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And welcome to Commissioner Mike Flynn, you and your team.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Thank you all for joining us today for today's hearing and answering our questions.

chapters.summary: Lee outlines DOT's $1.6B FY27 budget, a 5% increase from preliminary, including funding for bus action plan, cycling and micromobility, Staten Island Ferry costs, and headcount shortfalls.
chapters.title: Chair Lee details DOT's proposed FY27 budget of $1.6 billion

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: On May 12, 2026, the administration released the executive financial plan for fiscal years 26 to 30 with a proposed FY27 budget of $124.7 billion.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: The Department of Transportation proposed fiscal 27 budget of $1.6 billion represents 1.3% of the administration's proposed FY27.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: budget in the executive plan and this is an increase of 78.4 million dollars or 5% from the 1.56 billion originally budgeted in the FY27 preliminary plan.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: This increase results from several actions including from their money added for several new needs including funding for a bus action plan

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: that has additional city funding of $16.9 million and 70 headcount positions in FY27, $30 million and

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: 89 new positions in FY28, $34 million and 103 positions in FY29, and $35 million and 104 positions in FY2030.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: There was also money added for cycling and micromobility, which includes additional city funding of $11.2 million and 45 positions in fiscal 27,

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: 8.6 million and 52 positions in FY28, and growing to 22.8 million and 54 positions by FY30.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: There were also several big adjustments due to fuel costs.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: There was $10.3 million in FY26 and $24.3 million of city funding that was baselined starting in FY27 for heat, light, and power costs related to the Staten Island Ferry.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: As of April 26, DOT's headcount was 647 less than their fiscal 26 budgeted headcount.

chapters.summary: Lee highlights the Council's preliminary budget response identifying $5M in savings from bridge bureau underspending and $179.9M in potential revenue from truck violation enforcement, red light cameras, and parking fines.
chapters.title: Chair Lee on Council's budget response for DOT savings and revenue

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: In the Council's preliminary budget response, the Council identified a few areas related to DOT where the City could allocate additional resources that would provide either additional

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: revenue or expense savings.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: The budget response called on the administration to reflect the annual expense savings of $5 million from re-estimates of underspending in the Bureau of Bridges.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: The Council also identified more than $179.9 million in additional revenue from increased enforcement of truck violations, expansion of the red light camera program, and renting space underneath the Brooklyn Bridge and collection of parking fines.

chapters.summary: Lee outlines her question focus areas including automated enforcement, red light cameras, vacancy reduction, Vision Zero, and franchise revenue, then turns to Chair Abreu for opening remarks.
chapters.title: Chair Lee introduces Transportation Chair Abreu

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: My questions will largely focus on the automated enforcement unit camera savings, red light camera revenue, vacancy reduction, vision zero, and anticipated revenue from franchises.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And I now want to turn it over to my co-chair for this hearing, Majority Leader and Chair, Sean Abreu, for his opening statement.

chapters.summary: Shaun Abreu emphasizes transportation as vital for economic growth, public safety, and quality of life, thanking DOT representatives, advocates, and colleagues for engaging on transportation issues.
chapters.title: Chair Abreu opening statement on transportation's importance

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: Thank you, Chair.

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: Good morning and welcome to the Committee on Transportation and Infrastructure's joint hearing with the Committee on Finance on the fiscal 2027 executive budget for the Department of Transportation.

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: My name is Sean Abreu and I chair the Committee on Transportation and Infrastructure.

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: I want to begin by thanking the representatives from the New York City Department of Transportation for appearing before this committee, as well as my colleagues, advocates,

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: labor representatives, community organizations, and members of the public who continue to engage on transportation issues that affect millions of New Yorkers every single day.

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: I have repeatedly emphasized that effective and efficient transportation is vital for economic growth, social mobility, and commerce.

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: Transportation is not simply about moving people from one place to another.

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: It is about economic opportunity, public safety,

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: accessibility, quality of life, and the overall functioning of our city.

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: Whether New Yorkers are commuting to work, bringing their children to school, operating small businesses, or accessing healthcare and essential services, they depend on a transportation system that is reliable, equitable, and safe.

chapters.summary: Abreu details DOT's $1.64B FY27 executive budget and $13.8B capital commitment plan, noting it comprises 11.8% of the city's total capital plan and is 3.7% less than the preliminary plan.
chapters.title: Chair Abreu on DOT's $1.64B executive budget and capital plan

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: Today we will hear from the Department of Transportation on its $1.64 billion fiscal 2027 executive budget.

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: DOT's commitments, as presented in the Executive Capital Plan for fiscal 2026-2030, the Executive Commitment Plan,

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: totals $13.8 billion, 3.7% less than the total for the same period presented in the preliminary capital commitment plan released in February.

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: The department's planned commitments comprise 11.8% of the city's total $117.14 billion fiscal 2026-2030 capital commitment plan.

chapters.summary: Abreu recounts how the Council pressed for funding on streets plan, public realm programs, pothole repairs, and street light repairs at the preliminary hearing, and notes the executive plan added funding for each.
chapters.title: Chair Abreu on preliminary hearing concerns addressed in executive plan

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: At our preliminary budget hearing in March, we pressed the administration to adequately fund the streets plan so DOT can accomplish their mandate.

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: And in this executive plan, we see additional funding.

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: At our preliminary hearing, we urged the administration to include more funding for public realm programs such as open streets so they can expand beyond their current levels.

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: And in this executive plan, we see additional funding.

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: At our preliminary hearing, we beseech the administration to include additional resources to help fill our potholes after this historic winter.

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: And in this executive plan, we see additional funding.

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: At the preliminary hearing, we implore the administration to include more funding to help reduce the alarming trend of longer waits for street light repairs.

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: And in this executive plan, we see additional funding.

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: Is this funding going toward improving these specific problematic areas?

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: Is it enough for DOT to accomplish all the basic and necessary functions the agency is supposed to carry out?

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: I look forward to digging into those details today.

chapters.summary: Abreu calls for focus on accountability, transparency on project timelines and funding decisions, and addresses community concerns about dangerous intersections, traffic congestion, and equitable resource distribution.
chapters.title: Chair Abreu on accountability and transparency at DOT

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: As chair, I want this hearing to focus not only on accomplishments, but also on accountability.

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: New Yorkers reserve transparency regarding project timelines, funding decisions, agency coordination, and measurable outcomes.

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: Communities across the city continue to raise concerns about dangerous intersections, traffic congestion, delayed street redesigns.

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: accessibility barriers, and the equitable distribution of transportation resources.

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: These concerns require serious attention and concrete action.

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: As Jacob Javits once said, a budget is not just a collection of numbers, but an expression of our values and aspirations.

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: I look forward to better understanding how this year's executive budget can ensure DOT will meet legal requirements and more effectively serve communities citywide.

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: Before we begin, I'd like to thank council staff for their hard work on preparing for this hearing.

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: Adrian Jipal to my left, principal financial analyst of the committee, Dan Krupp, assistant director, finance division.

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: Chima, Deputy Director of Finance Division, Theodore Miller, Committee Counsel, Sierra Townsend, Committee Counsel, Kevin Katowski and John Bazile, Senior Policy Analysts, and of course, my staff,

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: including my, including Jaleesa Quigley, my Deputy Chief of Staff.

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: And with that, I'll now pass it back to Charlie.

chapters.summary: Lee announces that public testimony for all executive budget hearings will be on one day, June 10th at 9:30 AM, due to compressed hearing timelines.
chapters.title: Chair Lee announces public testimony logistics for June 10

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Thank you, Chair.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: We've also been joined by Councilmember Epstein.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And before we get started, just a couple logistical things.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: As a reminder, just wanted to let everyone know that for this year's executive budget, because the hearings are delayed and we need to scrunch everything in a shorter period of time, we are doing the public testimony all on one day.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Should be fun.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: On June 10th, beginning at 9.30 a.m.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So public testimony is June 10th, 9.30 a.m.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Join us, please, because we want to hear back from the public.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And so we've been sending out emails and notifications.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: But if you guys could tell your folks, that would be great as well.

chapters.summary: Committee counsel swears in Commissioner Flynn, Deputy Commissioner Forgione, Deputy Commissioner Beaton, Assistant Commissioner Rodriguez, and Associate Commissioner Yaroslavsky.
chapters.title: Swearing in of DOT witnesses

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And now I will turn it over to committee counsel to swear in the witnesses for their testimony.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Good morning.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Do you affirm to tell the truth, the whole truth, and nothing but the truth before this committee and to respond honestly to council member questions?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Commissioner Flynn.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: First, Deputy Commissioner Forgione.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: I do.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Deputy Commissioner Beaton.

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: I do.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Assistant Commissioner Rodriguez.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: I do.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And Associate Commissioner Yaroslavoski.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: I do.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: You may begin.

chapters.summary: Mike Flynn introduces himself and his team, testifying on behalf of Mayor Mamdani on DOT's FY27 executive budget and FY26-35 capital plan, emphasizing the budget protects daily services during a tough fiscal climate.
chapters.title: Commissioner Flynn begins DOT testimony on FY27 budget

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: Wonderful.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: Well, good morning and thanks so much, Chair Abreu, Chair Lee, and members of the Committees on Transportation, Infrastructure, and Finance.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: I'm Mike Flynn, Commissioner of the New York City Department of Transportation, and I'm joined today with First Deputy Commissioner Margaret Forgione,

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: Acting Chief Administrative Officer Igor Yaroslavsky, Deputy Commissioner of Transportation Planning and Management Eric Beaton, and Assistant Commissioner for Intergovernmental and Community Affairs Rick Rodriguez.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: Thank you for the opportunity to testify on behalf of Mayor Zoran Mamdani on DOT's fiscal year 2027 executive budget and fiscal year 2026 to 2035 capital plan.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: As Mayor Mamdani announced, this executive budget puts forward a strong budget during a tough fiscal climate that's balanced while also protecting the daily services New Yorkers rely on.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: And I apologize if I go a little quickly because I know we are tight on time.

chapters.summary: Flynn outlines DOT's mission for safe, equitable, sustainable movement, highlighting investments in street safety, roadway conditions, transit accessibility, freight mobility, and public realm improvements.
chapters.title: Flynn on DOT mission and investments in safer streets

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: This budget will support DOT to remain focused on our mission to provide for the safe, equitable, and sustainable movement of people and goods and create public spaces that strengthen our communities.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: We appreciate the mayor's continued investment in DOT, enabling us to maintain our infrastructure, programs, and essential operations.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: To help all New Yorkers and millions of visitors move around our city.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: DOT's executive budget advances the mayor's commitment to make New York City transportation safer, more affordable, and more reliable for working families.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: Through investments in street safety, roadway conditions, transit accessibility, freight mobility,

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: and public realm improvements, DOT continues to deliver core services New Yorkers depend on every day while maintaining strong fiscal stewardship.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: This budget supports the city's long-term economic vitality by investing in safer streets and faster buses, modernizing critical transportation assets, and delivering capital projects efficiently and responsibly.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: DOT remains focused on achieving measurable results for New Yorkers across all five boroughs, while supporting the administration's broader goals of affordability, accessibility, sustainability, and operational excellence.

chapters.summary: Flynn emphasizes DOT is moving with urgency under the Mamdani administration, scaling up protected bike lanes, bus lanes, pedestrian spaces, and improving state of good repair across all assets.
chapters.title: Flynn on thinking big and delivering transformational change

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: As I said in March, we're thinking big and being bold.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: Less than five months into this administration, DOT is moving with urgency and ambition to make our streetscape the envy of the world.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: We're advancing major projects, getting shovels in the ground, and raising the bar for what New Yorkers should expect from their streets and from DOT.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: Our commitment is simple, deliver transformational change that New Yorkers can see and feel.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: With Mayor Mamdani's partnership and the additional resources committed by this administration, we're positioned to aim higher, move faster, and deliver at the highest level.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: Because at the center of this work are people.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: The infrastructure we build and maintain is ultimately about protecting and serving New Yorkers, making streets safer, buses faster, transportation more affordable and sustainable, and public spaces more welcoming and accessible.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: That's why we're scaling up protected bike lanes.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: It's why we're expanding protected bus lanes and bus priority.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: It's why we're creating more space.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: for pedestrians, and it's why we're working around the clock to improve the state of good repair and the safe operations of all of our assets with efficiency and technical rigor.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: Roadways, sidewalks, bridges, traffic signals and street lights, and the Staten Island Ferry, among other things.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: We're putting people before politics and laser focused on achieving the on the ground results that New Yorkers expect.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: In March, I reviewed many of our accomplishments of ongoing work from previous years and highlighted our top priorities for my first year on the job.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: Today, I'll provide updates on a few of those priorities and some of the key changes in our executive budget.

chapters.summary: Flynn states DOT's FY27 expense budget is $1.6B and FY26-35 capital plan is $33B, providing funding for critical operations that make New York safer and more livable.
chapters.title: Flynn on FY27 expense budget and capital plan breakdown

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: Our FY27 expense budget is $1.6 billion, and you can see the more detailed breakdown in the chart in my testimony.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: Our FY26 to 35 capital plan is $33 billion, and again, you can see the detailed breakdown in my testimony.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: This budget provides funding to support critical and innovative DOT operations that make New York safer and more livable.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: The Mondani administration is delighted to make these investments in bus riders, cyclists, and pedestrians in this budget, which I'll highlight a bit more in the testimony.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: These investments strengthen our vision to create a transportation system that provides safe, convenient, and equitable mobility for all residents and visitors.

chapters.summary: Flynn details the bus action plan investment of nearly $17M and 70 positions in FY27 growing to $35M and 104 positions by FY30, plus over $300M for future capital projects.
chapters.title: Flynn on record bus investment with $17M and 70 new positions

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: Starting with fast buses, building on the mayor's commitment to deliver faster, more reliable bus service, and the down payment made in the preliminary budget, we're thrilled by the level of investment this executive budget makes in creating streets that are safer and better for all.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: This budget provides record levels of funding and resources for bus projects, demonstrating a clear commitment from the mayor to expand faster, safer, and more efficient mobility across the city.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: It adds nearly $17 million in 70 new positions in FY27, growing to $35 million in 104 positions by FY30, and an allocation of more than $300 million for future capital projects.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: For too long, critical infrastructure projects have lacked urgency, and we're thrilled by the mayor's financial and political investment to strengthen our transportation network.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: This investment is a commitment to improving New Yorkers'quality of life with reliable and efficient services that work.

chapters.summary: Flynn highlights $11M and 45 new positions for cycling in FY27 growing to $23M and 54 positions by FY30, plus nearly $100M in capital, and announces construction on McGinnis Boulevard redesign.
chapters.title: Flynn on bike infrastructure investment and McGinnis Boulevard

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: Moving on to strengthening bike infrastructure.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: As we close out Bike Month, we're particularly excited by the investments announced to meet rapidly growing cycling trends.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: Each day, more than 600,000 cycling trips are taken in New York City, which is up 64% from 2013.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: Under Vision Zero, we're using every tool available.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: From protected bike lanes to safer street designs, to deliver safety for all New Yorkers no matter how they choose to travel.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: This budget provides the opportunity for DOT to meet the moment during this time of rapid change on our streets by adding more than $11 million

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: in 45 new positions in FY27 alone, growing to nearly $23 million in 54 positions by FY30, and allocating nearly $100 million for future capital projects.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: And just as the mayor challenged us to deliver faster and more reliable,

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: bus service, this investment challenges us to deliver more bike infrastructure across the five boroughs to ensure that cycling is a convenient and affordable choice available to New Yorkers in every neighborhood.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: Just this week, we announced the start of construction to complete the redesign of McGinnis Boulevard, which will deliver parking-protected bike lanes across the entire corridor from Meeker Avenue to the Pulaski Bridge.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: With projects like these, New Yorkers are seeing the investment in real time to deliver safe streets for all New Yorkers.

chapters.summary: Flynn announces $6.4M in baseline investments starting FY27 for open streets, transforming asphalt into opportunity and meeting community demand for safe, lively public spaces.
chapters.title: Flynn on public space and open streets baseline funding

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: Enhancing public space.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: As mentioned, safety is our top priority when determining design treatments, and our public spaces are a critical transportation asset, supporting social, economic, and cultural exchange at the pedestrian level.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: This budget includes an additional $6.4 million of baseline investments starting in FY27 in open streets, which transform asphalt into opportunity.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: This funding helps us meet the strong demand we're seeing from communities to keep these spaces safe, lively, and welcoming, thereby enhancing quality of life for residents as well as retail sales for small businesses.

chapters.summary: Flynn discusses commitment to reducing speed limits to 15 mph at eligible school locations, with 8 new positions and $845K to accelerate school slow zone delivery across all 2,300 eligible schools.
chapters.title: Flynn on SAMHSA law expansion for school slow zones

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: Expanding SAMHSA law.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: In March 2026, the mayor and I committed to reducing the speed limit to 15 miles per hour at every eligible school location across the five boroughs.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: This announcement marked the largest expansion to date of the city's use of SAMHSA law, which will improve street safety for children and families.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: We know that lowering speed saves lives and that the announcement in March was just the beginning.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: So with a long-term goal,

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: of expanding school slow zones to all 2,300 eligible school locations citywide.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: This budget also invests in eight additional positions and $845,000 in baseline funding to expand a program that currently delivers approximately 80 school slow zones each year, accelerating critical safety improvements in communities across the city.

chapters.summary: Flynn describes the SPEED initiative adding 8 new positions and $1.5M to strengthen DOT's capacity to review projects efficiently, supporting critical housing and infrastructure projects.
chapters.title: Flynn on SPEED initiative to expedite development

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: Speed Task Force.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: As the mayor recently announced, we're advancing the city's commitment to faster, fairer, and more efficient development through the Streamlining Procedures to Expedite Equitable Development, or SPEED, initiative.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: And DOT is doing our part.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: To address these delays, the mayor's investment for the SPEED initiative will add eight new positions, including transportation specialists, engineers, preservation experts, and community coordinators to strengthen our capacity to review projects efficiently

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: while maintaining necessary compliance standards.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: Altogether, this investment provides $1.5 million in baseline funding to help support critical housing, transportation, and public infrastructure projects moving forward faster, more efficiently, and with the urgency New Yorkers deserve.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: As the country continues to face a national shortage of engineers, we're also grateful for the Mamdani administration's investment to hire for essential roles like these.

chapters.summary: Flynn recounts the historic winter's impact on roadways, DOT's aggressive pothole blitz, and $10M in baseline resurfacing plus $3M for emergency response efforts.
chapters.title: Flynn on pothole blitz and resurfacing funding

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: On resurfacing and potholes, this past winter was unlike any other in recent memory, with the city seeing historic snowfall and record freezing temperatures.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: In return, New York City faced severe roadway conditions caused by repeat freeze-thaw cycles that created an extraordinary number of potholes.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: across the five boroughs.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: In response, DOT launched an aggressive citywide pothole blitz, deploying crews around the clock to repair streets, restore safe driving conditions, and respond quickly to communities impacted by deteriorating roadways.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: To cover baseline resurfacing needs, this budget adds $10 million starting in FY26 and the addition of nearly $3 million in FY26 to support critical emergency response efforts, covering the substantial costs

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: associated with mobilizing the additional personnel, equipment, materials, and roadway repair operations that were necessary to address that surge in potholes.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: Thanks to the hard work of our DOT crews, thousands of potholes were repaired across major corridors and neighborhood streets alike,

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: helping to improve safety, reduce damage to vehicles, and keep New Yorkers moving during one of the most challenging winter road seasons we've seen in recent years.

chapters.summary: Flynn announces a citywide program to replace every first-generation LED streetlight fixture, adding real-time monitoring technology to identify outages and dispatch crews more efficiently.
chapters.title: Flynn on $331.5M LED streetlight replacement program

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: Illuminating NYC.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: We're making historic investments in the safety, reliability, and quality of life of our neighborhoods and plan to start a $331.5 million citywide program to replace every first-generation LED streetlight fixture across New York City.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: More than a decade ago, New York led the nation by converting nearly 385,000 streetlights to energy-efficient LED technology, dramatically improving visibility, reducing energy use, and lowering emissions.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: Rather than relying on a costly and inefficient system that waits for lights to fail before responding to complaints, this initiative will launch a

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: coordinated multi-year replacement program that upgrades fixtures neighborhood by neighborhood, block by block, ensuring streets remain safely illuminated while reducing maintenance delays and long-term costs.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: With emerging technologies, the program will also modernize the city street light network with new real-time monitoring technology that can immediately identify outages, diagnose problems remotely, and dispatch crews more efficiently.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: We're a national leader in using sustainable street lighting, and we look forward to a comprehensive installation process citywide.

chapters.summary: Flynn reports $6M in FY27 savings from lower AEU contract costs and $3M in annual vacancy reductions, demonstrating DOT's fiscal responsibility while advancing street safety.
chapters.title: Flynn on savings from automated enforcement and vacancy reductions

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: On our savings program, the executive budget reflects DOT's continued commitment to identify efficiencies and responsibly manage taxpayer resources while maintaining critical transportation and street safety services.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: We achieved $6 million in FY27 in savings from lower than anticipated automated enforcement unit contract costs, reflecting careful management of program expenditures and operational needs.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: In addition, the agency identified $3 million in annual vacancy reductions beginning in FY27 through ongoing workforce and hiring management.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: Together, these initiatives demonstrate DOT's dual ability to not only contribute meaningfully to the city's fiscal strategy through technologies that increase revenue, but also strengthens our mission to advance street safety.

chapters.summary: Flynn highlights record-breaking MWBE participation with $470M committed in FY26, representing 33% of DOT's contracted spending, building a diverse business ecosystem.
chapters.title: Flynn on MWBE contracting achievements

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: Finally, we're continuing to expand contracting opportunities for minority and women-owned business enterprises, or MWBEs.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: In fiscal year 2026, we're on track to achieve record-breaking results.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: with $470 million already committed towards MWBE participation, representing 33% of our contracted spending as a department.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: This significant progress is both a numeric achievement and also demonstrates our intentional efforts to create opportunities to build a resilient and diverse business ecosystem to strengthen communities citywide.

chapters.summary: Flynn credits DOT's 6,000 employees and Mayor Mamdani's leadership, pledging to immediately put new funding to use hiring staff, expanding contracts, and scaling up operations.
chapters.title: Flynn concludes testimony on DOT progress under Mamdani

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: In conclusion, just five short months into the Mamdani administration, I'm proud of the tremendous progress we've made at DOT.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: We've unlocked the energy, creativity, and drive of this agency and its employees, quickly elevating our work and delivering results.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: All of this is a credit to the 6,000 employees at DOT who wake up each and every day determined to make our city better.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: And it's also a credit to the leadership of our mayor.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: The investments Mayor Mamdani is making through this budget will serve as a catalyst for the work we do now and for many years to come.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: We'll immediately put this new funding to good use, hiring staff, expanding our contract capacity, and purchasing the supplies and equipment we need to scale up every facet of our work.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: As I said at the March budget hearing, government's a big ship that takes time to turn, but as the agency that runs the Staten Island Ferry, we know a thing or two about that,

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: and we're moving the projects that were stuck in the pipeline, we're rapidly announcing new ones, and just as importantly, we're laying the groundwork for even bigger and more impactful initiatives to save New Yorkers money, save time, and save lives.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: In the months ahead, we'll be rolling out big, holistic plans on everything from faster buses to safer streets, and these will be promises we can keep because

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: of the investments made in this budget and in the years ahead.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: I want to thank the Council for its partnership and for the opportunity to testify today, and we now welcome any questions.

chapters.summary: Lee asks how the $6M FY27 savings from automated enforcement were determined and whether DOT anticipates higher revenue from newer cameras. Forgione explains a new procurement brought down costs through careful negotiation.
chapters.title: Chair Lee questions AEU camera savings of $6M

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Great, thank you.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Great timing.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, so really quickly, let's just dive right in.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: For the automated enforcement unit camera savings, the executive plan, as you mentioned, includes a funding reduction of $6 million in FY27, with $4 million baseline starting in FY28.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: How were these savings determined, and does DOT anticipate higher revenue from the newer AEU cameras?

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgione
utterances.text: Thank you for the question, Chair.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgione
utterances.text: So we did a new procurement for automated enforcement camera program, and through careful negotiation and diligent project management with the vendor, we were able to bring down the costs.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgione
utterances.text: We now have more detail and visibility into the cost of the camera program, and that's just going to bring in more dollars to the city.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, just out of curiosity, how many vendors applied?

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgione
utterances.text: We'll have to double check that and get back to you.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay.

chapters.summary: Lee asks about safety enhancements in communities with new red light cameras. Forgione reports 450 intersections currently covered, with all 600 to be completed by year's end.
chapters.title: Lee questions red light camera expansion to 600 intersections

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And in October 2024, Governor Hochul signed into law the expansion of the existing red light camera program.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Sorry.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Allowing the city to quadruple the number of red light cameras 150 to 600, marking the largest expansion of the program.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And the executive plan includes $97.4 million in FY27 and $230.1 million over the five-year plan.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: What additional safety enhancements have DOT implemented or will implement within the communities where the additional cameras are in use?

utterances.speaker: Eric Beaton
utterances.text: Just while they're looking at information, a slight point of clarification, it's 600 intersections rather than cameras.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgione
utterances.text: Thank you for that question.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgione
utterances.text: So regarding the red light camera expansion to date, we have 450 intersections covered by red light cameras and about 600 cameras because, of course, at each intersection we can put a camera on both approaches.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgione
utterances.text: And by the end of this year, we'll have all 600 intersections completed.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Sorry, I thought I felt a sneeze coming on.

chapters.summary: Lee asks about funding for speed camera signage and how DOT decides camera locations. Forgione explains placement is data-driven based on speeds, crashes, and crash nature.
chapters.title: Lee on speed camera signage and placement criteria

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, does DOT have adequate funding for signage specific to the speed cameras?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Yes, we do.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And I know this is a question that comes up a lot when we're looking at different boroughs, different parts of the city, thinking about equity.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: How does DOT decide when and where to move speed cameras?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Like what's the criteria, metrics that you use to place them?

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgione
utterances.text: So DOT is, of course, a data-driven agency in all of the safety measures that we have.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgione
utterances.text: When we're citing speed cameras, we're looking at, obviously, first and foremost, speeds.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgione
utterances.text: We're looking at crashes.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgione
utterances.text: We're looking at the nature of those crashes.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgione
utterances.text: And we are constantly sort of recalibrating our streets to determine what's happening on them and where the cameras can be most effective.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And then is it taken into consideration that some parts of the city obviously, you know, because I think sometimes there's,

chapters.summary: Lee raises concerns about transit desert areas like Southeast Queens having more cameras versus midtown Manhattan, and asks about racial and economic equity in placement decisions.
chapters.title: Lee on equity concerns in camera placement across boroughs

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: when we're looking at the data, there are some parts of the city that have more crashes, but it's also probably because those are transit desert areas where there's more cars being utilized versus other modes of transportation.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And so just wondering how that's factored in to a place, for example, like Southeast Queens that has a very high number of red light speed light cameras versus somewhere in midtown Manhattan.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: I think it's important to note to start that the vast majority of drivers, once they get one violation, it doesn't happen again.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: So, you know, I think in an ideal world, we would not get any revenue, you know, from these programs if everybody's following the law.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: So as First Deputy Commissioner Forgione said, we really try to follow the data and ultimately our North Star is moving towards zero traffic fatalities.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Yeah, no.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: That's definitely the goal.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And actually, that leads to my next question, because if the cameras move around, because I think what happens is that usually drivers become familiar with where certain speed cameras are.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And, you know, it's usually on some of the apps like Waze, for example, right?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So it sort of notifies you, which is not a bad thing because it'll help people to slow down.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: But if the new cameras get moved, how's the community notified?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Is it done in different?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Has DOD conducted any analysis specifically on equity, whether it's based on racial or economic data related to camera placements, which is sort of what I was getting at?

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgione
utterances.text: So we are able to relocate the cameras.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgione
utterances.text: In terms of notifying the community, frankly, to be honest, the idea with all of our camera enforcement programs is not that you're aware that there's a camera in any given spot and you adjust your behavior.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgione
utterances.text: We want drivers to drive as if they need to comply with all of the rules and the laws, regardless of whether or not there's a camera.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgione
utterances.text: And maybe they should assume that there could be a camera everywhere, and that will better guide the behavior.

chapters.summary: Lee presses on whether DOT has specifically analyzed racial and economic data. Beaton confirms they reviewed distribution and found cameras are evenly distributed based on city demographics.
chapters.title: Lee on racial economic data analysis for camera placements

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Right, but have you specifically looked at racial economic data in terms of where the cameras get placed?

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgione
utterances.text: We have looked at that.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgione
utterances.text: I don't have any of that readily available.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgione
utterances.text: Deputy Commissioner Beaton, go ahead.

utterances.speaker: Eric Beaton
utterances.text: Yeah, we did look at that, and as the first deputy said, we can share it after.

utterances.speaker: Eric Beaton
utterances.text: But we did look to make sure that they're not being either over- or underserved in either low-income or non-white communities.

utterances.speaker: Eric Beaton
utterances.text: We start with the data and doing it where they're most needed, but then we did go back and double-check to make sure they're not all in communities of color or not avoiding communities of color.

utterances.speaker: Eric Beaton
utterances.text: And we found that they really are distributed very evenly around the city based on the racial and economic makeup of the city.

utterances.speaker: Eric Beaton
utterances.text: And that's part of why they're effective, I think, is because they're not concentrated in communities, but that they are really all over the city.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: I know some of my colleagues will probably have different opinions, but I'll let them ask their questions on that.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: But what, so going where they're most needed, but then we did go back and double check to make sure they're not all in communities of color or not avoiding communities of color.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Back to my original question, but what additional safety enhancements?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: I know you spoke about the 450 intersections where the red light cameras have been placed, but what other...

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: additional safety enhancements have DOT implemented?

utterances.speaker: Eric Beaton
utterances.text: And we found that they really are distributed very evenly around the city based on the racial and economic makeup of the city.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgione
utterances.text: Right.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgione
utterances.text: So, I mean, you know, Vision Zero really drives so many of our investments and design decisions out on the street, and it's become a massive program, and with this historic investment we're seeing in the current budget, it's going to grow even more.

utterances.speaker: Eric Beaton
utterances.text: And that's part of why they're effective, I think, is because they're not concentrated in communities, but that they are really all over the city.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: I know some of my colleagues will probably have different opinions, but I'll let them ask their questions on that.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: But what, so going

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgione
utterances.text: So we have a really broad toolbox, and we follow the data if it's a high crash corridor or a specific intersection or a particular neighborhood, and then depending on the context, is it a wide street, a narrow street, there's different kinds of traffic calming, right?

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgione
utterances.text: It could be redesigning intersections, it could be speed humps, it could be adding or changing the traffic signals, stop signs, road diets, reallocating the space.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgione
utterances.text: It's a very broad toolbox.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay.

chapters.summary: Lee asks whether residents are adequately informed about camera programs in multiple languages and for disabilities communities. Forgione describes robust community engagement across multiple channels and languages.
chapters.title: Lee on community outreach and multilingual notification

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And then, are the residents adequately informed about the programs or any changes, meaning multiple languages and, of course, reaching out to the disabilities community, for example?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And how does that outreach look like?

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgione
utterances.text: We do very robust community engagement.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgione
utterances.text: We're always looking to improve upon it, but I think the agency's made really great strides in the last 10 plus years

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgione
utterances.text: in engaging communities across multiple channels, multiple languages, whether it's pop-up outreach or larger scale community workshops, online, social media.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Any detail?

utterances.speaker: Eric Beaton
utterances.text: And just to add on, we have our street engagement teams that go into communities directly to meet where community members are.

utterances.speaker: Eric Beaton
utterances.text: And we use materials that are translated across the 10 most frequently used languages in the city.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, great.

chapters.summary: Lee calculates potential $199.7M in additional annual revenue if cameras expanded to 1,325 intersections. Beaton clarifies the state authorization only permits 600 intersections.
chapters.title: Lee on red light camera revenue projections for expanded program

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: In the council's budget response, we noted that there were 211 red light cameras mounted around the city, which accounted for $31.8 million in revenue, or approximately $150,710 per camera.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: If the red light cameras program were to be expanded to 1,325 intersections as contained in the state authorizing bill, additional $199.7 million could be generated annually based on the fiscal 2025 revenue numbers.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Does DOT and the administration support expanding the red light camera program to the scale prescribed in the state authorizing legislation?

utterances.speaker: Eric Beaton
utterances.text: Can you just say the front part one more time?

utterances.speaker: Eric Beaton
utterances.text: I just want to make sure I have the details crisply.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Yeah, so it's 211 red light cameras mounted currently with roughly 31.8 million in revenue.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So if you take that same sort of breakdown,

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: If the program were to expand to 1,325 intersections, as was in the state authorizing bill?

utterances.speaker: Eric Beaton
utterances.text: The state authorizing bill is only for 600 intersections.

utterances.speaker: Eric Beaton
utterances.text: The 1,350 was the original negotiation point.

utterances.speaker: Eric Beaton
utterances.text: The 2025 negotiations for the reauthorization that reflected, I think, 10% of all intersections, which is where that number came from.

utterances.speaker: Eric Beaton
utterances.text: But we're only authorized up to 600 intersections.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, so given that it's 600.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Would that concurrently, in terms of the ratio of the amount of revenue generated, do you foresee that being the same sort of ratio of the revenue that's being generated in proportion?

chapters.summary: Lee asks about proportional revenue from more cameras. Flynn emphasizes zero revenue would indicate success, and the program is about changing driver behavior, not generating revenue.
chapters.title: Lee and Flynn debate whether camera program is revenue-based

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: Chair, just to highlight what the Commissioner said earlier, if we generated zero revenue, that would be a successful program.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: This is not a revenue-based program, and we're just trying to change driver behavior.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: I'm sorry, can you repeat that?

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: Just to highlight what the Commissioner said earlier, if we generated zero dollars in revenue, that would be a successful program.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: We're never looking at it as a revenue-based program.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: It's more about changing driver behavior.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Agree, but I think when we're looking for ways to generate, it's just, I guess I'm just not asking about the intent, but it's more about,

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: do you foresee that if there's more red light cameras, there would be, in theory, more revenue?

utterances.speaker: Eric Beaton
utterances.text: And to speak at a high level, I think one of the things that we see is in the initial period where installation happens, there is a spike.

utterances.speaker: Eric Beaton
utterances.text: Right?

utterances.speaker: Eric Beaton
utterances.text: People are unfamiliar.

utterances.speaker: Eric Beaton
utterances.text: But what we also see is that driver behavior improves.

utterances.speaker: Eric Beaton
utterances.text: So I don't think we can say for the status quo number for what you see in your metrics there, that would be...

utterances.speaker: Eric Beaton
utterances.text: of what we would see in a scalar program.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: I mean, yes, just for the record, I agree.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: We want to change driver behavior.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: That's the ultimate goal.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: But I'm just trying to get a sense, since it's a budget hearing, right, of where you think the numbers will land.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay.

chapters.summary: Lee asks which neighborhoods saw highest camera revenue increases and what the error rate is for violations. Flynn says they don't have that data today but describes a robust review process.
chapters.title: Lee on camera revenue neighborhoods and violation error rates

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Does DOT expect, oh, what neighborhoods have seen the highest increase from camera revenue?

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: We don't have that information with us today.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Were cameras moved with the aim of increasing, hold on, what is the error rate, sorry, what is the error rate for red light camera violations?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Do you have that information?

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: Sorry, what do you mean by that exactly?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: If there are, for example, if there's a car that mistakenly got this or there's a license plate, I'm just trying to figure out if there are any errors and what that percentage would be.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: Okay, we have a very robust review process where we have a city employee reviewing each and every violation before it is issued.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: We have an extremely high rate, like we don't receive them back in error, and we can get you the exact percentages.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, and what is the process actually for the violation review?

chapters.summary: Lee asks about the violation review process and whether cameras have been removed for technical problems. Flynn describes DOT employees reviewing each violation and says no false tickets are issued.
chapters.title: Lee on violation review process and camera removal

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: So we have DOT employees in our traffic operations division who have been carefully trained for all the different camera type.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: violations to examine each and every video.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: Obviously looking at it carefully, confirming if it's red light, confirming that in fact the signal has changed, where the vehicle is in the intersection.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: There's an SOP and a very detailed protocol of how that evaluation is made.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: And then that person certifies that it is a valid violation before it is issued.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, and so I think you're sort of answering my question, but for the record, have any cameras been removed because of technical problems or excessive false tickets?

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: We would never get to the point of issuing a false ticket.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: If there's any technical problem with the camera, we would work with the vendor to bring that back online, but if there were any issue, we would not even consider those violations.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Perfect.

chapters.summary: Lee asks how often yellow light timings are reviewed and whether extending duration reduces violations. Forgione explains yellow phase is set as a ratio of speed limit, so it shouldn't be variable.
chapters.title: Lee on yellow light timing review and studies

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And how often are yellow light timings reviewed and adjusted?

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: How often is it calibrated or checked?

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: Is that the question?

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: Yes.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: I need to confirm that unless any of my colleagues have that.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Because I know that some traffic lights have longer yellow light timings versus others, so I just wanted to see.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: We'll have to get back to you on that.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And have you done any studies about whether extending the yellow light duration reduces violations?

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgione
utterances.text: So the yellow phase is set as basically a ratio of the speed limit.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgione
utterances.text: So whatever speed we expect drivers to be going or that they should be going or staying under, that's what the time is.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgione
utterances.text: based on and so it shouldn't really be a variable thing in terms of the results, if that makes sense.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: What engineering alternatives were considered besides enforcement cameras?

chapters.summary: Lee asks about engineering alternatives besides cameras. Forgione emphasizes a broad toolbox approach including intersection redesigns, speed humps, traffic signals, stop signs, and road diets.
chapters.title: Lee on engineering alternatives to enforcement cameras

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: I know that you explained some of them.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgione
utterances.text: Yeah, it's not, I don't think it's really, we don't approach it as an either or.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgione
utterances.text: I think it's really all of the above.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgione
utterances.text: And I just really want to stress how effective automated enforcement has been and how we see it, but also that 96% of drivers don't get more than two violations.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgione
utterances.text: So we really...

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgione
utterances.text: Again, from our vantage point, it is a safety tool, and it's a very effective one.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgione
utterances.text: So we really appreciate the council's partnership also, since we have to go back to the state every few years to get these programs reauthorized.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Right.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay.

chapters.summary: Lee describes the arduous process communities face requesting stop signs and speed bumps, especially around schools. Forgione and Beaton explain the holistic approach to evaluating community requests.
chapters.title: Lee on traffic calming request process for communities

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, so moving on to the process to obtain traffic calming measures, because I know that this is the case in my neighborhood as well, especially in the parts of Southeast Queens in my district where either around schools or,

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: you know, in certain intersections, there are very long...

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Sort of like streets between the avenues because it's a very long distance and people speak by.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And so in the situation where community members would like four-way stop signs, it seems like

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: the process itself to want to have other traffic calming measures aside from the cameras, right?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Whether it's a speed bump or four-way stop signs, it seems like a very arduous process to go through that many community members are not understanding, especially if they have the signatures from the streets in their neighbors.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: To want to get something like this done.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And I know that usually DOT comes out and does a study and observes, but I think oftentimes the frustration is it's not during the times where kids get let out or where there's a lot of parents in one area, right, especially around the schools.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And so just wondering if you could walk us through the process to obtaining other traffic calming measures and what factors does DOT consider in that?

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgione
utterances.text: Sure, I can start.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgione
utterances.text: You know, I think we certainly don't want the processes to feel arduous.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgione
utterances.text: I think we've tried to create good pages on our website that explain these, but always open to feedback about how we can make it easier for the community to let us know what issues they're facing.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgione
utterances.text: The other point I would make is just that, you know, again, we do try to approach these things holistically.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgione
utterances.text: So it's helpful for us to hear from the community what's the issue, what's the challenge, and then we can work with them to figure out what the right solutions might be.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgione
utterances.text: It may be a speed hump.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgione
utterances.text: It may be a stop sign.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgione
utterances.text: It may be a traffic signal.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgione
utterances.text: It could be something else.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgione
utterances.text: But if they want to request like a specific tool, then we do have, you know, a process.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgione
utterances.text: processes for each of those.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgione
utterances.text: We can speak a little more.

utterances.speaker: Eric Beaton
utterances.text: Right, and the processes all work together.

utterances.speaker: Eric Beaton
utterances.text: If someone requests a stop sign and we think it's not appropriate for a stop sign, we don't say, well, sorry, nothing we can do here.

utterances.speaker: Eric Beaton
utterances.text: If we see speeding, then a speed hump might be appropriate or other traffic calming.

utterances.speaker: Eric Beaton
utterances.text: We do try to take the concern and find the right treatment.

utterances.speaker: Eric Beaton
utterances.text: And, you know, we definitely hear about the times of day and things.

utterances.speaker: Eric Beaton
utterances.text: You know, our signal engineers do go out and look at many times of day.

utterances.speaker: Eric Beaton
utterances.text: It's also helpful sometimes when we get feedback.

utterances.speaker: Eric Beaton
utterances.text: You know, there's a location where it turned out that, you know, summer Saturdays there was a big little league thing that goes on.

utterances.speaker: Eric Beaton
utterances.text: And so a weekday check, which is usually the busiest time of the city,

utterances.speaker: Eric Beaton
utterances.text: wasn't quite right, but because we heard about that traffic pattern, we could go out and look differently.

utterances.speaker: Eric Beaton
utterances.text: So the more information we get, the better we can be responsive to exactly what's going on there.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay.

chapters.summary: Lee asks whether the SPEED initiative could help with traffic calming processes. Forgione clarifies the 8 new positions are for expediting housing and development project reviews, not general traffic studies.
chapters.title: Lee on SPEED initiative scope and traffic studies

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And the new initiative that the mayor had outlined, which was the speed initiative, I mean, is this something like this?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Would that be included in terms of helping with the process of getting those things in place?

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgione
utterances.text: Chair, do you mean the state law, the stop super speeders?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: No, no, no.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: The thing that was in your testimony.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Oh, the speed.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Speed, yeah.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgione
utterances.text: To speed up affordable housing and other sustainable development.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Yes, because I know that you were saying, well, I mean, in terms of the processes, and it says, for example, your portion.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Was to hire eight positions.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So is that just for DOT?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: It's not for the other agencies, right?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Correct.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And this is, I know it's to help support critical housing, but it's also transportation, public infrastructure projects moving forward faster.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So I just wanted to clarify if something like this could also be included in that or no.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgione
utterances.text: Yeah, so yes, this is giving us more resources to basically do the traffic studies, essentially, which is sometimes on the critical path for these developments.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgione
utterances.text: And, you know, it's one important step.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgione
utterances.text: So by having the resources we need on that team, we'll be able to process.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgione
utterances.text: Those reviews a lot faster.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, and if this is sort of alluding to new developments, I would encourage that if this could be done overall, that'd be great too, in terms of just the overall processes.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So I just wanted to make a note of that.

chapters.summary: Lee asks what 49 positions were eliminated and how DOT will carry out functions. Forgione explains the reduction coincides with ending the two-for-one hiring policy, making it a net positive.
chapters.title: Lee on vacancy reduction of 49 headcount positions

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: In terms of vacancy reduction, executive plan includes baseline funding of 3 million starting in fiscal 27 from 49 headcount positions.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So what were these 49 positions that were eliminated and how will DOT be able to carry out the functions?

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgione
utterances.text: Sure, I can say to start, you know, that the vacancy reduction was done in tandem with the ending of the two-for-one hiring policy.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgione
utterances.text: So while, you know, it's a reduction in sort of theoretical headcount, it's actually a lot more helpful for us to have real headcount that we can allocate, you know, based on the needs.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgione
utterances.text: So in the end, this is where...

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgione
utterances.text: really a net positive for us.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgione
utterances.text: Igor could talk more about the specifics, but that's how kind of we're looking at it.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: Thank you, Commissioner.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: So we looked at all of our vacancies and looked at longstanding vacancies.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: There were a couple parts of the agency that just weren't able to hire for a long time.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: So to the Commissioner's point, to allow us to enable hiring faster with the reduction of the two-for-one hiring freeze, we're going to take some vacancies down and hopefully that'll help us speed up.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: Hiring for all of our other positions that need it.

chapters.summary: Lee asks if the Office of Sustainable Delivery is being eliminated. Forgione confirms the function remains as the Sustainable Delivery Unit, focused on policy and regulatory approaches rather than enforcement.
chapters.title: Lee on Office of Sustainable Delivery status

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, and is the entire Office of Sustainable Delivery being eliminated?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: If not, what positions remain and what will be the scope of the office?

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgione
utterances.text: No, we're definitely keeping that function.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgione
utterances.text: I think we've rebranded it as the Sustainable Delivery Unit, as you mentioned.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgione
utterances.text: Still a very important focus area for us to ensure delivery worker safety and safe behavior.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgione
utterances.text: And we're, well, yeah, we could talk more about, I guess, the specific headcount, but basically we're focusing the group more on the policy and regulatory approach needed to get

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgione
utterances.text: safer outcomes in the delivery bike space versus an enforcement forward approach.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Great.

chapters.summary: Lee asks about other savings from reviewing consulting contracts. Flynn says they constantly review contracts for efficiencies, and notes three vendors bid on the enforcement contract with Verra Mobility selected.
chapters.title: Lee on consulting contract review and savings

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And I know that you mentioned this earlier with the contractor that you rebid for.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: a lower cost and so are there other savings measures like that where you're looking at different consulting contracts or other areas?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Because I know some of the agencies haven't looked at actually some of their consulting contracts in years and so just wondering if you guys have done a review of that.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: Thank you for the question, Chair.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: So we're constantly looking at all of our contracts, both professional services, engineering contracts, and seeing if there's efficiencies that could be made for contracts that are already in place.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: It's a little bit hard because the terms are set, but every time we rebid, we look at ways to do it faster and better.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: And to the question you asked earlier, there were three vendors that bid on the enforcement contract, and Vera Mobility was the chosen vendor.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Great.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And last question before I pass it off to the chair.

chapters.summary: Lee asks about using technology differently for operational efficiencies. Forgione emphasizes this is a priority, with staff specifically focused on efficiency and organizational excellence.
chapters.title: Lee on technology and efficiency improvements

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Is DOT looking to use technology, training, or deployment any differently defined efficiencies in its operations?

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgione
utterances.text: Absolutely, and this is an important priority for me, honestly.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgione
utterances.text: I mean, I think it's something that we do as a standard practice, but I really want to take a holistic look across the board at how we can improve our operations and efficiency, technology being one critical piece of that.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgione
utterances.text: And we're going to have folks really whose role is specifically focused on efficiency and organizational excellence.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Great, thank you.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And just quickly, we've been joined by Justin Sanchez and Christopher Marte.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And Chair, go ahead.

chapters.summary: Justin Sanchez confronts Beaton about conflicting daylighting cost estimates: $10,000 per intersection versus $3 billion total for 40,000 intersections. Beaton explains the discrepancy is per location versus per intersection with eight potential daylighting spots.
chapters.title: CM Sanchez challenges Deputy Commissioner Beaton on daylighting cost discrepancy

utterances.speaker: Justin Sanchez
utterances.text: Thank you, Chair Lee.

utterances.speaker: Justin Sanchez
utterances.text: This is a question for Commissioner Beaton.

utterances.speaker: Justin Sanchez
utterances.text: Deputy Commissioner Beaton, in April of last year, you testified under oath to this committee that as it pertains to the cost of daylighting, 10,000 is a very round rule of thumb number for a single intersection.

utterances.speaker: Justin Sanchez
utterances.text: You further emphasized this cost to Streetsblock saying, the cost of hardened daylighting infrastructure comes at about 10,000 per location, which includes labor and regular maintenance.

utterances.speaker: Justin Sanchez
