utterances.text: However, in that same hearing, your submitted testimony to the committee stated universal hardened daylighting would cost in the neighborhood of $3 billion.

utterances.speaker: Justin Sanchez
utterances.text: New York City has 40,000 intersections, Mr.

utterances.speaker: Justin Sanchez
utterances.text: Beaton, at a cost of $10,000 per intersection that comes to $400 million.

utterances.speaker: Justin Sanchez
utterances.text: Is this just a simple case of bad math or a huge disagreement you have with yourself?

utterances.speaker: Eric Beaton
utterances.text: So the $10,000 was per location.

utterances.speaker: Eric Beaton
utterances.text: An intersection has four corners.

utterances.speaker: Eric Beaton
utterances.text: Each corner can have two sides that are daylit.

utterances.speaker: Eric Beaton
utterances.text: So I think that that's the discrepancy is it's not per intersection, it's per location.

chapters.summary: Sanchez cites previous OMB estimates averaging $17,000 per intersection for Intro 1138, far below the $3 billion figure. Flynn and Beaton argue costs don't scale linearly and universal implementation includes more complex sites.
chapters.title: Sanchez on OMB daylighting estimates showing lower costs

utterances.speaker: Justin Sanchez
utterances.text: I mean, even if you were to multiply that by four, it would still be far less than the $3 billion in your testimony.

utterances.speaker: Justin Sanchez
utterances.text: Is that correct?

utterances.speaker: Eric Beaton
utterances.text: Well, to answer that question, is that correct?

utterances.speaker: Eric Beaton
utterances.text: To get a full number, you would multiply by eight.

utterances.speaker: Eric Beaton
utterances.text: So, again, these are round numbers, but that's how you get there.

utterances.speaker: Justin Sanchez
utterances.text: Are you aware that the previous administration's OMB estimates for Intro 1138, which called to daylight 1,000 intersections per year, included several large additional costs, including $8.7 million for

utterances.speaker: Justin Sanchez
utterances.text: hardening barrier materials, $1.2 million for personnel services, including fringe to hire staff,

utterances.speaker: Justin Sanchez
utterances.text: 5.5 million for annual personnel services, 780,000 for drive rails, and 420,000 for signage, 63,000 in year one, and 126,000 in year two

utterances.speaker: Justin Sanchez
utterances.text: for the purchasing of new vehicles and still only average close to just 17,000 per intersection.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: So that is an average for one intersection.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: It doesn't scale up equally.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: And with every number, there's always outliers.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: So to the point Deputy Commissioner Beaton was making, you can't just take one intersection and extrapolate it that way.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: Linearly.

utterances.speaker: Justin Sanchez
utterances.text: This is an OMB estimate for intro 1138.

utterances.speaker: Justin Sanchez
utterances.text: Which is 1,000 intersections per year.

utterances.speaker: Eric Beaton
utterances.text: I think when you're picking a limited number of intersections, you can intentionally look for ones that are more straightforward and cost less.

utterances.speaker: Eric Beaton
utterances.text: When you're talking universally and doing it at every location in the city, you inherently have to do all the more complex ones.

utterances.speaker: Eric Beaton
utterances.text: And so as Igor said, it doesn't scale exactly the same way.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgione
utterances.text: I mean, I think at the end of the day, we're eager to sit down and work through the details.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgione
utterances.text: And if it's a matter of figuring out what's the most accurate cost, you know, we're happy to be a partner in that.

chapters.summary: Sanchez compares NYC's estimates to Hoboken's low-cost daylighting interventions starting at $1,500 per intersection and Portland's $700-800 per intersection, arguing NYC's costs are inflated.
chapters.title: Sanchez on previous administration and Hoboken daylighting models

utterances.speaker: Justin Sanchez
utterances.text: I know you are a committed commissioner, but I have to come after Commissioner Beaton on his information from the past.

utterances.speaker: Justin Sanchez
utterances.text: You're a good boss.

utterances.speaker: Justin Sanchez
utterances.text: Thank you for coming to his defense.

utterances.speaker: Justin Sanchez
utterances.text: The previous administration, which was no friend of this policy, could only manage to inflate the cost to $17,000 per intersection.

utterances.speaker: Justin Sanchez
utterances.text: This is baffling considering if we apply this number to all 40,000 intersections of the city, the total cost is around 22% of that $3 billion number you and the administration touted.

utterances.speaker: Justin Sanchez
utterances.text: That the previous administration has touted.

utterances.speaker: Justin Sanchez
utterances.text: Are you aware of that, Mr.

utterances.speaker: Justin Sanchez
utterances.text: Beaton?

utterances.speaker: Eric Beaton
utterances.text: So, yeah, I think we discussed it a little bit here, and as the commissioner said, we're happy to get into more detail about what it costs and what it takes to do this work.

utterances.speaker: Justin Sanchez
utterances.text: Are you aware that Hoboken was able to roll out daylighting in mass by starting with low-cost interventions like paint and plastic delineators, which can start for as low as $1,500 per intersection?

utterances.speaker: Eric Beaton
utterances.text: We regularly talk to all of our neighbors about what and how they do things and what they cost in different locations.

utterances.speaker: Justin Sanchez
utterances.text: Are you also aware that Hoboken studied levels of interventions, including $2,000 to add some bike racks intersections?

utterances.speaker: Eric Beaton
utterances.text: I cannot speak to familiarity with every piece of...

utterances.speaker: Justin Sanchez
utterances.text: I mean, this is information I'm confident in, and it still comes nowhere near the cost that this administration is estimating.

utterances.speaker: Justin Sanchez
utterances.text: Last year at the daylighting hearing, Councilmember Wan mentioned that DOT reported to her office that it would cost $10,000 to harden daylight and intersection, which agrees with the council's analysis and which she stated publicly for the record.

utterances.speaker: Justin Sanchez
utterances.text: Do you disagree with your own agency's analysis?

utterances.speaker: Eric Beaton
utterances.text: I'm sorry, I wasn't totally following, but we've said it's 10,000, right?

utterances.speaker: Eric Beaton
utterances.text: That sounds the same.

utterances.speaker: Eric Beaton
utterances.text: I think it's difficult to get into some of these details without everything in front of us.

utterances.speaker: Eric Beaton
utterances.text: We're more than happy to talk to you and make sure we are all looking at the same cost.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgione
utterances.text: Can you just clarify?

utterances.speaker: Justin Sanchez
utterances.text: Sorry, I'm not done with the line of questioning, Councilmember.

chapters.summary: Sanchez calls for committing $400M for universal hardened daylighting, praises Commissioner Flynn's commitment, and yields to Councilmember Won for follow-up questions.
chapters.title: Sanchez urges $400M commitment for universal hardened daylighting

utterances.speaker: Justin Sanchez
utterances.text: Even if DOT just did unhardened universal daylighting at certain intersections, it only caused the city of Portland 700 to 800 to erect four signs per intersection.

utterances.speaker: Justin Sanchez
utterances.text: I understand this administration agrees with prior administrations'extremely questionable claim that unhardened daylighting is more dangerous in certain situations.

utterances.speaker: Justin Sanchez
utterances.text: But then, can this administration commit $400 million to implement universal hardened daylighting in our city?

utterances.speaker: Justin Sanchez
utterances.text: Every New Yorker deserves to feel safe and be safe in the areas where we live and work and play.

utterances.speaker: Justin Sanchez
utterances.text: Our seniors and children deserve to cross our streets without fear, and every New Yorker deserves better from their government.

utterances.speaker: Justin Sanchez
utterances.text: I remain disappointed with the resistance to this measure, but I'm grateful that Commissioner Flynn has committed, not only in public conversations, but in private conversations, that he's really committed to addressing this.

utterances.speaker: Justin Sanchez
utterances.text: Commissioner, I really, really am grateful for your leadership on this.

utterances.speaker: Justin Sanchez
utterances.text: I'm going to let Councilmember Juan ask questions on this specific issue, and then I'll transition to other topics.

utterances.speaker: Eric Beaton
utterances.text: Thank you so much, Chair Breu.

chapters.summary: Julie Won demands a detailed breakdown of what $10,000 per location covers, expressing frustration with DOT's redefinition of 'location' versus 'intersection' and noting 100 advocates outside calling for daylighting.
chapters.title: CM Won presses on daylighting cost breakdown per intersection

utterances.speaker: Julie Won
utterances.text: Getting a little frustrated.

utterances.speaker: Julie Won
utterances.text: Okay, so why don't we just ask in simpler terms.

utterances.speaker: Julie Won
utterances.text: So why don't you explain to us your calculations for how much intersections cost per intersection, what is the actual cost?

utterances.speaker: Julie Won
utterances.text: Not four intersections or four streets or four sidewalks, however way you want to put it.

utterances.speaker: Julie Won
utterances.text: How much does each intersection cost to daylight?

utterances.speaker: Eric Beaton
utterances.text: So again, we're happy to go through this with you in much more detail.

utterances.speaker: Julie Won
utterances.text: Yes, let's go through it in detail now.

utterances.speaker: Eric Beaton
utterances.text: I'm happy to go through it in much more detail when we have all the figures in front of us and can be all looking at the same sheet.

utterances.speaker: Julie Won
utterances.text: So you came to this hearing on the budget without the figures in front of you for a budget hearing?

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgione
utterances.text: It costs roughly $10,000 per location.

utterances.speaker: Julie Won
utterances.text: So per location, he's saying that it's not $10,000 per location because he's defining location in a different way than the way that we define location.

utterances.speaker: Julie Won
utterances.text: What is the $10,000 for?

utterances.speaker: Julie Won
utterances.text: Give us the breakdown.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgione
utterances.text: So it's about $10,000 per location, which would be about $10 million for 1,000 intersections.

chapters.summary: Won notes Mayor Mamdani previously stood with her demanding universal daylighting at rallies, and asks how DOT now qualifies which locations are 'daylightable,' questioning the reversal from the mayor's campaign position.
chapters.title: Won on mayor's campaign position on universal daylighting

utterances.speaker: Julie Won
utterances.text: Yes, we understand that, which is why we were outside with over 100 advocates who have been calling year after year because hundreds of people die due to lack of daylighting.

utterances.speaker: Julie Won
utterances.text: With cars turning at intersections, which I know, Commissioner, you already know, because we've discussed this, many people have discussed this with you before you took this.

utterances.speaker: Julie Won
utterances.text: your position and we understand that it's going to be $10 million for a thousand intersections.

utterances.speaker: Julie Won
utterances.text: So help us understand what you mean by location.

utterances.speaker: Julie Won
utterances.text: Do you mean location means four corners?

utterances.speaker: Julie Won
utterances.text: Why are you debunking what $10 million costs us?

utterances.speaker: Julie Won
utterances.text: To daylight.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: Maybe I can try to clarify, if I may.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: So every intersection, typical intersection, has four corners, right?

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: There's two sides to each of that corner as the streets come together.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: So if you can visualize that, there are actually eight, as Commissioner Beaton said, there's eight potential places that you can do daylighting.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: So when we say we can daylight one corner or one intersection for 10,000, that's simply one part of the eight, and that may be inadequate, right?

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: In order to truly address safety at that intersection, depending on what our engineers feel would be beneficial there.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: That's why the 10,000 is oversimplifying the conversation.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: And it would help, you know, and I know we're trying to take care of some of this here, but if you have time at another time, we'd love to sit there just like with a plan of a few streets and go through that with you.

utterances.speaker: Julie Won
utterances.text: So I have a few follow-up questions.

utterances.speaker: Julie Won
utterances.text: For daylighting, can you help me understand how do you qualify what you deem as daylightable?

utterances.speaker: Julie Won
utterances.text: Help me understand that.

utterances.speaker: Julie Won
utterances.text: What are your qualifications to determine?

utterances.speaker: Julie Won
utterances.text: Because we went from the mayor, Zoram Amdani, when he was an assembly member, he stood with me when a six-year-old was mauled by a car in Astoria.

utterances.speaker: Julie Won
utterances.text: At Domaine de Dunes,

utterances.speaker: Julie Won
utterances.text: daylighting rally and he demanded that we daylight every single intersection and he's been on record multiple times calling for universal daylighting and now he has taken office and we're now hearing the opposite of that from you all

utterances.speaker: Julie Won
utterances.text: so we want to understand what do you qualify as somewhere that should be daylit because you are saying that you no longer believe in universal daylighting despite all of the times that the mayor has gone on record in support of universal

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgione
utterances.text: Thank you, Councilmember.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgione
utterances.text: I just want to be clear that we share the same goal of eliminating traffic fatalities, and we absolutely see daylighting as a key piece of that.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgione
utterances.text: We are all for figuring out and eager to work with you and other partners to understand the most effective and fast way to expand the use of daylighting.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgione
utterances.text: I just want to be clear on the record about that.

chapters.summary: Won asks how DOT evaluates appropriate daylighting locations. Forgione says they focus on where drivers can make legal turns and where daylighting can be most effective for pedestrian visibility.
chapters.title: Won on daylighting evaluation criteria

utterances.speaker: Julie Won
utterances.text: Okay, so how do you evaluate what locations are appropriate for daylighting?

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgione
utterances.text: We focus on where it can be most effective.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgione
utterances.text: So generally, that's where a driver would be able to make a legal turn.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgione
utterances.text: That's where the daylighting can be most helpful, right?

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgione
utterances.text: We want pedestrians to be visible and to be seen.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgione
utterances.text: And so if you think about one-way streets, some corners are more helpful than others for daylighting, and that's what we would want to prioritize.

utterances.speaker: Justin Sanchez
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Justin Sanchez
utterances.text: All right, we're going to transition now to a few other topic areas.

chapters.summary: Sanchez asks how many miles of bus lanes will be built with the $16.9M bus action plan funding. Forgione says exact numbers aren't available yet but expects historic output significantly exceeding prior years.
chapters.title: Sanchez on bus action plan funding and expected mileage

utterances.speaker: Justin Sanchez
utterances.text: Bus action plan.

utterances.speaker: Justin Sanchez
utterances.text: The executive plan includes additional city funding of $16.9 million.

utterances.speaker: Justin Sanchez
utterances.text: million and 70 headcount positions in fiscal 2027 that grows to 35 million and 104 additional positions in fiscal 2030 for the bus action plan.

utterances.speaker: Justin Sanchez
utterances.text: How many miles of bus lanes will be built with this funding and how many will be new bus lanes?

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgione
utterances.text: Thank you, Chair.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgione
utterances.text: Yeah, so we're thrilled with the level of investment.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgione
utterances.text: It's a historic level of investment.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgione
utterances.text: It's going to let us bring this program to a historic level of output.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgione
utterances.text: While I don't have specific miles for this year because the program is kind of a dynamic thing that we're developing and implementing, we're expecting a historic level of output this year, significantly more than prior years.

utterances.speaker: Justin Sanchez
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Justin Sanchez
utterances.text: Do you know how much more that would be?

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgione
utterances.text: Unfortunately, I don't have exact numbers, but I think it will be significantly more than what we're on track for.

utterances.speaker: Justin Sanchez
utterances.text: All right.

utterances.speaker: Justin Sanchez
utterances.text: Once you get that information, if you could share that with us, that would be greatly appreciated.

chapters.summary: Sanchez asks for average cost per bus lane mile. Beaton explains costs vary significantly, with red paint at about $4 per square foot, and different lengths and intersection configurations affecting totals.
chapters.title: Sanchez on bus lane cost per mile

utterances.speaker: Justin Sanchez
utterances.text: On average, how much will each bus lane cost to install?

utterances.speaker: Justin Sanchez
utterances.text: Please be specific.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: I might need a moment for that one.

utterances.speaker: Justin Sanchez
utterances.text: Take your time.

utterances.speaker: Igor Yaroslavsky
utterances.text: Do we have that?

utterances.speaker: Eric Beaton
utterances.text: Yeah, I mean, it does vary quite a bit.

utterances.speaker: Eric Beaton
utterances.text: You know, just for example, the red paint is about $4 a square foot in round numbers.

utterances.speaker: Eric Beaton
utterances.text: But different lengths of bus lane, if there's more intersections, then you don't have the red paint through the intersection.

utterances.speaker: Eric Beaton
utterances.text: The costs are not the same for every.

utterances.speaker: Eric Beaton
utterances.text: Every mile in the city.

utterances.speaker: Eric Beaton
utterances.text: So this is a tremendous investment that the city has gotten us.

utterances.speaker: Eric Beaton
utterances.text: It's why we are planning it out street by street and rather than just saying, here's an average, we're going to use this money to do as many bus lanes as we possibly can.

utterances.speaker: Justin Sanchez
utterances.text: Thank you.

chapters.summary: Sanchez asks about the scope of 70 new positions. Beaton explains the funding includes planners, engineers, concrete workers, signal timing specialists, and outreach staff across the entire program.
chapters.title: Sanchez on scope of new bus plan positions

utterances.speaker: Justin Sanchez
utterances.text: What is the scope of the new positions and is DOT hiring for these new positions now?

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgione
utterances.text: Well, it's FY27 funding.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgione
utterances.text: So as soon as we get the money, we're going to move quickly to staff up as quickly as we can.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgione
utterances.text: Tell all your friends.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgione
utterances.text: We're going to need a lot of good people.

utterances.speaker: Justin Sanchez
utterances.text: I have some folks in the audience here who are willing to apply for work.

utterances.speaker: Justin Sanchez
utterances.text: Not kidding.

utterances.speaker: Justin Sanchez
utterances.text: So we're definitely going to spread the word.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgione
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Justin Sanchez
utterances.text: What is the scope for these new positions?

utterances.speaker: Eric Beaton
utterances.text: Yeah, so one of the things that we really did very thoughtfully and the mayor funded is that this isn't just more planners, you know, more people to think about where we should do bus lanes.

utterances.speaker: Eric Beaton
utterances.text: It includes the engineers who design them and it includes work throughout our operations, includes people for new concrete elements and new people for signal timing changes and just all the things that go into making a bus lane successful.

utterances.speaker: Eric Beaton
utterances.text: So we were very pleased.

utterances.speaker: Eric Beaton
utterances.text: is that it's not just sort of starter money, it's really the money to plan and deliver the entire program.

chapters.summary: Lee asks what percentage of bus funding goes to implementation. Flynn estimates 50-70% goes to actual bus lanes, with the rest supporting related work. Lee also asks about new bus shelters, with 100 planned this year.
chapters.title: Lee on public engagement and implementation percentage for bus lanes

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Does any part of this funding go towards public engagement?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: How much and what specific actions would be taken to engage the public?

utterances.speaker: Eric Beaton
utterances.text: So absolutely, public engagement is a key part of expanding any project, including bus lands.

utterances.speaker: Eric Beaton
utterances.text: And just an example of that, as was mentioned before, we have our street ambassador on street units that go out and meet people at bus stops, go business to business down corridors.

utterances.speaker: Eric Beaton
utterances.text: And as we're doing more of these projects, we want to make sure we're not just spreading that group more thinly and doing less outreach, but we're staffing up that group, for example, to be able to do the same amount or more outreach with each of the projects that we do.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: As a percentage, how much of this funding is dedicated to the implementation of bus lanes?

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: Thank you for the question, Chair.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: So as Deputy Commissioner Beaton said, a bus lane can be many different things.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: I'd say on average it's probably about 50 to 70% of it will be for the actual bus lanes, and there will be support for all this work in the new need as well.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: When you get the percentage, I would be grateful if you can share that with us.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: How many new bus shelters will be installed this year, particularly in transit deserts and along high ridership routes?

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: So we're going to be installing an additional 100 shelters this year, and thanks to the investment in this executive budget, we're able to install another 100 per year for the next two years as well.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Great.

chapters.summary: Lee asks if DOT will meet the 30-mile bus lane mandate under Local Law 195 and what factors determine BRT implementation. Flynn discusses ridership, bus speeds, and transit-oriented development as key factors.
chapters.title: Lee on 30-mile bus lane mandate and BRT factors

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: With this funding, will DLT be able to meet the 30 miles of bus lanes as mandated by Local Law 195?

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: As I mentioned, we're working as hard as we can to deliver as many and as much output as we can with the new funding.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: With respect to bus rapid transit, bus rapid transit is a high-capacity bus-based urban transit system designed to combine the speed and efficiency of rail transit with flexibility and lower cost of buses.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: What factors does DOT consider when deciding to implement BRT?

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: Thank you for the question, Chair.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: Yeah, we're really excited, and we know there's things we can learn from other cities around the world and really taking our bus priority efforts to the next level.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: We're really excited that Flatbush Avenue is in construction as we speak, which is really going to be one of the first on-the-ground examples of this type of approach.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: That's something, you know, in the BRT spirit.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: I'm trying to remember now the question was around...

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: What factors does your agency consider when deciding to implement?

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: That's right.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: Yeah, so certainly ridership, certainly bus speeds, but we also want to be looking forward to and think about, you know, what's the potential folks may have seen in the...

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: announcement, I guess it was yesterday or the day before, that the administration's looking to sort of pair transit-oriented development and affordable housing with things like BRT or other rapid transit.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: So these are all factors that we consider, and we also work closely with our partners at the MTA because they're also a big piece of this.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: A lot of the focus is on the street design and all that.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: But the other really important part is how MTA runs the buses and making sure we can maximize the speeds and the throughput.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Does DOT have a process in place at the moment for BRT approval?

chapters.summary: Lee asks about DOT's BRT approval process and puts on record her support for a BRT system along 125th Street in Harlem, urging DOT to prioritize it.
chapters.title: Lee on BRT approval process and 125th Street corridor

utterances.speaker: Eric Beaton
utterances.text: So we have a transit development unit that works, as the commissioner said, in very close partnership with the MTA.

utterances.speaker: Eric Beaton
utterances.text: And as part of looking at a corridor, one of the things that they do is look at all the data, look at the street design, and figure out where these higher levels of investments are appropriate.

utterances.speaker: Eric Beaton
utterances.text: So that's something that we've been starting to look at as part of the new bus plan, and I think there'll be more details as part of that.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And I would state for the record that, you know, my office has sent DLT a letter, as well as other advocates have sent DLT a letter

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: in support of a BRT bus rapid transit system along 25th Street, which would, you know, touch my district as well as other districts throughout Harlem.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: It's something that I support.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: But now the ball is on your court is where I will leave it.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: But this is something that for us is very important.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: We want to put on the record.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: I think it's a great place for thinking big and really making sure that Harlem gets the attention it deserves.

chapters.summary: Lee asks what the $11.2M cycling funding will go toward. Beaton explains it covers planners, outreach specialists, concrete work, and markings program to deliver projects, not just plan them.
chapters.title: Lee on cycling and micromobility funding allocation

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: I'll move over to cycling and micromobility.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: The executive plan adds funding for cycling and micromobility.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: It includes 11.2 million and 45 new positions in fiscal 2027, 8.6 million and 52 new positions in fiscal 2028, reaching 22.8 million and 54 new positions by fiscal 2030.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: What will this funding go towards and how many bike lanes will be installed because of this funding?

utterances.speaker: Eric Beaton
utterances.text: Sure.

utterances.speaker: Eric Beaton
utterances.text: So in terms of what it goes towards, it's a little bit similar to what we talked about with the bus plan earlier, where it's both the planners and outreach specialists who can

utterances.speaker: Eric Beaton
utterances.text: get the project moving, but it also really reinforces the operations around the agency in terms of our ability to deliver.

utterances.speaker: Eric Beaton
utterances.text: So it includes money for concrete work.

utterances.speaker: Eric Beaton
utterances.text: It includes money for our markings program.

utterances.speaker: Eric Beaton
utterances.text: Again, we want to be very committed to not just saying we're coming up with a lot of ideas, but to really delivering them.

chapters.summary: Lee asks about average bike lane cost and whether DOT will build 50 miles of protected bike lanes in FY27. Flynn says costs vary widely and they expect substantial increase in output with historic investment.
chapters.title: Lee on bike lane installation costs and 50-mile target

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: On average, how much does it cost to install a bike lane?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Do you have that information?

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: There's definitely a lot of variables, I think, as much or maybe in some ways even more than the bus lanes, because it depends on the specifics of the design of the bike lane, the concrete, islands, or traffic signal changes.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: So I'm not sure if we have a rough range.

utterances.speaker: Eric Beaton
utterances.text: We can get back to you with the range, but as the commissioner said, it's a very wide range, right?

utterances.speaker: Eric Beaton
utterances.text: A fully built out greenway, you know, where we did get a lot of capital money in this budget as well, but the cost to that is very different from a painted on street lane.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Yeah, if the agency could come up with some type of breakdowns.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Of square miles for this type of bike lane versus that type of bike lane.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Just something for us to have some baseline of how this stuff gets priced out.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: That would be very helpful for us and for members of the public.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: Will do.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: With this funding, will DOT be able to build out the 50 miles of protected bike lanes in fiscal 27?

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: So, you know, again, I'm not able to give an exact number yet, but it is a historic level of investment, and therefore we're expecting

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: a very substantial increase in the output of bike lane.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Compared to years past, you'd say?

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: Yes.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: And increasing even more in the future years, right?

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: And I didn't mention that earlier, but part of it, of course, is once we get the funding in July, as we hire up, you know, as we expand these contracts, et cetera, all the things it takes to build these,

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: we're going to do as much as we can this year, but we also expect very significant growth in future years.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: I'm going to ask another set of questions, the last set of questions before I pass it on, and then I'll come back for more.

chapters.summary: Lee asks about DOT's World Cup role. Flynn describes two roles: creating celebration opportunities for New Yorkers and traffic management, noting dynamic real-time planning across multiple agencies.
chapters.title: Lee on DOT's role in upcoming World Cup and Knicks finals

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: I have questions on the Knicks and the World Cup.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: This summer, New York City region will play host to a round of group matches and the final match of the World Cup.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And in a few weeks, our very own New York Knicks will be playing in the NBA finals.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Has the administration outlined to DOT what their role will be in the upcoming World Cup and what does the scope of the involvement entail?

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: Thank you, Chair.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: Yeah, we're really excited about the World Cup and also about the Knicks.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: Go Knicks.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: And, you know, I think the mayor has said, and I agree, that the World Cup presents an exciting opportunity not only to show our city off to the world, but to make sure that all New Yorkers benefit from these.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: So that's kind of, I think DOT's role is twofold.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: Number one, we're really trying to leverage these events, World Cup in particular, to create opportunities for New Yorkers to celebrate and feel like they're a part of the World Cup, even if they're not going to see a match in person.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: And there's been some announcements around that, and there's more to come in the coming weeks around watch parties and that kind of stuff.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: I think also we had the announcement around the soccer streets that we're doing to engage school kids.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: And then the other side of it is obviously more like the traffic management side, right, and trying to ensure that all this activity doesn't,

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: that it works smoothly and that we put our best foot forward for visitors, but also that it's not inconveniencing New Yorkers while all this stuff is happening.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: And my colleagues could provide more details, but that's kind of our overall role.

chapters.summary: Lee asks about Streetsblog reports of canceled watch parties. Flynn says there was no final set of events scaled down, describing dynamic planning conversations happening in real time.
chapters.title: Lee on reports of canceled World Cup watch parties

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Over a month ago, the Streets blog reported that there have been watch parties that had been canceled.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Is that true?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Or city events that have been scaled down by any chance?

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: Well, you know, what I'll say is for myself, like coming into this role and realizing that the World Cup was five, six months away, it's a lot.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: And so a lot of these conversations and planning have been happening in a very dynamic, real-time way.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: Multiple agencies are involved, and there's a lot of different pieces.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: So everything's been kind of, you know.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: It's been dynamic, but we're excited with where we've landed.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: We think it's going to be a wonderful series of activities.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: and events for New Yorkers.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Was it DOT's decision to scale down events, or is that a different agency?

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: There was no final set of events that was scaled down.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: Like I said, it's a lot of conversations and planning that have been happening in real time.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: All right, thank you.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Great, thank you.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Just want to recognize we've been joined by Council Members Hanif and Murano on Zoom and also Council Member Brooks Powers.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And just as we go into member questions, we just want to remind colleagues and of course, if we could keep questions and responses both succinct because we want to make sure we get through everyone.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And of course, if any members wants to go for round two, let us know.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And we have, we're going to start with Council Member Wan followed by Council Member Lewis.

chapters.summary: Won asks whether new dollars have been added for hardened daylighting by the Mamdani administration. Beaton explains the new bus and bike lane funding includes hardened daylighting as part of those projects, though not as a separate line item.
chapters.title: Won on new daylighting funding from Mamdani administration

utterances.speaker: Julie Won
utterances.text: Thank you so much, Chair Lee.

utterances.speaker: Julie Won
utterances.text: So I want to break it down a little bit further.

utterances.speaker: Julie Won
utterances.text: So when it comes to a road, an intersection is usually four corners, and you're repeatedly saying it's $10,000 for an intersection.

utterances.speaker: Julie Won
utterances.text: But then you're saying that the intersection is a location.

utterances.speaker: Julie Won
utterances.text: There are eight per intersection.

utterances.speaker: Julie Won
utterances.text: So now you're redefining intersection, which we constantly feel like there's a lot of disagreement on.

utterances.speaker: Julie Won
utterances.text: Then it's less than $3 billion, even with that calculation as well, $3 billion.

utterances.speaker: Julie Won
utterances.text: So in relation to the mayor campaigning, is there a new...

utterances.speaker: Julie Won
utterances.text: Are there new dollars added to this current administration for harder daylighting, yes or no?

utterances.speaker: Julie Won
utterances.text: Because at the last budget hearing, you said that the only daylighting funding that you have is a rollover from...

utterances.speaker: Julie Won
utterances.text: The Eric Adams administration.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: Well, I just, I mean, I want to start by saying that I apologize if there's been any miscommunication or misunderstanding around how we're defining or how we're laying out the costs.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: You know, as I mentioned earlier, we're here in good faith to work with you to figure out the best pathway forward on daylighting.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: On cost, you were asking about cost, Councilmember?

utterances.speaker: Julie Won
utterances.text: Yeah, as new funding been added by the Zoran M.

utterances.speaker: Julie Won
utterances.text: M.

utterances.speaker: Julie Won
utterances.text: D.

utterances.speaker: Julie Won
utterances.text: Administration for daylighting, because at the last hearing, the only amount of dollars for hardened daylighting was from the Eric Adams'administration.

utterances.speaker: Julie Won
utterances.text: So are there any dollars added?

utterances.speaker: Eric Beaton
utterances.text: So there was a tremendous amount of money added to our budget.

utterances.speaker: Eric Beaton
utterances.text: It's primarily being called bus lane and bike lane.

utterances.speaker: Eric Beaton
utterances.text: But as part of a lot of those projects, we do include hardened daylighting as part of the safety treatment we bring to the corridor.

utterances.speaker: Eric Beaton
utterances.text: For example, most of our protected bike lanes include hardened daylighting going down the corridor to make sure that there's visibility for turning vehicles to be able to see cyclists.

utterances.speaker: Eric Beaton
utterances.text: So it's not called out as a separate line item, but the city is very much committed to and is funding these safety improvements.

utterances.speaker: Julie Won
utterances.text: So what is the dollar amount that you're going to set aside for hardened daylighting in your new added budget?

chapters.summary: Won asks for specific dollar amounts per fiscal year for hardened daylighting and how many intersections are budgeted. Flynn says they're confident they can exceed prior output but won't commit a specific line item.
chapters.title: Won demands dollar amount for hardened daylighting in budget

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: So just to the point that Deputy Commissioner Beaton was making, there's no line item, and we're not looking at it as one piece.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: Every time we do a project, we'll make sure if daylighting is an appropriate treatment.

utterances.speaker: Julie Won
utterances.text: So then help me understand, what civic street design elements are you looking at?

utterances.speaker: Julie Won
utterances.text: And can you help us understand what the criteria is exactly so that we can understand your criteria for your evaluations of which locations you will have hardened daylighting then?

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: Sure, and I just want to start by saying that we're very confident in our ability with the budget that we have now to exceed the prior administration's level of output of hardened daylighting.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: As Deputy Commissioner Beaton said, the bike lanes, the bus lanes, all the other Vision Zero work we do, we're making hardened daylighting a standard part of our approach.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: The nuance is just that there are cases, you know, we look at the crash data, and there's other treatments in addition to daylighting that are sometimes even more effective.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: So we really value the flexibility to use daylighting and or other approaches based on the situation.

utterances.speaker: Julie Won
utterances.text: So we would like to understand how many dollars are going to be spent.

utterances.speaker: Julie Won
utterances.text: For each fiscal year since you gave us a calculation of how much it will cost.

utterances.speaker: Julie Won
utterances.text: In return, we would like to understand how many intersections are in your budget for this fiscal year.

utterances.speaker: Julie Won
utterances.text: I'll turn it over back to the chair, but I would like to come back for a second round of questions.

utterances.speaker: Julie Won
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, great.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Councilmember Lewis followed by Councilmember Wong.

chapters.summary: Brooks-Powers asks about year-long waits for traffic studies, funding for traffic engineering staff, and why the city relies on federal standards that don't reflect dense urban neighborhoods. She also asks about contractor accountability for defective street restorations.
chapters.title: CM Brooks-Powers on traffic study delays and federal standards

utterances.speaker: Selvena Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: Thank you, Chair, and good morning, Commissioner, and to your whole team.

utterances.speaker: Selvena Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: I have four questions, so I'm going to just throw them out, and then hopefully you'll be able to respond.

utterances.speaker: Selvena Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: So the administration has made significant investments in traffic operations, and my first question targets traffic studies and federal design standards.

utterances.speaker: Selvena Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: I wanted to know...

utterances.speaker: Selvena Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: There are significant communities that are all going through this, but I wanted to know, despite these significant investments made by the administration, many communities continue to experience year-long waits for traffic studies, signal timing reviews,

utterances.speaker: Selvena Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: stop sign requests, and pedestrian safety improvements.

utterances.speaker: Selvena Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: I wanted to know how much of...

utterances.speaker: Selvena Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: How much the increase in funding is dedicated specifically to traffic engineering staff, study capacity, and what is the current average timeline from community requests to final agency determination for those studies.

utterances.speaker: Selvena Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: Also, given that New York City contributes significant local funding towards its transportation network,

utterances.speaker: Selvena Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: I wanted to know why is the city continuing to rely so heavily on standards, federal standards specifically, that make communities believe that they've...

utterances.speaker: Selvena Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: Communities believe they fail to reflect the realities of dense urban neighborhoods.

utterances.speaker: Selvena Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: I wanted to know how is DOT exploring alternatives to methodologies that better account for pedestrian

utterances.speaker: Selvena Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: and activity, senior population, schools, and houses of worship, and transit usage.

utterances.speaker: Selvena Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: That's still for traffic studies.

utterances.speaker: Selvena Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: And my last question is in regards to contractor accountability, utility costs, and cost recovery.

utterances.speaker: Selvena Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: I wanted to know, so my constituents frequently raise questions and concerns regarding utility contractor street restorations that fail shortly after work is completed,

utterances.speaker: Selvena Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: leaving residents with deteriorated roadways despite recent construction activity.

utterances.speaker: Selvena Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: So how many summonses or enforcement actions did DOT issue in fiscal year 26 against utilities and contractors for defective street restorations?

utterances.speaker: Selvena Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: How much money did the city actually recover through those penalties, corrective work requirements and reimbursements?

utterances.speaker: Selvena Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: And is DOT seeking additional resources in the budget to strengthen this enforcement function?

utterances.speaker: Selvena Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Selvena Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: I know that was a lot.

chapters.summary: Flynn addresses federal standards and NACTO partnership. Forgione discusses staffing up for traffic studies and reports 474,000 inspections with 88% compliance rate for permitted street work.
chapters.title: Flynn and Forgione respond on traffic studies and utility inspections

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: No, thank you, Council Member.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: Those are great questions.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: I'll just speak briefly to, I think, one of the points, and then maybe let the team chime in on some of them.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: But regarding federal standards, definitely appreciate that point.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: And I think, you know, we would agree that

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: cities are very different than, let's say, rural areas or even suburban, and we really do seek to maximize our flexibility in how we approach these things.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: It's also why we have a great

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: partnership with organizations like NACTO that advocates for cities interests.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: So I just wanted to mention that.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: I think then there was the funding for traffic engineering staff.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: And the utility cars.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: So for traffic operations staff, we already have a robust team that look at signal and other traffic devices.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: With this budget, we're adding another 20 people as part of the bus and bike.

utterances.speaker: Selvena Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: And those 20 are dispersed citywide.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: They look at the whole entire city, and it's not like they're just going to focus on each distinct piece.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: They're going to continue to move all the pieces of the traffic operations portfolio forward.

utterances.speaker: Selvena Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgione
utterances.text: Okay, you packed a powerful question, a lot of good stuff in there.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgione
utterances.text: So I think first and foremost, what you're getting at is how can we be more responsive to communities?

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgione
utterances.text: People believe they have a problem.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgione
utterances.text: Correct.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgione
utterances.text: And we're taking too long to look into some of these things, like traffic signal studies, which we do agree that is taking a long time, as well as the ability to then, if we find a traffic signal is warranted, to put that in a contract.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgione
utterances.text: So we're working really hard to staff up in some of these areas and also to make sure we have more contracting capacity, which we should have this coming year.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgione
utterances.text: But what I'd like to urge council members, as you see an issue, we want to get with you and your constituents on that issue.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgione
utterances.text: We have mobile speed camera units, for example.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgione
utterances.text: We can put those out if it falls within our criteria for that.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgione
utterances.text: We can look at some other measures for traffic calming.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgione
utterances.text: Some things we can do in the short run.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgione
utterances.text: We can even look at a longer-term redesign if that's warranted.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgione
utterances.text: But we want to make sure you and your constituents know that's what we're here for.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgione
utterances.text: We will try to address those needs.

utterances.speaker: Selvena Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: That will be helpful.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgione
utterances.text: And you mentioned MUTCD, this manual for uniform traffic control devices.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgione
utterances.text: So that is something that we do need to comply with to retain a federal standard across the whole country.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgione
utterances.text: However, we are being proactive, as you mentioned.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgione
utterances.text: You mentioned more pedestrian-focused warrants and things of that nature.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgione
utterances.text: We recently worked with the federal government in order to be able to use micromobility in our studies, which is, of course, something new, and we were effective at getting that in, and we would want to keep refining as we go forward.

utterances.speaker: Selvena Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: We look forward to that.

utterances.speaker: Selvena Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Selvena Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: Thank you, Chair.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgione
utterances.text: And we do have some real quick.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgione
utterances.text: Yes.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgione
utterances.text: Inspection information, so the last full fiscal year for fiscal 25, we conducted 474,000

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgione
utterances.text: inspections of permitted work out in the streets.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgione
utterances.text: 88% of those inspections showed that they were in compliance and then they passed basically our inspection.

utterances.speaker: Selvena Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: How many were not in compliance?

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgione
utterances.text: That would leave 12%.

utterances.speaker: Selvena Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgione
utterances.text: And that could be something like they're working outside of

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgione
utterances.text: permitted hours or a street cut was not properly backfilled or something like that.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, thank you.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Thank you, Chair.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Just wanted to recognize we've enjoyed my Councilmember Krishnan.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And before we go to Councilmember Wong, I just wanted to give a special shout out to the students in the balcony from PSIS 155.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Thank you so much for being here today.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: I know you're from Councilmember Rita Joseph's district, who I see back there.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: But thank you so much for coming to observe how civic participation in city government works.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, so Councilmember Wong, followed by Councilmember Epstein.

chapters.summary: Phil Wong raises a fatal e-bike/scooter collision on Queensborough Bridge involving a device capable of 53 mph, asking what DOT and the Finance Committee can do to enforce speed limits and ban high-speed e-scooters.
chapters.title: CM Wong on e-bike safety after fatal Queensborough Bridge crash

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Okay, thank you, Chairs.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Commissioner Flynn, at the last hearing, I was asking about how can we, as the committee, how can DOT improve the safety of

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: pedestrians from e-bike accidents and unfortunately I got to talk about it again today because yesterday

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: 830 there were there was a e-bike that had a head-on collision with a scooter on Queensborough Bridge and unfortunately both both men were killed

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: I looked at the pictures and based upon what I saw, the e-bike involved was this Blade GT2, which has a speed of up to 53 miles per hour.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: And I asked this before and here I am again, what can we do?

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: do is the finance committee and what can you do as DOT to enforce speed limits and why are these high speed e-scooters be allowed to be on the streets?

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Please talk about it.

chapters.summary: Flynn expresses condolences, notes one rider used an illegal stand-up e-scooter capable of over 50 mph, and underscores that highest-speed devices are not appropriate for NYC streets, requiring education and confiscation.
chapters.title: Flynn responds on e-bike tragedy and illegal high-speed devices

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: Yeah, thank you so much for the question, Council Member.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: And, you know, I want to start by saying yesterday's crash was a tragedy, and our hearts are with the victims, their families and loved ones.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: You know, it's tough, too, because...

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: Pretty recently, we were celebrating the opening of the South Outer Roadway on the bridge that separated pedestrians and cyclists.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: And, you know, to be clear, we do see that as a major step forward in safety for those cycling over the bridge.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: But nevertheless, we clearly have work to do.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: I know the NYPD investigation is ongoing, but based on the facts as we understand them, one of the riders was using a stand-up.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: e-scooter capable of going over 50 miles an hour, which is very much illegal in New York City.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: So, you know, that I think it really underscores and we hear it and there's a lot of confusion, even myself sometimes, like there's so many different kinds of devices.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: Some are legal, some are not.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: Are they pedal assist?

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: Are they throttle?

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: Stand up, sit down, all of that, different classes.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: To me, what it underscores is that the highest speed

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: devices are not appropriate for New York City streets.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: And by and large, our laws reflect that.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: The question is, how do we get them off our streets?

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: Because again, the stand-up scooter involved yesterday, it wasn't legal to begin with.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: So, you know, we work closely with NYPD and we have a good partnership, but clearly it underscores that as these devices proliferate, we really have even more work to do to get them off of our streets.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: That's educating consumers, businesses, but also just confiscating them.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Yes, and if this budget is an issue, please talk to the finance chair of this committee.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: How can we help?

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Because we just don't want to see any more of these fatalities, and it's very unfortunate for we to even talk about this again.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: Absolutely.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: We appreciate that.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Do I have time for one more question?

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Okay, I'll do round two.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, perfect.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Councilmember Epstein, followed by Councilmember Marano on Zoom.

chapters.summary: Epstein asks about community board input in street design decisions and requests bus lane expansions in lower Manhattan, including Madison Avenue and the 14A bus.
chapters.title: CM Epstein on community engagement and bus pilots in lower Manhattan

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: Thank you, Chair.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: And we'll follow up with a council member's questions around pedestrian safety.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: I know you said you do a lot of community engagement when you're making street design changes.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: How much are you committed to ensuring that the community board and local leaders'feedback is taken into consideration when making decisions?

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: And changes?

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: Absolutely.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: We value the input that we get from communities, from electeds, from community boards, and it's one of the important factors that we take into account.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: I really do appreciate it because of a community.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: community board says we don't want to do something and the elected say we don't want to do something, it sounds like DOT should side with the local elected and the community board in their decisions.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: So I want to hear from you, Commissioner.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: Well, I think at the end of the day, we have an important mission to save New Yorkers'lives, and so we're balancing all different factors.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: Right, exactly.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: So we're talking about saving New Yorkers'lives and pedestrians, and I appreciate you listening to communities when issues come up like that.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: I know you mentioned expansion of bus pilots, and I really appreciate you wanting to do that.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: It's really important to expand those bus pilots.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: Can you, in lower Manhattan, do, I know we talked about Madison Avenue, I've talked to you a lot about the 14A bus.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: Are there any expansions potentially coming for lower Manhattan at this point?

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: Around buses specifically?

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: Yes, bus lanes.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: You said speeding up buses.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: You know, this is something I've been working on for many, many years prior to coming to the council.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: I'm wondering if you have any proposed projects coming to Lower Manhattan.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: We will have the Faster Buses Action Plan coming out in the coming weeks, and there'll be a lot of big ideas in there.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: We're excited to share.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: We're excited to work with you and other partners to basically help flesh out what those projects look like.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: And we're prioritizing routes, like I said earlier, based on ridership or bus speeds or things like that.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: So I don't...

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: I'm not sure specifically about Manhattan, but...

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: Sure, sure.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: I'm sure we have some ideas there.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: I would love that.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: And, you know, we talked about the Avenue A bus lane.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: I'd love to see that expanded as we've talked about before.

chapters.summary: Epstein requests at least one publicly available EV charging station in lower Manhattan after asking DOT for 10 years. Flynn commits to working with him on finding the best locations.
chapters.title: Epstein on EV charging infrastructure in lower Manhattan

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: I want to talk about charging infrastructure.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: We're talking about trying to be more environmentally sound, putting publicly available charging infrastructure in place.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: I've been asking DOT for the last 10 years to make one publicly available charging station in lower Manhattan someplace.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: Can you commit to making publicly available chargers available in lower Manhattan for people who want to convert to electric vehicles?

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: Well, you know, I'll start by just reinforcing, you know, absolutely we're working across with all the levers we have to try to expand EV charging.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: Starting, you know, with the starting point being that we want to expand options for New Yorkers to walk, bike, and take transit, but we know some folks have to travel by car sometimes, and we certainly want them to be using electric vehicles in those instances.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: So we're really taking in all of the above approach to EV charging, and that includes on and off street, level two, and fast charging.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: One thing we're doing is trying to prioritize neighborhoods where we see a lot of higher vehicle drivers live, taxi drivers, things like that, and prioritize charging in those areas.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: But we're definitely rolling it out across the city, and I can definitely commit to working with you to figure out the best places to put it.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: I'd love to see at least one in lower Manhattan, and I appreciate you working with me on that.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: I know I'm out of time, so I'll come back later, Chair.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: Thank you, Councilmember.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Oh, sorry.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Councilmember Ressler followed by Councilmember Justin Sanchez.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Is he here?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, we'll see if he comes back.

chapters.summary: Restler asks whether DOT has added funding to comply with the local law requiring pedestrian lighting on 300 commercial corridor blocks annually starting January 2027. DOT officials say they're in active coordination with OMB on funding needs.
chapters.title: CM Restler on pedestrian lighting mandate and funding

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Thank you so much, Chair Lee and Chair Abreu for your leadership of this hearing.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: And thank you to the DOT team for joining us today.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: I will say I generally feel that we have leadership at City Hall and leadership in the agency that have the right values and the right vision.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: I still think that there are areas where the bureaucracy within the agency is not working well, and I'm really hoping that we're going to start to see tangible progress on those fronts in the weeks and months to come.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: So I want to highlight a few of those areas in my questioning today.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: First up is pedestrian lighting.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Last year we passed a local law.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: To require DOT to install pedestrian lighting on 300 commercial corridor blocks every year.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: The law takes effect January 1st of 2027, so this fiscal year.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Has DOT added funding for this to comply with this legislation, and are you confident in your ability to be in compliance this coming year?

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: I can just, I'll start out and then maybe first deputy commissioner, you want to speak about the ped lighting.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: Yeah, I just want to underscore in so many of the conversations I've had since starting this job, it's really become clear how important lighting is to New Yorkers and to council members and communities.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: It's something that people really feel day to day.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: So it's something I can assure you I'm very focused on, street lighting across the board.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: But maybe you want to speak to the ped lighting specifically.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgione
utterances.text: Yes, and before I mention that, I also want to mention that we do have significant funding in our budget to replace the LEDs around the whole city, which will also include a component that will alert us if any given

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgione
utterances.text: street light is out or needs attention.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgione
utterances.text: So, you know, we're very committed to this area, both keeping up with what we currently have and then making sure we have additional lighting.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgione
utterances.text: We are working through with OMB on how we're going to comply with the council law.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Just to be clear, there's no additional funding in the budget to comply with the law this time?

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgione
utterances.text: Well, we did get a lot of funding for street lighting, and we're going to have to work through that.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Do you have funding in the budget to comply with the law?

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Do you have the funding to comply with the law?

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: It's just a yes or no question.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: We're in active coordination with OMB and City Hall on all funding needs.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: But you have your...

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Executive budget.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Do you have the funding to be able to comply or not?

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: I just want a yes or no.

utterances.speaker: Eric Beaton
utterances.text: We're in active coordination with the law.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: That's not a yes or no.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Could you please answer the question?

utterances.speaker: Eric Beaton
utterances.text: We have a significant amount of funding for lighting, and we're evaluating all needs.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Can you comply with the law with the resources you have?

utterances.speaker: Eric Beaton
utterances.text: That's exactly what we're evaluating, Councilmember.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Why can you not answer that question if you know what the funding is that you have in the executive budget?

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgione
utterances.text: Councilmember, we're in the planning stages.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgione
utterances.text: And as you noted, there's still more time to comply.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: I just, you sense the frustration in my voice, A, because you're not answering the question, but B, because whenever we have lighting needs across our district, the best case scenario is three or four years to address them, and it's often much longer.

chapters.summary: Restler expresses frustration over 3-4 year waits for lighting needs and asks about average time from traffic light approval to installation. Forgione says about six months, with more contracting capacity coming online.
chapters.title: Restler on traffic light installation timeline and bureaucratic delays

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: We identify issues where DOT says these streets are dark, which leads to major safety conditions, which undermines how small businesses do in our community, and yet we see...

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Incredible slowness.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: I mean, not, I'm sure it's a different portion of DOT, but lighting related.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: When DOT approves the need for a new traffic light, what's the average time right now from approval to installation of a new traffic light?

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgione
utterances.text: It's a number of months.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgione
utterances.text: We do have more contracting capacity coming online.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Do you track that?

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Do you have a date?

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Is there a time set for how long it takes once a traffic light is approved to install it?

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: What's the average time today?

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgione
utterances.text: It's about six months.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Okay, we're getting estimates of even longer.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: But you understand how frustrating it is for folks in our community.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: We go to DOT, we say there's major safety issues at a corner, there's tracks.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Crashes at a corner.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: People are dying at this corner.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: We finally get a study done.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: We finally get approval.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: And then it's six, eight, nine months before there's any change on the ground.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: No, I hear your frustration, absolutely, Council Member.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: And, you know, I think I speak for everyone that we have no interest in things taking longer than they should.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: It's something we're very focused on.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: As First Deputy Commissioner mentioned, some of it is tied up in contracts, and we're really glad that we're going to have additional capacity coming online.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: So I just, you know, I'm over time.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: I didn't even get to ask about outdoor dining.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: The morass that that is.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: But I just, this is, just as my opening statement is where I'll close.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: I'm confident in the values, I'm confident in the vision.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: There are components of the bureaucracy in this agency that are not working well, and they need to be addressed.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: And we need rigorous leadership, strong focus on fixing these issues, or we're not gonna see the improvements that we all are committed to seeing in this administration.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: Thank you, Councilmember.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Thank you, Councilmember.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Selvena Brooks-Powers, followed by Councilmember Krishnan.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Oh, and I'm sorry, we've been joined by Councilmembers Banks and also our Deputy Speaker.

chapters.summary: Brooks-Powers asks about protected bus lane mileage, pothole repairs in Southeast Queens, street lighting outages on Rockaway Beach Boulevard, and dangerous pothole conditions at the Rockaway YMCA entrance.
