chapters.title: Brooks-Powers on bus lanes, potholes, and Rockaway street lighting

utterances.speaker: Selvena Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: Thank you, Chairs, and thank you, Commissioner and Commissioners, for your testimony today.

utterances.speaker: Selvena Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: I'm going to ask all my questions at once.

utterances.speaker: Selvena Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: I can repeat anything that you need me to thereafter.

utterances.speaker: Selvena Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: The executive plan includes an additional $16.9 million of city funding in fiscal year 27 for the bus action plan.

utterances.speaker: Selvena Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: 25 DOT completed nearly 21 miles of protected bus lanes short of the 30 mile requirement set by the New York City Streets Plan.

utterances.speaker: Selvena Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: With this additional funding in place for fiscal year 27, how many miles of protected bus lane does DOT

utterances.speaker: Selvena Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: plan to build in the upcoming fiscal year.

utterances.speaker: Selvena Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: This executive budget also allocates an additional $3 million for a pothole blitz.

utterances.speaker: Selvena Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: We continue to see terrible road conditions in the 31st Council District.

utterances.speaker: Selvena Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: Can you share which roads in Southeast Queens and the Rockaway Peninsula

utterances.speaker: Selvena Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: DOT plans to repair with this additional funding.

utterances.speaker: Selvena Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: And can DOT provide an update regarding the street lighting along Rockway Beach Boulevard from Beach 62nd Street to Beach 73rd Street?

utterances.speaker: Selvena Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: These lights have been out for months now and cause a major public safety hazard to local residents.

utterances.speaker: Selvena Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: And then last, I'm going to pick up on an email exchange that we had with Commissioner Garcia earlier.

utterances.speaker: Selvena Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: In my district, there are persistent...

utterances.speaker: Selvena Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: In dangerous pothole conditions at the parking lot entrance of the Rockaway YMCA on Beach 73rd Street.

utterances.speaker: Selvena Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: The current state of the entrance is unacceptable.

utterances.speaker: Selvena Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: The large potholes pose a significant risk of vehicle damage.

utterances.speaker: Selvena Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: damage and potential physical injury to our youth and seniors who rely on the YMCA daily and or who just cross that corridor because we've had a number of crashes at that intersection as well.

utterances.speaker: Selvena Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: Could you please confirm if this location is currently on the DOT list for repairs?

utterances.speaker: Selvena Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: DOT has also indicated that that coordination with DEP is needed to move forward, but we can't wait any longer, so someone has to take the mantle on this.

utterances.speaker: Selvena Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: What is the timeline for this joint repair with DEP?

utterances.speaker: Selvena Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: And again, I can repeat anything that needs to be.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: Thank you so much, Council Member.

chapters.summary: Flynn expects historic bus lane output. Forgione commits to addressing the YMCA pothole within 24-48 hours, reports 100,000 pothole conditions addressed, and discusses the Rockaway lighting issue involving HPD coordination.
chapters.title: Flynn and Forgione respond on bus mileage and pothole repairs

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: I'll just kind of start at the top here.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: You asked about the bus action plan and bus mileage.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: One thing I also want to note is that the mileage is kind of comprised of two pieces.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: So there's the physical design work we do to create or upgrade bus lanes, busways, and the street design.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: And then there's also the automated camera enforcement from MTA.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: So you mentioned the mileage from last year.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: So, you know, our bus program for this year, it's in active development, so I don't have an exact number, but what I can say is that it's a historic level of investment that we're seeing

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: starting in July, and therefore we're expecting a historic level of output this year, significantly exceeding past years when it comes to mileage, and even more in future years in terms of output.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: I think next you asked about pothole blitz and just generally paving conditions in southeast Queens.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgione
utterances.text: I noted the location that you just provided at the entrance to the parking lot, so we will get out there in the next 24 to 48 hours.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgione
utterances.text: We will address that.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgione
utterances.text: You asked about pothole repair as a result of the funding.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgione
utterances.text: The funding is paying for all of those pothole blitzes that we had, where we addressed over 100,000 pothole conditions.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgione
utterances.text: But at this moment, in terms of 311 input for potholes, we have only a few hundred.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgione
utterances.text: We can keep up with potholes now that we've gotten through the worst of it.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgione
utterances.text: So in general, for you or for the whole council, for the public, any potholes, let us know.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgione
utterances.text: Call them into 311.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgione
utterances.text: You just call us anytime, and we will have them addressed very quickly.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgione
utterances.text: You also mentioned, I think, Auburn by the Sea, the lighting issue, Councilmember.

utterances.speaker: Selvena Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: Yes, we've been working on that since, as you know, since last year.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgione
utterances.text: Correct.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgione
utterances.text: Our borough commissioner updated me a few days ago that we're doing everything we can to help the developer and HPD understand exactly what light fixtures need to get installed

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgione
utterances.text: to the extent that we are even going to provide one that they can use and then they can purchase more to have them all rectified and replaced.

utterances.speaker: Selvena Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: I think it's pretty alarming how long it's taken with coordination with sister agencies with something that's a public safety matter, like literally with lighting being completely out and for such a duration of time.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgione
utterances.text: Okay, we are...

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgione
utterances.text: 100% available to assist in any way.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgione
utterances.text: Those streets don't belong to us.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgione
utterances.text: No, they belong to HPD.

utterances.speaker: Selvena Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: But there needs to be greater coordination because DOT is still responsible for the conditions of the streets.

utterances.speaker: Selvena Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: I understand because of the...

utterances.speaker: Selvena Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: Urban development that took place is under HPD right now, but there has to be coordination.

utterances.speaker: Selvena Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: It can't continue to be finger pointing between the agencies.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgione
utterances.text: No, I understand and I agree.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgione
utterances.text: So we are providing actually several light poles to them in order to really help them understand what has to happen.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgione
utterances.text: We'll be on the ground with them at any moment that they need us out there to help figure out the problem.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Great.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Councilmember Krishnan, followed by Councilmember Banks.

chapters.summary: Krishnan asks about open streets funding allocation and raises concerns about mopeds creating dangerous conditions on the 34th Avenue open street, suggesting Northern Boulevard as a solution for class three motor vehicles.
chapters.title: CM Krishnan on open streets funding and moped safety

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: Thank you so much, Chairs.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: Good to see you all.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: Commissioner, First Deputy Commissioner, DOT team.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: I said a few questions.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: So one, and I apologize if this came up before, too, but as you all know, we've been very close partners working together along our Open Street program across the city, especially in Jackson Heights, where we got the most successful one, in my non-biased opinion.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: But I just want to get a sense of what funding have you allocated for the program and how are you envisioning for the program overall across the city and how are you envisioning prioritizing open streets with the new budget?

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: Thank you, Council Member.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: Yeah, so we're really glad that thanks to the $6.4 million that was added to the budget, we're able to keep the baseline for the open streets.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: Also excited that we have, I guess, last week we announced the launch of the open street season, and we already have over 150 initial locations, which is about 20% more than where we were at this point last year, and still more to come.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: So that's great.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: Yeah, so our intention is really to keep the program growing and actually to keep kind of innovating and thinking across the board with all of our open space and public realm initiatives, how we can

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: support communities, how we can build in even better structures from an operations and maintenance standpoint.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: Great.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: I'm glad to hear it.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: I'm glad to hear that's a priority, too.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: And I wanted to mention one thing with that as well, and I appreciate you and your team, Commissioner, and the Deputy Mayor coming out to visit our open street.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: As you know, and Council Member Wong mentioned this too, it was very, very tragic to read the story about what happened on the Queensborough Bridge with the e-bike and our scooter and cyclist.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: My real, real concern, and you all know it well too, is that we can have similar.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: We've had narrow misses or children hit by mopeds on our open street on 34th Avenue, which is very successful.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: And I'm very concerned about this conflict between mopeds that are taking over and creating a highway there in a pedestrian space.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: And so what I'd love to keep working on together is we really need to look at Northern Boulevard as a solution for mopeds, which are class three motor vehicles.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: They belong with cars, not with people.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: And I think to get the open street to be successful, we need to really solve this moped situation in an effective way.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: And that involves looking at other places for micromobility uses and lanes.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: And I'd hope to be able to work with you all on doing so.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: Thank you, Council Member.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: I agree that, you know, as we've seen more proliferation of these two-wheeled devices, and again, some are classified as e-bikes, some, like you mentioned, are actually should be in the road with, you know, motor vehicles.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: This is really an area of focus for us, and we know it's very urgent for a lot of New Yorkers as well.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: So we're always happy to look at big ideas.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: and figure out how to solve these things.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: Well, I look forward to that work together, too, on Northern itself.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: And my last question is on Northern Boulevard, we have a bus lane now, which has been very effective, and we appreciate all your work on that, too.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: The biggest problem we're seeing, and I see it myself, is on the weekends, at nighttime,

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: So many cars are double parked and use it as a parking lot, basically.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: And it's down miles in front of all the bars there, too.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: And the biggest issue there is enforcement.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: So can we work together to actually create an effective enforcement system to make sure that the bus-only lanes are for buses?

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: Absolutely.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: You know, I think managing our curbs and having, you know, streets that are efficient and work for everyone, that's really the next frontier.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: And there's so much more we can be doing, which is why we created this Office of Curb Management in the agency.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: But certainly the bus lane specifically, the automated camera enforcement, and, you know, what other tools do we have?

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: We also work closely with NYPD.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: So the short answer is yes.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: Sure.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: We would love to partner with you.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: And we'll follow up on that, too.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: Thank you, Chair.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Great.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Councilmember Banks, followed by Deputy Speaker Williams.

chapters.summary: Chris Banks demands action on transferring streets from HPD to DOT jurisdiction in his district, noting years of delays, no alternate side parking, sanitation concerns, and abandoned cars. Forgione reports DDC is fixing streets to meet DOT standards.
chapters.title: CM Banks on HPD street jurisdiction transfer delays

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: Thank you, Chair.

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: Good to see you, Commissioner, and to your team.

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: Several streets within the Gateway area of my district, within the 42nd Council District, remains under ownership and jurisdiction of HPD.

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: Now, I'm frustrated as well because I've been in office for close to three years.

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: three and a half years or two and a half years.

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: And we've been getting the same thing, the same response from DOT that the transition is going to take place.

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: And we're still waiting.

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: We're still waiting.

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: So when are we going to finally get something done?

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: And I believe this even dates back going five, six, seven, eight, ten years.

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: To my predecessor, we need some solid answers as to when you're going to transfer the streets over the DOT.

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: Because currently, you know, the streets, there's no alternate side parking, there's sanitation concerns, there's abandonment of cars back there.

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: Our office is constantly getting calls and complaints to deal with those situations.

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: What we need, DOT, we need you to act.

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: We need DOT, HPD, start talking.

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: If you're not talking, figure it out.

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: But we need something done.

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: We can't, I can't leave this meeting today and get the same response we got the last time we spoke.

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: We need some type of action.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: I hear you loud and clear, Council Member.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: I can give an update.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgione
utterances.text: Yes, so the good news is that something is actually happening right now because the Department of Design and Construction funded.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgione
utterances.text: What's the something?

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: What's the something?

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgione
utterances.text: What's the acceptance date?

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: What's the something?

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: You said something is happening now?

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgione
utterances.text: Right, something good is happening in that they're fixing the streets to comply with DOT standards.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgione
utterances.text: So as you probably know, the streets were built in a way that we were.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgione
utterances.text: not going to be able to accept those streets into our ownership unless they were kind of brought up to the proper standards.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgione
utterances.text: They're being fixed by DDC.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgione
utterances.text: We're talking about it a lot internally.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgione
utterances.text: If you like, we can get on a call and understand when that work will be done and exactly the steps for us to then take the streets into our ownership.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgione
utterances.text: But we've been working closely to make sure that they're built in the way that we need them to be built.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgione
utterances.text: And so I think it's finally moving in the right direction.

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: So is there more work to be done?

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgione
utterances.text: I believe the work is happening.

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: Because I believe it has been completed.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgione
utterances.text: Okay, I'd have to double check if it's been fully.

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: Yeah, because a portion of the work was actually in front of my office.

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: And it was badly needed.

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: And also, when it comes to the actual...

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: The signs, the lot of no standing signs that are placed throughout the entire, that particular area, residents are getting ticketed, and construction is done back there.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgione
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: And that's another concern, another issue, because, you know, those are signs that shouldn't be there, you know.

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: Right, we want to centralize it.

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: Folks should be able to park there without those type of barriers that are there now.

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: So, again, we're looking forward to working with you on this because the residents, every time I get screamed at, I'm going to scream at you.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgione
utterances.text: Fair enough.

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgione
utterances.text: No problem.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgione
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgione
utterances.text: Deputy Speaker?

chapters.summary: Williams asks for camera location data by council district or zip code, noting members feel cameras are disproportionately located in certain communities. She also questions DOT's racial equity plan goals on fine and fee-based programs.
chapters.title: Deputy Speaker Williams on camera location data and racial equity

utterances.speaker: Nantasha M. Williams
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Nantasha M. Williams
utterances.text: I know that the chair asked some of these questions, but does DOT have a map of both red light and speed cameras, where they're located?

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: We don't.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: We don't have a public map, although I know that a lot of the driving apps have.

utterances.speaker: Nantasha M. Williams
utterances.text: So you have no, you have a map, it's just not public, but are you able to share that with the council?

utterances.speaker: Eric Beaton
utterances.text: It's intentionally not shared because we don't want to give the information to the public.

utterances.speaker: Nantasha M. Williams
utterances.text: I know, the public, but can you share it with the council?

utterances.speaker: Nantasha M. Williams
utterances.text: I know the cameras are not to be shared.

utterances.speaker: Eric Beaton
utterances.text: We can certainly follow up with you offline.

utterances.speaker: Nantasha M. Williams
utterances.text: I know because multiple members of the council have asked, and I know maybe you can't put like street corner, but can you at least even share like by zip code how many exist?

utterances.speaker: Nantasha M. Williams
utterances.text: If you can't give location.

utterances.speaker: Nantasha M. Williams
utterances.text: I don't understand why this is so difficult.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgione
utterances.text: Sorry to sound like we're being difficult.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgione
utterances.text: Council members, so as I mentioned earlier, we don't want people to adjust their behavior.

utterances.speaker: Nantasha M. Williams
utterances.text: I know.

utterances.speaker: Nantasha M. Williams
utterances.text: I'm not asking for the map to be shared publicly.

utterances.speaker: Nantasha M. Williams
utterances.text: I'm asking if you guys can share it with the council.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgione
utterances.text: And I'll be honest with you, even internally we don't share things.

utterances.speaker: Nantasha M. Williams
utterances.text: Okay, so can you share how many speed cameras and red light cameras are located?

utterances.speaker: Nantasha M. Williams
utterances.text: Are located.

utterances.speaker: Nantasha M. Williams
utterances.text: By zip code.

utterances.speaker: Nantasha M. Williams
utterances.text: That's not, that has nothing to do with actual location.

utterances.speaker: Nantasha M. Williams
utterances.text: You can say, or even by council district.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: Yep, we could definitely.

utterances.speaker: Nantasha M. Williams
utterances.text: Council district one has 10 red light cameras and 10 speed cameras.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: Yes, council member, definitely we can share some type of breakdown.

utterances.speaker: Nantasha M. Williams
utterances.text: Can you share the breakdown by the end of the day, today?

utterances.speaker: Nantasha M. Williams
utterances.text: Like, I know most people, I'm like, we're kind of like up to our wits end with this kind of thing.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: Understood, yeah.

utterances.speaker: Nantasha M. Williams
utterances.text: Okay, and largely why I'm asking that question, and I'll get into like questions right now, is because many members feel like the cameras are disproportionately located in certain communities.

utterances.speaker: Nantasha M. Williams
utterances.text: We have heard that that is not the case, but because we have no anecdotal, well, it's anecdotal, we have no actual data that can actually say that, it's kind of like hearsay at this point.

utterances.speaker: Nantasha M. Williams
utterances.text: And so we would love to know where the cameras are across New York City.

utterances.speaker: Nantasha M. Williams
utterances.text: To be able to say whether or not they're equitably distributed across New York City.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: Understood, 100%.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: So I think like just given, I don't know, I'm not sure exactly what time it is, on a Friday, we can aim to try to get you something today.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: Yeah, I'm just adding pressure, you know.

utterances.speaker: Nantasha M. Williams
utterances.text: I feel like you guys need deadlines.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: Yeah.

chapters.summary: Williams questions why DOT's racial equity plan includes reviewing fine and fee-based programs if previous assessments found no disparities, and asks about transportation equity goals for underserved communities by 2030.
chapters.title: Williams on racial equity plan and previous disparity assessments

utterances.speaker: Nantasha M. Williams
utterances.text: Okay, so one short-term racial equity goal was actually to review critical fine and fee-based programs by 2028 for potential racial and ethnic disparities.

utterances.speaker: Nantasha M. Williams
utterances.text: So,

utterances.speaker: Nantasha M. Williams
utterances.text: Why was this selected as DOT's only short-term goal, and were any other short-term goals considered?

utterances.speaker: Nantasha M. Williams
utterances.text: Because this, as you see the theme, right?

utterances.speaker: Nantasha M. Williams
utterances.text: Like if you have these fee-based programs, red lights, speed cameras, and then you yourself and the racial equity plan said that you're going to review these programs, right?

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgione
utterances.text: Right.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgione
utterances.text: You want to understand.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgione
utterances.text: You want some tangible information that supports our statement that they're not predominantly in one neighborhood.

utterances.speaker: Nantasha M. Williams
utterances.text: Yeah, and then in your own racial equity plan that you submitted, you all said that you were going to review.

utterances.speaker: Nantasha M. Williams
utterances.text: The fine and fee-based programs.

utterances.speaker: Nantasha M. Williams
utterances.text: And what do you mean by that?

utterances.speaker: Nantasha M. Williams
utterances.text: Do you mean just the automated enforcement programs or other things maybe like outdoor dining?

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: I mean, we don't have a whole lot of fine and fee-based programs, to be honest, and those that we do have, a lot of times the fees are stipulated in the legislation,

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: or the fees are the same across the board, irrespective of...

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: Of the neighborhood or they may be graduated more based on where there's more demand, things like outdoor dining.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: So yeah, I mean, I was saying we don't have a lot of fine and fee-based programs.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: Many of them, those costs are already stipulated.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: So there's not a lot where we're really, we have discretionary

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: costs, but I'm

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: I'm not sure if that's answering your question.

utterances.speaker: Nantasha M. Williams
utterances.text: I mean, we just wanted to know what critical finance fee-based programs were, because that's what you all put in the racial equity plan.

utterances.speaker: Nantasha M. Williams
utterances.text: Okay, I'll just ask one more question and come back for a second round.

utterances.speaker: Nantasha M. Williams
utterances.text: My understanding is that DOT has previously found no racial or ethnic disparities with respect to its automated enforcement programs, as well as many other DOT programs.

utterances.speaker: Nantasha M. Williams
utterances.text: Were those previous assessments insufficient or inaccurate?

utterances.speaker: Nantasha M. Williams
utterances.text: Because I don't, it doesn't seem like anybody on this dais has any idea of the racial equity plan that you put forward.

utterances.speaker: Nantasha M. Williams
utterances.text: So you all said that you wanted to review the fine and fee-based programs, right, as a means of determining whether or not there are any disparities based off of race and ethnicity in New York City.

utterances.speaker: Nantasha M. Williams
utterances.text: But previously, DOT has said that you didn't find anything.

utterances.speaker: Nantasha M. Williams
utterances.text: And so the question is, is it that what you might have assessed in the past was inaccurate, which is why you are now suggesting that this should be something to review?

utterances.speaker: Nantasha M. Williams
utterances.text: Just want to understand again and also based off of like both community and council members who have said repeatedly that they feel like the cameras specifically are located in primarily communities of color.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgione
utterances.text: So council member for every every program, I think we want to continually be checking year after year that nothing is going astray.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgione
utterances.text: And we're not enforcing or providing anything in a way that's hurting one community over another.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgione
utterances.text: So if there seems like there's a contradiction there, it's just that we want to keep checking year after year.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: Yeah, my understanding is that the previous assessments did not find disparities, but these programs are always changing and dynamic, so we do assess them on a regular basis.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: Absolutely.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, round two.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: If we could actually switch the clock to two minutes, because I know we're pressed up for time.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: But for round two, we have Council Member 1, Wong, and then Epstein.

chapters.summary: Restler asks about funding timelines for the LIC waterfront esplanade committed under the rezoning, and whether capital plan changes will delay the 2031 completion. Beaton confirms no funding delays and all commitments are moving forward.
chapters.title: Restler on LIC waterfront esplanade and 1LIC commitments

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Thank you so much.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: So to pivot, I want to go back to with 1LIC.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Through this rezoning, the city committed to creating a new waterfront esplanade and open space under the Queensborough Bridge by 2031, including $79 million in capital funding.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: On page 303 of the capital plan, all that funding has a planned commitment date of 2031, project ID SEED HWQ7.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Can you clarify the start date for the funding for this project?

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: And will this funding present any delays to the city's commitment to complete the new open space by 2031?

utterances.speaker: Eric Beaton
utterances.text: Thank you for the question, Councilmember.

utterances.speaker: Eric Beaton
utterances.text: First of all, I want to say that any movement in the capital plan does not signify any delay, and we're moving steadfast on the commitments made in the rezoning.

utterances.speaker: Eric Beaton
utterances.text: Given capital stretches, we've had to move some funding around, but we're working with EDC and other partners to make sure that it's moving as quickly as we promised in the points of agreement.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: So is that a yes or a no?

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Is there any funding delays for the city's commitment?

utterances.speaker: Eric Beaton
utterances.text: There are no funding delays.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: There are no funding delays to open this park by 2031.

utterances.speaker: Eric Beaton
utterances.text: There are no funding delays.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Okay, so we want to see this open by 2031.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: As part of 1LIC, the city made the following commitments to street safety and public realm improvement projects.

chapters.summary: Restler asks about funding for lighting studies, public toilets, and pedestrian improvements committed under 1LIC. Beaton confirms all projects are fully funded and moving forward as promised in the points of agreement.
chapters.title: Restler on 1LIC street safety and public realm project funding

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: One of them was $1.25 million for a comprehensive lighting study in Law and City IBZ to be completed by 2026, $700,000 for an automated public toilet at Court Square to be completed by 2026.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: by 2027, $250,000 for a study of possible public ground improvements under the Queensborough Bridge ramps to be completed by 2027.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Can you clarify whether the funding is committed to these projects in the executive budget?

utterances.speaker: Eric Beaton
utterances.text: We're fully funded for all the commitments made in the points of agreement of the rezoning, and they're all moving forward, as promised.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Do you know what page we can find these projects?

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Because we've been reading every single page, and we could not find it.

utterances.speaker: Eric Beaton
utterances.text: I think you might be looking in the capital plan, but they're folded into some of our expense.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Eric Beaton
utterances.text: So that might be the issue.

utterances.speaker: Eric Beaton
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Can I just ask one more question?

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: On the timeline of these projects funded,

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: So we're seeing that you are saying on record that the timelines of the commitment will be on the POA.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: The city also committed $30 million in pedestrian and cycling infrastructure improvements along the 44th Drive corridor and $13 million to create an intermodal hub at 21st Street Station with designs both projects beginning in

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: The capital funds of these projects beginning in 2029 of June to June 2032.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Can you clarify the start date for the capital funding of the 44th corridor improvements?

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: And will this funding timeline present any delays to the city's committed timeline to begin design 28?

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: And do you expect any need for additional funding to complete these projects?

utterances.speaker: Eric Beaton
utterances.text: Thank you for the question, Councilmember.

utterances.speaker: Eric Beaton
utterances.text: So we're actively scoping the projects, which means that we're getting the package ready to transmit to DD.

utterances.speaker: Eric Beaton
utterances.text: for them to start design.

utterances.speaker: Eric Beaton
utterances.text: Let me...

utterances.speaker: Eric Beaton
utterances.text: So to your last question about other funding needs, no.

utterances.speaker: Eric Beaton
utterances.text: We feel that what was promised in the POA will be the accurate funding need.

utterances.speaker: Eric Beaton
utterances.text: We're in preliminary scoping for 44th Drive right now, and then the Center of Vernon Boulevard project will follow after that.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Okay, thank you so much for those answers.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: We're going to follow up in writing with all these questions on the details, especially on the waterfront greenway and everything else.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: And we'll also follow up for written criteria on how you calculate and evaluate what intersections qualify for dealing.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Thank you so much to both the chairs.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgione
utterances.text: Councilmember.

chapters.summary: An unknown council member asks whether DOT proactively looks for potholes or relies on 311, and asks about the $326.1M Grand Street Bridge reconstruction timeline. Beaton confirms full funding with construction in FY2029.
chapters.title: CM on pothole detection and Grand Street Bridge reconstruction

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Thank you, Chairs.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Really quick answer, please.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: One-line answer.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Does DOT travel around looking for potholes, or does it rely entirely on 3-on-1 complaints?

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgione
utterances.text: We do look for potholes in the course of all of the rest of our work.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgione
utterances.text: They are called in by multiple operational staff.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Okay, thank you.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Next.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: I went to junior high school in Maspeth, and that was 1979.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: One of the projects was design a Grand Street bridge because the city had plans of replacing it.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Fast forward, we're now at 2026.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: I'm glad to see that in this budget that we have $326.1 million allocated for the reconstruction of the Grand Street Bridge over Newtown Creek.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Question, is this money already here?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Because I went to the session a couple days ago and they said it's federal funds.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And my question is, is the money already in place or you're still waiting for it?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And what's the timeline for the construction?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Eric Beaton
utterances.text: Thank you for the question, Councilmember.

utterances.speaker: Eric Beaton
utterances.text: So we are fully funded for the Grand Street Bridge, and it is projected to go into construction in 2029, fiscal year 2029.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Thank you, John.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: 79, that's when I was born.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, Councilmember Epstein, followed by Deputy Speaker.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: I thank you, Chair.

chapters.summary: An unknown council member asks if the Broadway redesign is fully funded and whether public bathrooms and water fountains will be included. Flynn confirms funding and says EDC will announce a public toilet vendor soon.
chapters.title: CM on Broadway capital plan and public bathrooms

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Is the capital plan for Broadway fully funded at this point, the redesign of Broadway?

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: Broadway and Manhattan.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Manhattan.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Sorry, my borough.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: My understanding is that the certain segments of it are funded and moving ahead as capital projects.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: Right.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: You mean when you say the full quarter, you mean?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: You mean the 34th down to Union Square, the redesign.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: Yes, that's fully funded.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: That's fully funded.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And is that included?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: I know you're going to do two new kiosks in there, and those are going to have public water, have publicly available water fountains too?

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: Yeah, that is the plan.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Yeah, and we talked about having a publicly available bathroom in that.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Is that in the new bathroom plan?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Do you anticipate having a publicly available restroom available?

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: I'm not sure.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: I'd have to check and get back to you.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Yeah, it's something, you know, obviously we talked, the mayor's talked a lot about, you know, especially with the Madison Park right there, 23rd, Flatiron area.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: We'd really like to see a publicly available bathroom in that location.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: I hope that DOTU can commit to doing that.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: Yeah, we understand this administration has been very committed to increasing access to public bathrooms, and so we'll just...

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: I have to check on that specific question.

utterances.speaker: Eric Beaton
utterances.text: And we're in active scoping, so this is the perfect time to have that conversation to make sure that the infrastructure is built for a public bathroom.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Yeah, I flagged it for your team.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: I want to make sure, since the Capitol is there, I want to make sure the Capitol is available to put that infrastructure in place, including the bathrooms and the water fountains and the bottle fillers and all the public amenities that we would need in that part of the city.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: And I'll mention on that topic, because I know it is front of mind for many of us on the public toilets.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: I believe the new program that the mayor announced, that EDC should be announcing a selected vendor very soon.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: So that program is going to be getting off the ground.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Great.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And that selective event will determine where in the city, what boroughs, what neighborhoods will have?

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: No, we'll be working DOT in some coordination with parks to figure out.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: To figure that out.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: With public input, right?

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: Well, yeah.

utterances.speaker: Mike Flynn
utterances.text: I think based on where we're hearing these kinds of, yeah, these types of needs.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: Thank you, Chair.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgione
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Forgione
utterances.text: Deputy Speaker?

chapters.summary: Williams asks whether the SPEED initiative will update travel demand data for newly rezoned neighborhoods like Jamaica. Officials confirm they're studying Jamaica separately as part of normal operations.
chapters.title: Williams on SPEED initiative for newly rezoned neighborhoods

utterances.speaker: Nantasha M. Williams
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Nantasha M. Williams
utterances.text: Just really quickly, the streamlined procedures to expedite equitable development speed.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: the update travel demand responses for newly upzoned neighborhoods like Jamaica or LIC.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Sorry, Councilmember, can you just clarify travel demand responses?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Yeah, so the speed effort, as I understand it, is to really look at communities that are being upzoned, travel demand, et cetera.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And so what I want to know is, will you update?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Any information for newly upzoned neighborhoods?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Because this is a new thing going forward for this upcoming fiscal year, but there are neighborhoods that were recently rezoned.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So just want to understand if this speed effort would apply to them.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So the speed effort specifically is about new rezonings and new developments.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: I know.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: But we're also still committed to what we've agreed to of doing a very comprehensive traffic study of Jamaica to make sure that we understand exactly how traffic patterns have changed.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: I just want to make sure because we just rezoned and then this effort is new.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So just want to make sure.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: That was the question.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Like, are you going to also retroactively look at newly rezoned areas?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Because we just rezoned it last year.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So, yes, we are studying that area.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: It's not part of the new funding, but it's part of the work that we do in our normal course of business.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Okay.

chapters.summary: Williams questions how DOT can achieve 100% infrastructure equity for underserved communities by 2030 when another goal doesn't expect meaningful community relationships until 2036, noting Southeast Queens infrastructure deficits.
chapters.title: Williams on transportation equity goals and underserved communities

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Back to the racial equity plan.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So the desired outcome of one of DOT's midterm goals was to achieve citywide equity in transportation infrastructure.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: by building and implementing a comprehensive plan to serve 100% of underserved communities by 2030.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: This is a lot of a goal, but it does seem very vague and unrealistic.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And I just think a lot about my community in Southeast Queens, where we have a lot of sidewalks and curbs that contribute to a wide range of things like illegal dumping, flooding, et cetera.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So how can DOT achieve this transportation equity goal in underserved neighborhoods by 2030 if according to another one of its

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: long-term goals, DOT does not expect to build meaningful relationships with those underserved communities until 2036?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Essentially, none of the indicators for this outcome relate to any new infrastructure investments that would be needed to achieve a citywide equity and transportation goal for infrastructure.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So we just want to understand why there's a lack of indicators that match this critical aspect of the outcome.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Thank you, Council Member.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: I mean, I do want to underscore that transportation equity is central to what we're doing.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: I think the agencies make great strides, but it's going to be even more of a priority in this administration.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: We've brought on some new senior leadership, and we've kind of reorganized some of our offices to really create one group that can think holistically around long-term planning, how we embed equity in everything we're doing.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: We take it really seriously.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: You know, one thing we've done is...

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: As part of the New York City Streets Plan, it established for the first time a citywide metric for how we measure transportation equity and how we identify underserved neighborhoods and invest in them.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And it's really translating into all of our programs where we're prioritizing our investments in those top tier equity priority neighborhoods.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So I want to underscore that.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: I'm not sure, you know, I might have to get back to you on specifically the metrics in the racial equity plan, and we're happy to do that.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Yes, I would love for you all to get back to me because we're doing a budget hearing now, and if in your goals you set a goal

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: for 100% of underserved communities by 2030 to have equity in transportation infrastructure, to me all of those things carry a price.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And so we want to make sure that as we continue towards adoption, that your agency's budget reflects the resources that can get you to your goal.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And I think based off of what we've received, it doesn't, and that there are indicators that don't really match what you've put forward.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So I hope that as you go towards finalizing your racial equity plan, that you make the necessary adjustments to actually achieve your goal versus just writing some things on a piece of paper because it may sound good.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Understood.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Thank you.

chapters.summary: Lee asks about the December 2025 deadline for creating off-street tractor-trailer parking, requesting locations, timeline, and cost. Officials report DOT found no suitable land but is setting up metered on-street truck parking in industrial zones.
chapters.title: Lee on tractor-trailer overnight parking mandate

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: I'm going to ask a question on behalf of Councilmember Brooks-Powers, but this is actually an issue I know that is very important to myself as well as the Deputy Speaker and a bunch of other Council members on the tractor-trailer overnight parking.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Because on September 28, 2023, the Council passed a bill that requires the city to create off-street parking for tractor-trailers because this has been a huge issue for us.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And it requires DOT, DCAS, DCP to coordinate on creating that off-street parking by December 31st, 2025, which has already passed.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So just in the interest of time, where have you identified those commercial parking, off-street commercial parking lots?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And also, can you provide an update on the timeline as well as how much it's going to cost to build municipal lots?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So where, how long, and the cost.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So, yes, thank you for the question.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: We've been very aware of the issues with overnight truck parking and the new federal rules that have really put pressure on that.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: As you mentioned, we have been working with a lot of our sister agencies around the requirements of that law.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So, for example, DOT looked at all of the land that we own, which includes public parking lots now, as well as other pieces of land that we own.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And we determined that we, DOT, don't have space that's appropriate for use in that context, but we are working with our other sister agencies, you know, as EDC and others lead that effort.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So at the same time, we've recognized that this is a big problem, and that's why we've been setting up on-the-street metered overnight truck parking in industrial districts where it belongs.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: That's why the council passed another bill really encouraging us to focus on those areas last year.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And so we have been studying IBZs and other appropriate areas around the city for where we can provide more of that overnight truck parking so that it's not showing up in places where it's not appropriate.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So while that particular study is beyond DOT, we are still as an agency very invested in doing a lot of work to provide that overnight truck parking in appropriate places.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: How about DCAS, DCP, other agencies, private parking lots?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: I know that have been interested in renting out their spaces.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Have those come?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Conversation has been going.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Because we're past the deadline, so I just wanted to get a sense of when.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Yeah, I think as long as the facilities work functionally, like obviously the trucks need a lot of space to be able to maneuver, and so that's a big part of the conversation.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: That's one of the limiting factors.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: But as Deputy Commissioner Beaton said, those are active conversations.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: We're committed to trying to support this goal.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: I know I also bring it up every time I meet with the Trucking Association of New York or others and just really underscore because we know this is a big issue for communities.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Yeah, if you can follow up with the timeline, that'd be great.

chapters.summary: Lee asks about coordination with NYPD on illegal truck parking enforcement. Officials describe conducting blitzes in complaint areas and working with the Trucking Association to provide information on legal parking.
chapters.title: Lee on NYPD coordination for illegal truck parking enforcement

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And then also my last question is just around if you coordinate with NYPD regarding the illegal truck parking enforcement.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Yes, we work with them very closely, and we've worked with them to do blitzes in particular in areas where we get these complaints,

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: and we try to work with them to go back over a period of time so that it's not just they got a ticket once and then it goes away, but we really try to focus it for a while.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And then we work with the Trucking Association and others to provide information on where it is legal because we do understand that there is a need, and we want to make sure that we're doing both the enforcement and the providing alternatives.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, great.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Thank you.

chapters.summary: An unknown council member asks about $453K for Fifth Avenue resurfacing and $6.4M for public realm programs. Officials explain resurfacing is ahead of capital project, and public realm funding is expanding open streets.
chapters.title: CM on Fifth Avenue resurfacing and public realm funding

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Last set of questions.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Oh, I think I see my former staff in the audience.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Milo, how you doing?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Nice to see you.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: I'm going to turn it to just a few questions.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Resurfacing Fifth Avenue.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: The executive plan includes additional city funds of $453,000 in fiscal 2026 only to resurface Fifth Avenue.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: What will this funding be used for?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Please be specific.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So ahead of the capital project that's happening on Fifth Avenue, we wanted to make sure that the service has a good riding service ahead of FIFA and other events.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So that is why there was specific funding added for expense resurfacing of Fifth Avenue.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: If it's just resurfacing, why isn't this capitally eligible?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So resurfacing by itself is capital eligible, Chair, but because the capital project is coming up, we didn't want to mess with the capital eligibility of the larger Fifth Avenue project.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: If we paved it with capital funds, it would basically preclude us from coming back to do the capital streetscape project for a certain amount of time, and we didn't want to preclude it.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Regarding public realm, $6.4 million in city funding was baseline starting in fiscal 27 for public realm programs.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Will this funding just be used to support open streets at its current level, or will it be used to expand the program?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Yes, so first we're very grateful that the funding was baseline, so we're not going year to year anymore and not knowing what's coming up.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So that's a really important investment to us to be able to plan.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And as the commissioner said earlier, we do see the program growing.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: It's bigger at this point this year than it was last year, and it's not constrained by the money.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: We've always been able to do whatever has been needed, even if we have to move some money around internally.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So we're committed to growing the program, and not just from a money perspective, but providing

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: programming where it's needed.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: We've been working a lot on process to make sure that partners get paid much faster.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: I think the money and the long-term planning enable us to be able to do much more in terms of working closely with those partners.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So fair to say it is expanding?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Yeah, we don't turn anyone away because of money.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: What other plaza programs will receive funding, if any?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So we are committed to expanding our plaza program, and we have an applicant-based process for that every year.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And as part of that, we have our plaza equity funds, or sorry, our public space equity funds, because it goes beyond plazas now.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And so we want to make sure that whoever has an idea for a plaza, we can work with them, whether to test it short-term as an open street, whether to eventually build it out capitally, to provide ongoing maintenance support.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: This is something we're very committed to as an agency.

chapters.summary: An unknown council member raises concerns about increasing streetlight repair times and asks about $1.5M for streetlight maintenance. Forgione explains the funding goes to existing contracts and the aging infrastructure requires capital investment.
chapters.title: CM on streetlight maintenance delays and Con Ed coordination

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And my last question is on streetlights.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: In fiscal 2025, it took DOT an average of 5.3 calendar days to repair streetlights, which is a 20.5% increase from 4.4 days in fiscal 24 and 76% slower than the three days in fiscal 21.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: It took Con Ed 22 calendar days on average to repair streetlights, which is 17.6% slower than the 18.7 days in fiscal 2024 and 45.7% slower than the 50.1 days in fiscal 21.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: The MMR indicates that DOT delays were due to weather-related outages and coordination issues with utilities during underground infrastructure.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: No explanation of the Con Ed performance decline was included.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: The executive plan has an additional $1.5 million in fiscal 26 only for the streetlight maintenance.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: What will this money go towards?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Is this money going towards already existing contracts for streetlight maintenance?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Chair, yes it is.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So this funding is going toward already existing contracts with the vendors, what it sounds like.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: I'm a little concerned about that.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: With this money, will this money be directly used to increase coordination times with Con Ed?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So we meet with Con Ed very often.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: In fact, I met with them this week on various issues.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: We are constantly talking with them.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: But what is happening with our street lighting system is that overall it's pretty aged.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And we have, whenever we have a Con Ed issue, oftentimes it has something to do with the underground conduit needing to be replaced.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And that is not just changing a bulb.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: It's more than a quick repair.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So you're raising a valid issue.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: It is something that we are working on both internally with our contractors and with Con Ed.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: I mean, between now and adoption, is there some way to add resources to focus in on increasing the coordination?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Because it's something that we hear, you know, time on end.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: This is a basic and essential function of the department.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: If this funding is not being used to coordinate with Con Ed or to speed up streetlight maintenance,

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: You know, I then have to ask, what is the DOT doing differently?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Because the current contract.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: and systems of operation in place doesn't seem to be working well.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Right, so the coordination is not something that we need funding for.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Again, it kind of comes back to the physical plant, the infrastructure itself, and we're also looking at

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: advancing some capital projects for larger areas in each borough where we know that the street lighting infrastructure is old and needs to be replaced in order to be reliable.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So that's kind of the area that we're looking toward in the future.

chapters.summary: An unknown council member asks about additional budget for street light infrastructure. Forgione confirms $331M for LED replacement with real-time monitoring sensors in every streetlight.
chapters.title: CM on additional street light infrastructure funding

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And my last question, is there additional monies in the budget for updated street light infrastructure?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Yes, there is.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: We received $331 million in order to replace every LED in the city and then also to provide sensors in every streetlight that will give us real-time information on the functioning of that streetlight.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Thank you very much, Commissioner.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, I think we are actually done for this portion with the Department of Transportation.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So thank you so much, Commissioner.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Just want to give a special shout out.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: You have an amazing first deputy commissioner, Margaret, who is very helpful to a lot of us and has been.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So thank you so much.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Just wanted to give you a shout out.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: But not that I don't like the rest of you guys, but you know what I mean.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Thank you so much, everyone.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Thank you, council members.

chapters.summary: Chair Lee concludes the DOT portion and transitions to the Department of Consumer and Worker Protection hearing, with a break for agency changeover.
chapters.title: Transition from DOT to DCWP hearing

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, and we're going to roll right into DCWP, which is next.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So we're just going to take a couple of minutes to transition.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Keep it on, please.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Quiet, please.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: We're about to start.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: We're about to resume.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Please have a seat.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: At this time, please have a seat, please.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Thank you very much, Vickie-Paladino Corporation.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Can I start?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Yeah.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Oh, we're good.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Hello.

chapters.summary: Lee welcomes attendees to the DCWP hearing, joined by Speaker Menin and Chair Epstein, and introduces Commissioner Sam Levine and his team.
chapters.title: Chair Lee opens DCWP FY27 Executive Budget Hearing

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Good afternoon, everyone, and welcome to the second FY27 Executive Budget Hearing for the day, focusing on Department of Consumer Worker Protection.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Councilmember Lee, I'm joined by our speaker, Speaker Menon, as well as our Councilmember Harvey Epstein, Chair of the Committee on Consumer and Worker Protection.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And I would like to recognize we've been joined by Deputy Speaker Williams, Councilmember Krishnan, Banks, Wong, and Purina Sanchez, and Councilmember Shulman.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So welcome to our commissioner, Sam Levine, and your team.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Thank you all for joining us today to answer our questions.

chapters.summary: Lee details DCWP's $78.4M FY27 budget, a 5% increase, with 77 new positions in FY27 growing to 181 by FY29. She notes the mayor's pledge to double DCWP's budget and the agency's 19.3% vacancy rate.
chapters.title: Lee on DCWP's $78.4M FY27 budget and headcount increases

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: On May 12, 2026, the administration released the executive financial plan for FY26 to FY30 with a proposed FY27 budget of $124.7 billion.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: The Department of Consumer and Worker Protections proposed FY27 budget of $78.4 million represents less than 1% of the administration's proposed FY27 budget in the executive plan.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And this is an increase of 3.7 million or 5% from the

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: The $74.7 million originally budgeted in the FY27 preliminary plan.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: This increase results from several actions, mostly from the significant and sustained increase of 77 positions at DCWP in FY27, increasing to 181 positions in 2029.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: As of April 26, DCWP's budget headcount was 85 more than their fiscal 26 budgeted headcount.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Further, the mayor announced plans to double the budget of DCWP in order to strengthen worker protections and enforcement.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: In addition, DCWP has 19.3% vacancy rate that makes it difficult to meet its operational demands.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And we were pleased to see that these additional funds were partially included in the plan.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: My questions will largely focus on the mayor's campaign promise where he pledged to double the agency's budget, savings from OFE's re-estimation of the financial literacy expansion, and the savings that we are seeing from discontinuing the cloud migration program.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And now at this time, I will turn it over to Speaker Menon for her remarks.

chapters.summary: Menin emphasizes her deep appreciation for DCWP from her time as commissioner and chair, warns that insufficient funding could hamper enforcement, and announces the Council is requesting an additional $32M for DCWP.
chapters.title: Speaker Menin on DCWP importance and enforcement concerns

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Thank you so much, Chair Lee, and I really want to thank our finance chair for her great work in holding these hearings.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: And I want to thank our Consumer and Worker Protection Chair, Councilmember Harvey Epstein, for his terrific work on chairing this committee.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: And I want to thank you, Commissioner, and your team for being here, and to all the advocates that have taken time to participate in this budget process.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: So this hearing in particular.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: is very meaningful to me as I had the honor of serving as commissioner of this agency and as chair of this committee.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: And I've garnered a very deep appreciation over the very many years for the dedicated public servants who carry out this work each and every day.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: But those experiences also reinforce the importance of rigorous oversight and responsible fiscal management.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Our responsibility today is to ensure that the fiscal year 2027 budget adequately equips the agency to meet its mission while delivering transparency, efficiency, and measurable outcomes for New Yorkers.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: The mayor pledged to double DCWP's budget during his campaign, recognizing that the agency needs greater funding to be successful.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: However, in the executive budget, the mayor pledged an additional $3.7 million to DCWP, bringing the agency's total 2027 funding to $78.4 million under his proposal.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: We in the Council have consistently warned that insufficient funding could hamper DCWP's ability to investigate complaints and enforce local laws.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: To ensure that the agency has the staffing and operational capacity needed to carry out responsibilities assigned through local laws, the Council is asking for an additional $32 million for DCWP.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: I understand certainly that behind every single line in the budget, there is a real impact.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: on our communities.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: As we review it, the Council will closely examine whether the proposed investments are sufficient to meet current and future needs, whether resources are being allocated equitably, and whether the agency has the staffing,

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: infrastructure, and operational support necessary to succeed.

chapters.summary: Menin highlights concerns about staffing for laws covering for-hire vehicle deactivation, security guard minimum wages, protected time off, and delivery worker laws, stressing that laws need resources for enforcement.
chapters.title: Menin on staffing for new council-initiated laws

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: In particular, I'm concerned about whether the department has sufficient staffing for its wide range of oversight and enforcement responsibilities,

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: particularly on newer items that stem from council-initiated laws covering for high vehicle driver deactivation, security guard minimum wages, protected time off, delivery worker laws, and other items.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: I just want to say the council has passed so many important bills in this regard, and so we really want to make sure that the agency then has the proper resources to enforce them, because we've done all this work

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: to pass these laws, but if we don't have the resources to enforce them, we're not really getting the results that we need to do.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
