utterances.text: So we'll also be looking carefully at how the administration plans to address ongoing challenges, improve service delivery, and ensure that taxpayer dollars are being spent effectively and responsibly.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: New Yorkers deserve a government that is responsive, accountable, and capable of meeting the moment.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Today's hearing is an important opportunity to assess where progress is being made, where gaps remain, and where additional actions may be necessary in the future.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: I look forward to a productive discussion and working collaboratively to ensure that this agency

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: has all the tools and support needed to serve the people of the city.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: And I'm now going to turn it over to Chair Epstein for his remarks.

chapters.summary: Epstein thanks leadership and the commissioner, emphasizing the need to ensure DCWP is fully funded to protect workers and consumers, and thanking his committee and finance staff.
chapters.title: Chair Epstein opening remarks on consumer and worker protection

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: I want to thank you, Chair Lee and Speaker Menon, for your leadership on these issues.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: I know, especially Speaker Menon, your familiarity with these issues, both as a commissioner and the chair of this committee, is really invaluable.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: I want to thank the Commissioner for DCWP for joining us today to talk about the Consumer Worker Protection Budget for this upcoming fiscal year.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: You know, as we have heard already, and the questions we're going to be talking about is around the additional positions that you're hiring, the needs that the agency will have to ensure that it's fully funded to make sure that the workers and the consumers in New York get the support that they need.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: We really want to hear about the increased numbers in active workers for consumer protection laws that the speaker mentioned, and if the proposed budget will ensure that they have enough staffing in place to make sure we follow those needs.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: You know, my personal history as well as the speaker, we're really uniquely able to understand these concerns and see this history for New Yorkers.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: You know, I do want to thank you, Commissioner Levine, for being here, your Deputy Commissioner, Elizabeth Wogan, Chief of Staff, Carlos Ortiz, and General Counsel, Mike Tiger, for being here today.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: We really look forward to this active conversation where over the next few months, a few weeks, what our goals are together to ensure that the committee

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: and the staff.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: and the speaker as the council, make sure we have enough resources to ensure that you are getting done what you need for New Yorkers.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: I want to thank my team, the legislative division, Cyrus Wayne, Natalie Meltzer, my staff, Jackson Fisher Ward, Jen Amian, and our finance division, Glenn Maritoli, Daniel Krupp, and Chima,

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: who really put this together to make sure that we have a successful hearing.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: And I'll turn it back over to the chair, Lee.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Great.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: I also want to recognize we have been joined by Councilmember Murano on Zoom, as well as Justin Sanchez.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So before we get started, really quickly, just wanted to announce some logistics.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So as you

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: We all know if you've heard me speak at other hearings, because of the crunch in timeline for our executive budget hearings, we're going to do public testimony all on one day, Wednesday, June 10th, starting at 9.30 a.m.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So please join us that day.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: We're going to try to make it as fun as we can.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So executive budget hearings for the public testimony portions, Wednesday, June 10th, starting at 9.30 a.m.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So please make sure to let folks know.

chapters.summary: Committee counsel swears in Commissioner Levine, Deputy Commissioner Wagoner, Chief of Staff Ortiz, and General Counsel Tiger.
chapters.title: Swearing in of DCWP witnesses

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And with that, I'll turn over to committee counsel to swear in the witnesses.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Good afternoon.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Do you affirm to tell the truth, the whole truth, and nothing but the truth before this committee and to respond honestly to council member questions?

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Commissioner Levine.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: I do.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Deputy Commissioner Wagner.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: I do.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Chief of Staff Fortese.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: I do.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: And General Counsel Tiger.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: I do.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: You may begin.

chapters.summary: Levine describes DCWP as the nation's leading municipal enforcement agency, highlighting consumer protection law, protected time-off, fair work week, delivery worker laws, and licensing of 45,000 businesses.
chapters.title: Commissioner Levine on DCWP mission and consumer protections

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: Well, thank you, Speaker Menon, Chair Lee, Chair Epstein, and members of the Committee on Consumer and Worker Protection.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: I'm Sam Levine, Commissioner of the Department of Consumer and Worker Protection.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: Today, I'm joined by our General Counsel, Michael Tiger, our Chief of Staff, Carlos Ortiz, and our Deputy Commissioner of the Office of Labor Policy and Standards, Elizabeth Wagoner.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: Thank you for the opportunity to be here today before this committee to testify on the work of our agency and our executive budget for fiscal year 2027.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: DCWP is the nation's leading municipal enforcement agency charged with delivering economic justice.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: We leverage our authority to bring New Yorkers real economic relief and protect them from predatory, deceptive, and unconscionable practices that violate their rights as consumers and as workers.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: This includes pioneering cutting-edge protections such as the city's consumer protection law, protected time-off law, fair work week law, and delivery worker laws, including the minimum pay rate for delivery workers.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: Through licensing more than 45,000 businesses in over 45 industries, DCWP ensures fair competition and a level playing field for responsible small businesses

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: that are integral to New York City's vibrant communities.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: DCWP also provides essential services such as free tax preparation for individuals and families,

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: as well as tax prep services for self-employed workers and small businesses, and one-on-one financial counseling to ensure New Yorkers keep more of what they earn and can plan for their futures.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: Though it's been just sort of six months under my tenure, it's been a privilege to serve as the commissioner of DCWP, an agency that champions millions of consumers, workers, and families across the city day after day.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: Today I'll provide the committee with an overview of the executive budget for FY27, the work of our agency, and our path forward in continuing to build a fairer and more affordable city for all New Yorkers under Mayor Mamdani's administration.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: Starting with our budget.

chapters.summary: Levine details the FY27 executive budget of $78.5M with 409 active and 561 authorized headcount, the highest ever, receiving 181 new lines and $17.9M in personnel services by FY29, calling it the greatest investment in DCWP history.
chapters.title: Levine on FY27 budget with 181 new positions and $17.9M

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: For FY27, DCWP's executive budget is approximately $78.5 million, with an active headcount of 409 and an authorized headcount of 561, the highest authorized headcount our agency has ever had.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: This is due in no small part to the advocacy of our partners, this council, and of course the immense support and recognition of our agency's work by our mayor.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: Mayor Mamdani's executive budget represents the single greatest investment in consumer and worker rights in DCWP history.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: All told, DCWP will be receiving a total of 181 new lines and $17.9 million in personnel services by FY29.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: The breakdown is as follows.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: In FY27, DCWP will receive 77 lines and $4.3 million in personnel services.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: In FY28, we will receive 76 lines and $7.3 million in personnel services.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: And in FY29, we will receive 28 lines and the remaining $6.3 million in personnel services.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: The new lines we will be receiving in FY27 will be strategically deployed to immediately begin implementation of major new laws and much-needed protections for New Yorkers.

chapters.summary: Levine highlights expanded delivery worker laws, minimum pay rate for grocery delivery, expanded protected time off, upcoming deactivation protections, street vendor licenses, and Chair Epstein's student loan debt legislation.
chapters.title: Levine on new laws including delivery worker and deactivation protections

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: Going on to the important work we do.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: It has been just shy of six months since the start of my tenure, and I am beyond proud of what this agency has already accomplished under the Mamdani administration.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: Our team has been at the forefront of the administration implementing cutting-edge new mandates, aggressively pursuing new protections, and securing victory after victory for the people of New York.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: These range from our expanded delivery worker laws, including a minimum pay rate for grocery delivery workers, an expanded protected time off law, and soon to come, deactivation protections for key workforces and more licenses for our city street vendors.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: I also want to be sure to emphasize Chair Epstein's much-needed legislation to address the student loan debt crisis in our city.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: Under Local Law 84, we will be expanding our critical financial empowerment center network by providing student loan debt services, which will be essential for New Yorkers.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: In addition to the new laws and requirements, DCWP is

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: has also taken more proactive and innovative steps to strengthen protections for those who make our city run.

chapters.summary: Levine describes the strongest hotel junk fee prohibition in the US saving $35M annually, a click-to-cancel rule estimated to save $162.5M, and the SHIELD debt collection rule providing the nation's strongest protections.
chapters.title: Levine on hotel junk fees, click-to-cancel, and SHIELD rule

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: Earlier this year, tasked with tackling deceptive and predatory practices under the mayor's executive orders, DCWP adopted the strongest prohibition on hotel junk fees in the United States.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: This rule protects both New Yorkers traveling outside the city and those visiting our city from unexpected and costly fees, with projected savings of up to $35 million a year for New Yorkers and up to $31 million a year for visitors.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: We also propose rules to ensure that consumers can easily cancel automatic renewals and subscriptions.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: Our clit-to-cancel rule is estimated to save New Yorkers up to $162.5 million a year, according to an analysis by the Roosevelt Institute.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: Lastly, we finalized the SHIELD rule, a debt collection rule that provides New Yorkers with the nation's strongest protections against debt collector harassment.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: In addition to expanded protections under our rules, we also released a benchmarking report on a data-driven enforcement strategy for New York City's protected time-off law, leading and guiding the nation in education and compliance on a cornerstone workplace right.

chapters.summary: Levine describes launching a compliance blitz across industries, exposing Uber and DoorDash for deceptive design tricks that reduced worker pay by over $500M, and seeking millions in penalties from predatory businesses.
chapters.title: Levine on compliance blitz and enforcement against Uber and DoorDash

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: This work to further New Yorkers'rights will only continue as we proactively leverage our authority to pursue an ambitious agenda delivering on the Mayor's vision.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: While we have been working to implement and expand protections afforded to New Yorkers, we have also been fighting harder than ever before.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: to vigorously enforce our laws, cracking down on predatory corporate practices to hold bad actors accountable.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: We started 2026 by launching a robust compliance blitz, sending notices to various industries, reminding them of their obligations under the law and the repercussions that arise from noncompliance.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: This past January, we exposed Uber and DoorDash for deceptive design tricks that made it harder for consumers to tip delivery workers, driving down worker pay by more than half a billion dollars in New York City alone.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: We have shown major corporations that there are consequences for violating our laws, and we are actively seeking millions of penalties and restitution from numerous predatory businesses for exploiting and cheating New Yorkers.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: These efforts span across our work, delivering real results for consumers, workers, and small businesses alike.

chapters.summary: Levine lists enforcement victories including $5M from delivery apps for minimum pay violations, $1.5M from Dunkin' and Taco Bell for Fair Workweek violations, $875K from Hungry Panda for fee cap violations, and nearly $10M secured in the mayor's first 100 days.
chapters.title: Levine on enforcement results: $5M from delivery apps, $1.5M from Dunkin'

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: For example, we secured $5 million from three delivery apps for violations of the minimum pay rate, and more than half a million dollars for freelancers ripped off by the production company Splashlight.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: In March, we announced a $1.5 million settlement with Dunkin'and Taco Bell franchisees for violations of the Fair Workweek law, securing economic justice and fairness for more than 760 workers.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: We took legal action against predatory businesses like Extraspace, Radiant Solar, and Instant Recovery Corporation, seeking over tens of millions of dollars in penalties for deceptive and illegal schemes to raise costs for New Yorkers.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: We also went after May.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: major employment agencies, including CMP, Golden Rose, and Eleni's, and won almost $1 million in restitution for individuals who faced exploitation and a violation of their rights by these businesses.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: In April, we announced a settlement of more than $875,000 with Hungry Panda for violating New York City's fee cap law

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: and charging small immigrant-owned restaurants thousands of dollars in illegal junk fees, the first action our agency has ever brought under this authority.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: And most recently, the mayor announced her action against Honda of the Bronx, a used car dealer engaging in widespread deceptive practices.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: All of this work is a clear reminder that under this administration, there is zero tolerance for companies that violate the law, and that we will fight to bring justice to the New Yorkers that faced abuses.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: The numbers speak for themselves.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: Within the mayor's first 100 days, we had already secured nearly $10 million for New Yorkers, and that number is only growing as we continue delivering real economic justice to consumers, workers, and small businesses across our city.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: This is just the beginning of a transformative era, and we look forward to continuing our relentless fight to bring accountability, affordability, and economic justice to the people of New York.

chapters.summary: Levine calls this the beginning of a transformative era, thanking Mayor Mamdani, the Speaker, and committee chairs for their advocacy, and pledging to continue delivering economic justice to New Yorkers.
chapters.title: Levine concludes testimony on transformative era for DCWP

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: I thank you for the opportunity to testify today and DCWP's commitment to championing consumers, workers, and families in this new era of economic justice for all New Yorkers.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: I would again like to express my gratitude to Mayor Mumdani, to Chair Epstein, to the Speaker,

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: to Chair Lee, to members of this committee, and to all of our partners in this room for their steadfast advocacy on our agency's behalf.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: It really means the world.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: We are eager to continue our success delivering millions in real economic relief to countless New Yorkers and advancing Mayor Mamdani's agenda for a fairer, more affordable New York City.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: I look forward to answering your questions.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Okay, great.

chapters.summary: Menin challenges the claim of biggest investment, noting last year's budget was higher. Levine explains the prior year included a $12M summer contract for city residents program being eliminated, with money redirected to enforcement.
chapters.title: Speaker Menin questions headcount comparison with prior year

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Thank you very much.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So we appreciate the testimony today.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: I want to get a little bit more into the headcount because I know you said this is the biggest investment that's been made into DCWP.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: I think our finance team...

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Had some concerns about that assertion because it's our understanding that last year, in terms of where the budget was, both with headcount and overall amount, that it actually was higher last year.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So we sort of want to get into those numbers.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: I'm sure the chair will get into that as well.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Do you have any comments on that?

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: Sure, and I'm happy to explain that, Madam Speaker.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: Last year's budget was, you might say, inflated by the inclusion of the summer contract for city residents.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: That was budgeted at $12 million for FY26.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: Under the current plan, we are eliminating the program for city residents, effective in FY27.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: So that's going to reduce $12 million.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: $14 million in FY27, $16 million in FY28, and we're plowing that money into our enforcement work.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: So if you look at our actual budget for enforcement, it's growing significantly.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Okay, I guess what we want to get clarity on is you mentioned in your opening statement that the highest authorized headcount of 561, but your active headcount

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: is 409, which is lower than 417 this past July.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So we want to understand that.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: Well, thank you, Madam Speaker.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: We do have vacancies.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: We have a vacancy rate of 16% right now.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: So we have a lot of hiring to do in FY27.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: We have vacancies to fill.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: We have 77 new lines to hire.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: And I can tell you, if you go onto our website right now, we have jobs posted.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: We're already actively engaging in hiring.

utterances.speaker: Carlos Ortiz
utterances.text: And I would note, too, Speaker, that our authorized headcount for fiscal year 26 was 483.

utterances.speaker: Carlos Ortiz
utterances.text: But obviously with the mayor's investment for this fiscal year and moving into fiscal year 29, we're looking at an overall 40% increase in the headcount of the agency.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And how quickly do you think you're going to be hiring?

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: We think we're going to be on pace to hire.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: I don't have month-by-month, Speaker, but we are going to be on pace to both close our current vacancies and hire the 77 through FY27.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: We're already moving.

chapters.summary: Menin asks if the Council's proposed additional $32M would be sufficient. Levine says the executive budget's 181 new positions will meet current needs but welcomes more resources for potential new laws.
chapters.title: Menin on Council's $32M additional funding request for DCWP

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So the council, as you know, is calling on the administration to allocate an additional $32 million for DCWP's budget to meet the need of the numerous pieces of critical legislation on consumer and worker protection.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: that the council has passed in recent years.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Do you feel that this additional funding would be sufficient to appropriately carry out the agency's mission?

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: Well, I haven't had a chance to look at the details of the council's proposal.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: I learned about it today.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: I greatly appreciate the vote of confidence that you have placed in the agency.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: I know you have particular affection for DCWP, as do I, and the vote of confidence by the committee.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: With respect to the executive budget, which I have looked at, we feel confident that with respect to the laws that the council passed, the new mandates, as you referenced, Madam Speaker, for our agency, that the 181

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: headcount that the executive budget authorized will be sufficient to meet our needs.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: DCWP issue, well, let me just, before I move on from that, so you're saying that you do not need any additional funding beyond what is in the executive budget?

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: We always welcome more resources generally, and I know that the council is considering new laws, including with new responsibilities for DCWP, and I expect we'll have follow-up conversations about new needs there.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: But with respect to the bills the council's passed and that are already being implemented, we worked really closely with OMB and City Hall and we feel we have what we need.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Okay.

chapters.summary: Menin asks about compliance rates after DCWP issued warnings to 56,000 employers. Levine and Wagoner explain the goal was education, not investigating all businesses, and that there's no excuse for non-compliance.
chapters.title: Menin on earned safe and sick leave compliance warnings

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Earned safe and sick leave time.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So I want to talk a little bit about that.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So DCWP issued compliance warnings to roughly 56,000 New York City employers following the implementation.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: of the earn safe and sick time amendments.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: What percentage of those employers have achieved compliance and what enforcement actions have been taken against those that have not?

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: Well, I appreciate the question.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: We take a lot of pride in that warning blitz.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: I'm going to turn things over to my colleague to share a little more.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: But the goal of that was not to open investigations of all of those thousands of businesses.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: It is twofold.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: One, we wanted to make sure businesses understood their obligation.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: I don't believe in gotcha enforcement.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: I believe in making sure businesses understand their obligations, workers understand their rights.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: That was the goal there.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: But also we wanted to make sure businesses knew that we were watching, that given the high volume of complaints over the years we've gotten about PTO, I wanted businesses to know that we took violations really seriously.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: That's why we followed up with warnings.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: That's why we followed up with enforcement.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: With respect to the number of in compliance, I don't think we've investigated every one of those businesses, but I'll turn things over to my deputy commissioner to share more.

utterances.speaker: Elizabeth Wagoner
utterances.text: No, that's echoing everything the commissioner said.

utterances.speaker: Elizabeth Wagoner
utterances.text: And to generate that list, you know, we're looking at all of the restaurants in the city, which obviously is information that DOHMH maintains.

utterances.speaker: Elizabeth Wagoner
utterances.text: And, you know, this historically can be a high violation industry.

utterances.speaker: Elizabeth Wagoner
utterances.text: And all of our past targets going back years and years and years, every company we've ever investigated in the past also got a letter like that.

utterances.speaker: Elizabeth Wagoner
utterances.text: And, you know, as we hear from workers, you know, as the year goes on, we'll be able to look and see.

utterances.speaker: Elizabeth Wagoner
utterances.text: see, you know, did this company get a notice of the new laws?

utterances.speaker: Elizabeth Wagoner
utterances.text: There's absolutely no excuse not to be in compliance with the law.

chapters.summary: Menin asks about outreach to small businesses. Levine and Ortiz describe webinars, DCWP 101 events, VIP inspections, and direct outreach reaching thousands of businesses annually.
chapters.title: Menin on small business outreach for new requirements

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And what type of outreach is the agency doing, particularly for small businesses that might not be aware of the new requirements?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: How do we make sure that they are aware?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: What kind of outreach is happening in that regard?

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: This is a key priority for me.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: I spoke to the National Supermarket Association yesterday about this.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: We've held online seminars with small businesses.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: We're also educating workers.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: But business education is key.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: We have a deputy commissioner for still affairs here who can add more on these efforts.

utterances.speaker: Carlos Ortiz
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Carlos Ortiz
utterances.text: Madam Speaker, I think education is a core component of, I think, how DCP wants to approach our work.

utterances.speaker: Carlos Ortiz
utterances.text: As Commissioner mentioned, we don't play gotcha with our laws.

utterances.speaker: Carlos Ortiz
utterances.text: We want to make sure we're engaging with folks in an honest discussion.

utterances.speaker: Carlos Ortiz
utterances.text: Besides these webinars and what we call DCP 101 events with business associations and representatives, we also engage in direct outreach to businesses,

utterances.speaker: Carlos Ortiz
utterances.text: both through our community affairs team, but also our enforcement team that offers what we call VIP inspections where they offer any new business a free educational inspection to help them understand what their obligations are.

utterances.speaker: Carlos Ortiz
utterances.text: We reach thousands of businesses through these efforts across the year, the calendar year.

utterances.speaker: Carlos Ortiz
utterances.text: It's something we're looking to build upon.

chapters.summary: Menin questions why only 50 summons were filed from 1,400 FAIR Act complaints. Levine reports updated numbers of 73 summons and $27,000 in penalties, explaining some complaints don't involve actual broker fee charges.
chapters.title: Menin on FAIR Act enforcement and low summons rate

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And how many staff do you have dedicated to enforcement around this topic?

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: I'm going to kick that to our deputy commissioner.

utterances.speaker: Elizabeth Wagoner
utterances.text: So we actually have...

utterances.speaker: Elizabeth Wagoner
utterances.text: our investigators, attorneys, and data scientists in the Office of Labor Policy and Standards working on all of our laws.

utterances.speaker: Elizabeth Wagoner
utterances.text: So our, not including the new lines we've just received, we have 54 people in OLPS who work on all of our laws.

utterances.speaker: Elizabeth Wagoner
utterances.text: The biggest area of work for us is our threefold paid safe and sick leave, which we're now calling the protected time off law, the fair work week law, and the delivery worker laws.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Moving to a new topic, the FAIR Act and housing affordability.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So since the FAIR Act took effect on June 11, 2025, DCWP has received over 1,400 complaints related to illegal broker fees,

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: but it appears only 50 summons to real estate brokers, landlords, or property managers have been filed, which equates to roughly 3.5%.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Of total complaints.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Can you explain why that is the case?

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: I'm happy to, Madam Speaker.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: I'll say briefly that since the FAIR Act was enacted, we were now up to 73 summons, 27,000 in penalties awarded by oath, 15,000 awarded in restitution.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: But there's still a gap, as you recognize.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: The basis for that is twofold, and our general counsel can add more to this.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: One, sometimes we will get, often we will get complaints from folks who were not actually charged a broker fee, but maybe they're

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: friend told them they were charged a broker fee or they were tempted to charge a broker fee, we will follow up.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: We will not always be able to follow up and actually reach that broker.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: So part of it is just general awareness of the FAIR Act, and we're very proud of our outreach campaign on that.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: Part of that also is just the oath process.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: A lot of these cases are moving through oath, and you are seeing the number of recoveries for consumers steadily rising.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: Mike, do you want to add to that?

utterances.speaker: Michael Tiger
utterances.text: No, that's absolutely right.

utterances.speaker: Michael Tiger
utterances.text: We took, when this law came on board, we took it very seriously and prioritized it.

utterances.speaker: Michael Tiger
utterances.text: And I think, just to note, this new investment that the commissioner was talking about by the Mamdani administration is definitely something we are going to, the FAIR Act and other rental issues,

utterances.speaker: Michael Tiger
utterances.text: the issues that arose during the mayor's rental ripoff hearings are definitely a topic that we are going to use when we get these new lines on board to redouble our efforts.

utterances.speaker: Michael Tiger
utterances.text: Especially for the fair.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Okay.

chapters.summary: Menin raises concerns about dropping financial counseling success rates from 21.1% to 17.3% and 6% fewer clients served. Levine attributes some decline to shortened measurement timeframe and emphasizes wanting more New Yorkers to use services.
chapters.title: Menin on declining financial counseling outcomes

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: I want to switch gears to financial counseling, and then I'm going to pass it over to Chair Lee.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So the percentage of DCWP financial counseling clients achieving a financial goal within their first year dropped from 21.1% in fiscal year 25 to 17.3%

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: in fiscal year 26, which is the lowest rate in at least three years.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And well below the agency's directional target to improve this figure.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: The number of clients served also fell by 6%.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Given the administration's stated affordability agenda, what does the fiscal 2020

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: 27 executive budget include specifically for this program and what is driving the deteriorating outcome rates?

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: Yeah, well, let me start by saying financial empowerment centers serve thousands of New Yorkers every year.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: You know, with respect to the outcomes on the MMR, the time frame was shortened to a year.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: Many of our clients are looking for longer-term financial help where we just don't have data.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: So not all of the benefits that our clients in the financial empowerment centers realize are...

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: Come through in that data because it's a longer term estimate.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: As a general matter though, Speaker, I completely agree.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: We want to get more New Yorkers using our financial empowerment centers.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: We have capacity, we have wonderful partners all over New York City.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: We want people getting in the door.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: Many of our staff have used these counselors, gotten outstanding results.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: We want more New Yorkers to benefit.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: In terms of the budget, I don't know if you have that handy for financial empowerment.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: We are targeting an increase.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: It's going to be FY26.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: It's $4.8 million.

utterances.speaker: Carlos Ortiz
utterances.text: I would also, I appreciate the Commissioner's point about our staff using our services as well.

utterances.speaker: Carlos Ortiz
utterances.text: We're very proud of the financial empowerment centers.

utterances.speaker: Carlos Ortiz
utterances.text: We've even had two commissioners that have had their student loan debt forgiven through our financial empowerment centers.

utterances.speaker: Carlos Ortiz
utterances.text: And since the exemption, we've increased our savings for New Yorkers by $15.6 million, and we've helped reduce debt by $135 million.

utterances.speaker: Carlos Ortiz
utterances.text: So it's a fundamental program for the agency.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: All right.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: I'm going to turn it over to back to Chair Lee.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Actually, perfect segue.

chapters.summary: Lee asks about $2M savings from delayed financial literacy expansion. Levine explains the delay was due to background checks for vendors taking longer than expected, with the same end goal of reaching every school district by FY29.
chapters.title: Lee on financial literacy expansion savings of $2M

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So the budget reflects savings of $2 million in fiscal 26 from a re-estimation of OFE's financial literacy expansion.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So can you clarify which plan programs or expansions are being delayed and what the revised realistic timeline looks like?

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: Yeah, absolutely.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: This was purely a function, Madam Chair, of...

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: Background checks for vendors taking longer than we thought they would.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Got it.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: We have the exact same goal as we did a year ago, serving every school district by the end of FY29.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: We've had to delay the expansion by a little bit due to essentially a ministerial matter.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And what's the estimated timeline, revised timeline, I should say?

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: It's actually the same for reaching the goal of every school district.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: It's by the end of FY20.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So the end goal is the same, but it's just the middle part that's pushing out.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay.

chapters.summary: Lee asks about $500K savings from ending the cloud migration project. Levine explains they canceled an expensive contract with CIVIS and are insourcing services with 9 new DOTS division staff for better service at lower cost.
chapters.title: Lee on cloud migration project discontinuation

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And the budget reflects savings of $500,000 in FY27 from discontinuing the agency's cloud migration project.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So I just wanted to know what the rationale was for ending it.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Does the agency have a plan to modernize its technology infrastructure going forward?

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: Absolutely, and this actually reflects that attempt to modernize.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: One of the things we heard loud and clear from OMB and our outstanding admin division, or excuse me, DOTS division takes this really seriously, is we are insourcing more of our services, bringing expertise in-house.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: We canceled a very expensive contract with CIVIS.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: We're replacing it with boots on the ground in the building, which we think is going to deliver better services for less money.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, perfect.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And bringing it in-house staffing-wise, what does that look like?

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: I believe it's just not even very many people.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: We're going to have nine new folks for our DOTS division, and that's going to be transformational, we believe, in our ability to modernize our IT infrastructure.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, perfect.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Great.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And I'm going to stop there to allow for members to ask more questions, but I'm chairing.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Yeah.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Oh, yes, yes, yes.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: I'm sorry.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So we've also been recognized by Councilmember Aldebal, Councilmember Wilson on Zoom, and Councilmember De La Rosa.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And with that, I'll pass it off to Chair Epstein.

chapters.summary: Epstein asks about wait times for workers filing complaints, noting six-month backlogs. Levine and Wagoner say the goal is to reduce the backlog to zero with new staffing, though it may take about a year.
chapters.title: Epstein on enforcement wait times and six-month backlog

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: Thank you, Commissioner, and thank you all for being here and the time you put into these issues we care so much about.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: The preliminary hearing, we talked a lot around the wait time that people had when someone had an hours issue.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: It would take them six months before they'd even hear from someone.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: The investigations would take potentially a year.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: Based on the funding that were being proposed in the executive budget, what would the wait time look like for a regular New Yorker?

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: Who works at McDonald's and feels like they're not getting their schedules appropriately, and there's concerns around that.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: So the wait time for an ordinary New Yorker is going to depend, of course, on how much information we get and whether we detect a work-wide violation.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: I think the deeper problem that you're absolutely right,

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: Right to highlight is what you also said, which is six month backlog of enforcement.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: What that means is that folks who had their wages stolen in some cases, the employers who stole them are sitting on that money while workers are being left holding the bag.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: Our goal.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: And more than our goal, we believe we are now equipped, thanks to the mayor's investment, is to take that six-month backlog and reduce it to zero.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: And I want to turn things over to our deputy commissioner to say more about that.

utterances.speaker: Elizabeth Wagoner
utterances.text: No, that's right.

utterances.speaker: Elizabeth Wagoner
utterances.text: You know, in the metrics that led to the development of the ultimate number, that is explicitly what we're looking at.

utterances.speaker: Elizabeth Wagoner
utterances.text: What would it take to bring it down to zero?

utterances.speaker: Elizabeth Wagoner
utterances.text: And we believe we'll be able to achieve it.

chapters.summary: Epstein presses on how many investigators will be hired. Wagoner confirms 10 investigators are being hired now, nearly doubling the current 11, with the civil service exam open through June 23.
chapters.title: Epstein on hiring 10 new investigators for worker protection

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: And so how many investigators are you planning to hire?

utterances.speaker: Elizabeth Wagoner
utterances.text: And that's going to be happening over a period of years, but we have 10 vacancies posted today.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: Everyone hear that?

utterances.speaker: Elizabeth Wagoner
utterances.text: Anyone watching this hearing, please go online, find the job, and apply.

utterances.speaker: Elizabeth Wagoner
utterances.text: The civil service exam is also open right now and closes on June 23rd.

utterances.speaker: Elizabeth Wagoner
utterances.text: So we're looking to get it done very quickly to bring new people on.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: If you could help circulate those ad information, I'm happy to put it in an e-blast, let people know.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: So if you have sent something over to my team, we're happy to spread the word.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: It would be our pleasure.

utterances.speaker: Carlos Ortiz
utterances.text: Thank you, Chair.

utterances.speaker: Carlos Ortiz
utterances.text: We blasted our job postings out yesterday to all elected offices, so we can make sure we re-up that again.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: There are also consumer protection investigators.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: with the same civil service deadline for all those watching at home.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: Okay, so 10 is going to get you to zero wait time?

chapters.summary: Epstein expresses concern that zero backlog may not be achieved soon. Wagoner hopes to achieve it within a year, noting the pace allows proper training and absorption of new staff.
chapters.title: Epstein on timeline for eliminating six-month backlog

utterances.speaker: Elizabeth Wagoner
utterances.text: 10 will not, but obviously there's resources coming each year, and so this is really pacing us at...

utterances.speaker: Elizabeth Wagoner
utterances.text: A rate that will allow us to hire up and absorb new people.

utterances.speaker: Elizabeth Wagoner
utterances.text: You know, of course, if we have the ability to move faster and bring on more, and we're seeing that there would be a benefit to the backlog, we'll be working with our partners at OMB to show them those metrics and move forward on a...

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: So, I'm sorry.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: I just wanted to know, like, so we're not getting to zero in the fall.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: We're still, so people are waiting six months to even speak to someone, is what we were told, even if they submitted their documents.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: When are we going to get to a place, what day?

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: Would someone be able to get to a short wait time?

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: Are we talking about 2029?

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: Are we talking about 2030?

utterances.speaker: Elizabeth Wagoner
utterances.text: It is very hard to say, but I would love to try to achieve that.

utterances.speaker: Elizabeth Wagoner
utterances.text: I would love to try to achieve that within a year, but we're going to have to see how it goes.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: If I may make two points, Chair.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: One, just to be clear, our worker protection, LLPS, is getting 10 investigators this year, but that's out of a total of 29 new folks.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: Which is, I don't know what percentage that is for the division, but it's a massive increase.

utterances.speaker: Elizabeth Wagoner
utterances.text: It's almost a doubling of our investigators.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: So just to be clear, you have 10 investigators now.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: You're going to get 10.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: You'll double the number of investigators?

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: 10 investigators, but there are attorneys, there are some clerical, I don't know.

utterances.speaker: Elizabeth Wagoner
utterances.text: Right, I believe we have 11 investigators now, so adding 10 more is almost doubling it.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: And then you're going to get some lawyers too, I assume?

utterances.speaker: Elizabeth Wagoner
utterances.text: So supervising investigators, which also increases capacity on that team, lawyers as well.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: How many supervisors, how many lawyers?

utterances.speaker: Elizabeth Wagoner
utterances.text: So we're starting off with a total staff of 54 before this addition, and then we'll have 29 afterwards.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: 29 additional.

utterances.speaker: Elizabeth Wagoner
utterances.text: Additional, in addition to that.

chapters.summary: Epstein asks for detailed staffing breakdown. Wagoner confirms 29 new positions for OLPS: 11 lawyers, 10 investigators, 8 data scientists, including supervisory staff. GC gets 20 positions: 17 lawyers and 3 investigators.
chapters.title: Epstein on staffing breakdown: 29 new OLPS positions

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: And so 10 investigators, a couple supervisors.

utterances.speaker: Elizabeth Wagoner
utterances.text: And then we've got, now let me see if I can do this.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Elizabeth Wagoner
utterances.text: We've got, let me see, 10 lawyers.

utterances.speaker: Elizabeth Wagoner
utterances.text: A couple supervisors.

utterances.speaker: Elizabeth Wagoner
utterances.text: And the rest are going to be data scientists.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: Data scientists.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: So they're doing data analysis for folks.

utterances.speaker: Elizabeth Wagoner
utterances.text: So we use our data analytics team on our class-wide cases under all of our laws to take employer data or app data

utterances.speaker: Elizabeth Wagoner
utterances.text: and identify it precisely to count each violation that each worker in the class experienced.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: And so...

chapters.summary: Epstein asks how many cases are in court or negotiations. Wagoner says several dozen large class cases are in various stages but promises to follow up with precise numbers.
chapters.title: Epstein on number of active litigation cases

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: How many active litigation cases do you have now then?

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: For your current legal team.

utterances.speaker: Elizabeth Wagoner
utterances.text: So most of our enforcement work is pre-litigation.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: Sure.

utterances.speaker: Elizabeth Wagoner
utterances.text: And so we have dozens of large class cases that attorneys are handling.

utterances.speaker: Elizabeth Wagoner
utterances.text: And in various stages of the process, whether that's in the data analytics process or in the settlement negotiations process.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: Could you give me a general number?

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: How many cases are in court?

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: How many cases are in negotiations?

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: I'm sure you have that number somewhere.

utterances.speaker: Elizabeth Wagoner
utterances.text: We'd have to follow up with you to give you a precise number.

utterances.speaker: Elizabeth Wagoner
utterances.text: I can say today that it's several dozen, but we can definitely get those breakouts.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: I would appreciate that.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: And then I am concerned about the backlog.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: And, you know, I know the commissioner and you are deeply committed to this.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: You wouldn't be doing it otherwise.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And to say that maybe in a year we might be in a better position is a little concerning to me because, you know, this is a priority for all of us.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And I'm wondering if you had more resources staffing up sooner, like instead of hiring 12, 10 investigators, you hired 15.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Obviously, that backlog would reduce quicker.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Is that a fair statement?

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: Oh, go ahead.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: One point I would make, and then I'll turn it over to the deputy commissioner, is, you know, we do have the– it's a good problem to have, but we are such a lean agency that the same folks who are doing those investigations are interviewing candidates.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: And they're going to be spending a lot of the summer both full steam ahead on their cases and full steam ahead on interviewing candidates.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: And they're going to keep interviewing through the rest of the year.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: So the reality is that, again, new lines, it's a wonderful problem to have, but it does, there are only so many people we can onboard.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: And we really designed the 181 very, OMB would not have authorized that headcount if we didn't show them in granular detail that we have the capacity to bring those folks on.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: So I'm not saying, you know, we could have conversations about what additional capacity would mean in hiring, but I do just want to say that there is, there are operational limits on how many folks we can bring on.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: You know, we could hire a million people tomorrow that we would not actually be able to close the backlog tomorrow.

utterances.speaker: Elizabeth Wagoner
utterances.text: That's right.

utterances.speaker: Elizabeth Wagoner
utterances.text: In terms of the resources to train people, this is the right number to be able to absorb new staff, to have them

utterances.speaker: Elizabeth Wagoner
utterances.text: learn and follow our procedures for investigations, and to ultimately continue delivering the high level of service that we offer workers.

chapters.summary: Epstein asks about funding for organizations like Worker Justice Project to handle deactivation case intake. Levine and Ortiz confirm no direct funding is allocated but emphasize symbiotic partnerships with these organizations.
chapters.title: Epstein on supporting community organizations for deactivation cases

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: We've heard, you choose one industry, deactivation, right?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So, you know, we've heard from the deliverances that every day two, three hundred people are banging down their door about deactivation.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So they don't have capacity to do it.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So how do we support them to ensure that they, you know, they can maybe be a frontline person to limit the number of cases that are coming to you?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Do you have resources in the budget to help organizations like that who are really on the front lines trying to help?

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: We have resources in the budget to make sure that we have the staff to meet those needs.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: We're also working very closely with outside partners to make sure that there's an orderly process, that people know their rights.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: Do you want to talk a little bit about deactivation implementation?

utterances.speaker: Elizabeth Wagoner
utterances.text: Sure.

utterances.speaker: Elizabeth Wagoner
utterances.text: And just to first say, you know, WJP and all of our community partners do extraordinary work.

utterances.speaker: Elizabeth Wagoner
utterances.text: They are a critical part of the worker protection infrastructure.

utterances.speaker: Elizabeth Wagoner
utterances.text: And, you know, definitely want to see them continue to be supported to do that work so that they can continue to refer us cases so that we can continue to refer them cases.

utterances.speaker: Elizabeth Wagoner
utterances.text: matters outside of our jurisdiction, it's really critical.

utterances.speaker: Elizabeth Wagoner
utterances.text: And we will continue to work with organizations who are working with delivery workers to make sure they're able to get delivery workers to us so that we can pursue those deactivation matters for them.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So you have no money in the budget to subcontract them to do this work.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Is that what I hear you saying?

utterances.speaker: Elizabeth Wagoner
utterances.text: We do not manage those contracts.

utterances.speaker: Elizabeth Wagoner
utterances.text: That's right.

utterances.speaker: Elizabeth Wagoner
utterances.text: That runs through.

utterances.speaker: Elizabeth Wagoner
utterances.text: Carlos should speak to this, but yeah.

utterances.speaker: Carlos Ortiz
utterances.text: Well, that's correct.

utterances.speaker: Carlos Ortiz
utterances.text: The money that we were allocated was for personnel services and to increase the agency's capacity.

utterances.speaker: Carlos Ortiz
utterances.text: I recognize your point and full agreement that over the past 10 years, the agency has had historic disinvestment.

utterances.speaker: Carlos Ortiz
utterances.text: But that's why we're incredibly excited with what the mayor has done, obviously with the support of the council and our allies that you're bringing up in this conversation too, Chair, to bring us to a place where we can meet our mandates and hopefully grow even further as new mandates come.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Yeah, I mean, I do care about the street vendors, and they may not come to DCWP right away.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: They may need an organization like the Street Vendor Project to ferret out.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: what's an appropriate case to come to.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: They have the relationships.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: It would be great to see funding from the agency just as we know

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: we're going to have to enforce these laws, but we know these are really culturally competent organizations who are on the ground, who are going to be able to figure out what should come to us and what shouldn't.

chapters.summary: Epstein pushes for direct agency funding for Street Vendor Project and Worker Justice Project, noting they have more members than resources to handle. Levine acknowledges their vital role but says DCWP doesn't manage those contracts.
chapters.title: Epstein urges direct funding for street vendor and worker organizations

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Shouldn't there be resources from your agency to go directly to them?

utterances.speaker: Carlos Ortiz
utterances.text: Well, I would say, Chair, that that partnership exists and will.

utterances.speaker: Carlos Ortiz
utterances.text: And we plan to work closely with Street Vendor Projects and our new executive director for Street Vendor Services as well to go directly to street vendors, to go to the places where they're vending to ensure that they understand about what services and licenses are available by the agency.

utterances.speaker: Carlos Ortiz
utterances.text: So that's what our current PS allocation is.

utterances.speaker: Carlos Ortiz
utterances.text: And, again, we look forward to having these conversations if we're going to need more resources eventually.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Yeah, I mean, I appreciate you need more resources.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: But, Carlos, you wouldn't disagree that they need resources too, right?

utterances.speaker: Carlos Ortiz
utterances.text: I think no thank you thank you chair I was sorry I distracted him no I wouldn't disagree that I've had conversations directly with WJP and other organizations I know that they have conversations directly the council themselves over discretionary funding

utterances.speaker: Carlos Ortiz
utterances.text: so I understand that those ask her in as well too I I'm just can only speak to our budget and our resources and what we've been allocated yeah and I would just add to that council member chair

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: Right.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: We do not get money for those organizations.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: What I will say, though, is that I really believe that it is symbiotic in a way.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: We are working closely, for example, with Street Vendor Project to get the word out this summer about getting off the wait list and getting a license.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: That is going to provide a real value proposition for the Street Vendor Project to all the vendors out on the street.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: Similar with Worker Justice Project, NYCHA, they're going to work with their members to explain to them deactivation protections.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: We're going to work with NYCHA and WJP.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: I think the more that we partner closely with these organizations, the more that they're going to be able to offer value to their members.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: And I hope that creates a virtuous cycle.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: These organizations gain more.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: visibility and reach and that we can do the same.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Yeah, Commissioner, I couldn't agree more with that statement.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And I, you know, and I just would say that I know we have a lot of colleagues have questions, but I'd love to see if there's a way we can get them direct funding to do that because

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: I've heard directly from all of them, whether it's the Worker Justice Project, like you mentioned, or the Street Vendor Project we just talked about, who, you know, there are more members coming to them than they have the resources to handle.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And while you'll be the agency empowered to protect their members, as well as all the consumers out there,

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: there's not as many resources that they can turn to to be able to do this work.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And we'd love to see the administration play a vital role in getting those resources to them.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: I hear you on that.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: And to be clear, and I think I speak for everyone here, we could not do our job.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: We could not do the work of protection we've done without partners like WJP and NYCHA.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: I totally agree on the vital role these organizations play.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And thank you.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: I'll turn it back over to Chair Lee.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Great.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Just also want to recognize we've been joined by Councilmember Areola online, and Tiffany, come on.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Hello.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So we are going to start with Deputy Speaker, followed by Councilmember Banks.

chapters.summary: Williams asks how DCWP defines equitable enforcement and what metrics determine fair application across communities. Levine emphasizes targeting enforcement where abuses disproportionately affect communities of color and immigrant New Yorkers.
chapters.title: Deputy Speaker Williams on equitable enforcement and racial equity

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Hello.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Just a macro question around the racial equity plans.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So how does DCWP define equitable enforcement, and what metrics does the agency use to determine

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And the weather enforcement practices are being applied fairly across communities.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: Well, I would make two points, because I think this is a critically important issue.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: The first is that we know that some of the worst economic abuses are targeted at New Yorkers of color, primarily at black and brown New Yorkers and immigrant New Yorkers.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: That is why I am fully committed not only to continuing to vigorously enforce our laws, but making sure that our enforcement actually reflects where abuses are happening throughout our city.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: There have been studies, not in New York, but nationwide, that consumers of color, for example, are less likely to report violations to government agencies.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: What does that mean?

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: It means that government agencies are less likely to hold accountable those who prey on communities of color.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: That is not acceptable.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: My commitment at DCWP is to make sure we're serving everyone in New York City, especially those being harmed the most by these abuses.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: And that is why we take so much pride in our outreach efforts.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: We are aiming to do two events a day, more than 700 events every year.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: We go to every borough, every community.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: And what I say to every group is, if you don't feel comfortable filing a complaint with the government, you can go to Worker Justice Project, you can go to Make the Road, go to another group, file a complaint with us.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: It is critical that we not only kind of sit in our downtown office waiting for complaints, that we go out in the community and make sure that we're serving people who are most targeted by economic predation.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And so...

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Back to the racial equity plans, though, like what goals has DCWP put forward that matches what you just said?

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: Well, a key goal, and this came up in the core report that came out very recently, is on youth financial empowerment.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: You know, the fact of the matter is we're at the beginning of summer.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: We know that a lot of young New Yorkers are going to have their first jobs, and they're instantly going to be targets for predatory fintechs, predatory actors who are going to try to separate them from their paychecks.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: One of the points that was made by CORE was that DCWP should really step up its financial empowerment work, especially its youth financial empowerment work, including in partnership with Kids Rise.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: I could not agree more with that.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: We're never going to have the resources to take down every bad actor, even with the council's generous proposal this morning.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: So that's why I think it's really important to empower people in school banking, which was another core recommendation.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: It's something we are implementing, youth financial empowerment.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: We're going to reach every school district by the end of FY29.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: So I thought the core report was right on point in terms of our goals and how we're going to meet them over the years to come.

chapters.summary: Williams asks whether DCWP has conducted geographical or demographical analysis of where violations, inspections, and enforcement are concentrated, particularly regarding revenue generation across communities. Levine promises to share available data.
chapters.title: Williams on geographical analysis of enforcement and revenue

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And just to go back to equitable enforcement, because you answered that question by primarily talking about workers, but businesses.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So has DCWP conducted any geographical or demographical analysis?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: of where violations, inspections, and enforcement actions are concentrated citywide.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And so that's what I'm talking about.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: I see.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: I apologize.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: For me, because I think about budget equity, we're in a budget hearing, what I want to know, and I'm always interested in, is where is the city also generating revenue?

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: Yes.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Are you only generating revenue in certain communities over others?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And so that is sort of the source of my first question, coupled with the other one about if you have any geographical or demographical analysis of where violations are occurring across New York City.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: I don't have it at my fingertips.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: I do believe we have pretty good analyses of where we're seeing violations throughout the city.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: But I also want to be clear and repeat what I said earlier.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: I don't believe in gotcha enforcement, especially with small businesses.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: We've seen an exodus of small businesses last year under the final year of the Adams administration.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: We're really trying to reverse that.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: I was very proud that Mike's team, the general counsel team, returned more than half a million dollars to immigrant-owned small businesses, first enforcement action in our history to do that, who were ripped off by Hungry Panda, a delivery app.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: So I'm happy to get you additional data on where our enforcement is.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Yes, I would love to ask DOT a similar question.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Like, if you do have a map.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Of where you're primarily finding certain businesses across New York City.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: That would be helpful.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: I'm happy to see what we can get you on that.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: I think we do have that.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: We will follow up.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Okay, great.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Councilmember Banks.

chapters.summary: Banks asks about DCWP's steps to educate tax preparers and consumers about new itemized fee disclosure requirements under Local Law 168. Levine describes warning blitzes to all licensed tax preparers.
chapters.title: CM Banks on Local Law 168 tax preparer fee disclosures

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: Thank you, Chairs.

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: And Commissioner, to your team, when it comes to Local Law 168, which went into effect on May 27, just recently,

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: which requires tax preparers to provide consumers with itemized fee and disclosures before and after services are rendered.

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: Want to know what steps has a DCWP taken to educate preparers and consumers about these new requirements to ensure compliance with the law?

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: Yeah, we take this incredibly seriously.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: We see so much predation in the tax prep space.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: It's one reason why we were so aggressive this year in promoting our free tax prep and doing a warning blitz.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: I think the first in our history to every licensed tax prep provider in the city that we are looking out for violations.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: Mike, do you want to share more on our implementation?

utterances.speaker: Michael Tiger
utterances.text: No, I think that's a very important topic and something that we always have our ear to the ground on.

utterances.speaker: Michael Tiger
utterances.text: You know, we had a case against rocket tax last year.

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: Right, okay.

utterances.speaker: Michael Tiger
utterances.text: That you know and we're always looking for compliance and that and for that settlement and we're always looking for new targets you know one of the great things we have a consumer services you a unit

utterances.speaker: Michael Tiger
