utterances.text: that is our ear to the ground for all consumer complaints and we look at the trends and we look both in terms of or potential targets and this is in tax prep in all our categories like

utterances.speaker: Michael Tiger
utterances.text: Who are potential targets?

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: individual businesses that we see are repeat violators of the laws and rules we enforce?

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: And what are the new scams that we didn't even know about, but people on the street are telling us?

utterances.speaker: Michael Tiger
utterances.text: And this is in tax prep in all our categories.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: So we always encourage people to go directly to our website and file complaints.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: That's really the grist for how we determine

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: how we're going to direct our enforcement.

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: Thank you for that response.

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: Let me just get my questions out, and then you can chime in on the next one if you want.

utterances.speaker: Michael Tiger
utterances.text: Like, who are potential targets?

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: While tax season is technically over, many New Yorkers continue to file extensions, amendments on their returns, and they utilize the tax preparation services throughout the year.

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: Has DCWP already begun consumer outreach regarding local law 168?

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: And if so, what has the outreach look like and what lessons has the agency learned thus far?

chapters.summary: Banks asks about consumer outreach beyond tax season and multilingual educational materials. Ortiz describes updating materials, partnering with community-based organizations, and hitting hundreds of events during tax season.
chapters.title: Banks on year-round tax prep outreach and partnerships

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: Yeah, I'll ask Carlos to describe a little bit of some of the specific outreach, but if I may just brief point, public service announcement for everyone, our New York City free tax prep, which serves over 100,000 New Yorkers every year, no fees, no fines, no nothing,

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: except tax help, is available year-round.

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: But there's a large percentage of folks who don't take advantage of it.

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: Absolutely.

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: They use it on private.

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: Yes.

utterances.speaker: Carlos Ortiz
utterances.text: Yes, and, Counselor, I was going to mention that we are updating our educational materials for businesses as well, and we should be distributing those soon so they can understand their obligations to the law.

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: And then on the educational materials.

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: We're hoping that they're obviously multilingual.

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: Obviously, you talked about the public awareness campaigns.

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: And what partnerships are you going to be forming to develop to ensure that both consumers and tax preparers understand their rights and the obligations of Local Law 168?

utterances.speaker: Carlos Ortiz
utterances.text: Well, traditionally, our outreach model is based on deep ties with local community-based organizations, being able to plug into their regular membership and convenings of community members.

utterances.speaker: Carlos Ortiz
utterances.text: So I think we're going to activate those again.

utterances.speaker: Carlos Ortiz
utterances.text: Typically, throughout the tax season, we hit hundreds of events to help educate consumers, and that even starts before the tax season.

utterances.speaker: Carlos Ortiz
utterances.text: So I think we're going to plug into similar aspects and working with also business associations and the representatives.

utterances.speaker: Carlos Ortiz
utterances.text: I think that's been a trusted model for us in our outreach.

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: Excellent.

chapters.summary: Banks asks what compliance monitoring looks like and what enforcement tools are available. Levine and Tiger describe sweeps of tax preparers, field enforcement division, and lawyers reviewing complaints to identify targets.
chapters.title: Banks on compliance monitoring for Local Law 168

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: And what will compliance monitoring look like with Local Law 168?

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: What enforcement tools are available to address violations?

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: And has the agency identified any implementation challenges that may require additional staffing resources or legislative changes moving forward?

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: I think we have, thanks in part to the law and to the investment by the mayor, I actually think we have strong authority and capacity to take these on.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: One technique we've done in the past, which has been effective, is sweeps, where we do a broad prep.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: Because precisely for the reason you said, most New Yorkers are still using private preparers.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: Correct.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: Many of them are breaking the law.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: You know, when we do a sweep, we're sending a message that we're looking across the marketplace.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Do you want to add to that?

utterances.speaker: Michael Tiger
utterances.text: Right, and there are two ways we can conduct this.

utterances.speaker: Michael Tiger
utterances.text: We have a field enforcement division.

utterances.speaker: Michael Tiger
utterances.text: People are out in the street every day as we're speaking, going to business, making sure they're complying with the consumer face, mostly consumer facing laws and rules of this of the city.

utterances.speaker: Michael Tiger
utterances.text: And so we have people and around tax season, we have sweeps of those and for making sure that tax preparers are complying with the specific laws and rules that they need to comply with.

utterances.speaker: Michael Tiger
utterances.text: Then we have lawyers.

utterances.speaker: Michael Tiger
utterances.text: You know, back in the office, or looking at those complaints, like I mentioned before, council member, and saying, which businesses should we be taking a deeper look into?

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: Well, thank you for your responses, and I'm looking forward to continue working with you.

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Michael Tiger
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: We have Councilmember Pierina Sanchez, followed by Councilmember Krishnan.

chapters.summary: Pierina Sanchez asks about plans for processing 10,500 new general vendor licenses with only 6 new staff. Levine expresses confidence in the efficient licensing division with 4-minute average wait times and 4-day processing.
chapters.title: CM Sanchez on street vending reform and license processing

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: Thank you, Chair, and good morning.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: First, I just want to start with expressing how exciting it is to see the agency's trajectory these last six months.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: I think any mayor or any executive envisions a commissioner like you when they win office with coming in with a track record of success, but also an ambitious vision for the agency and a readiness to roll up your sleeve with the team that is there.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: I just want to congratulate you on everything that you guys are all doing together for New Yorkers.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: I'm going to do the thing where I ask all my questions up front and then hope that there's time for a response.

utterances.speaker: Michael Tiger
utterances.text: We'll take notes.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: So to start, DCWP, I mean, I'm biased.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: You are my favorite.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: You're one of my favorite agencies.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: Don't tell HPD.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: You've just been such a fantastic partner on reforming the street vending system in New York City.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: Special shout out to Carlos Ortiz and everyone back there.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: We have really reframed this problem together in New York City, and I appreciate it.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: So my first question here is, you know, while I appreciate and was very happy to see significant investments for the implementation of street vending reform, I remain concerned that the piece that

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: DCWP has around processing 10,500 new general vendor licenses is still too low.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: I understand that there is only six new staff that are going to be added for fiscal year 27.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: So I just want to understand the plans for processing these new applications per local law 54 with this staffing.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: Question two.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: The new licenses that are being made available in January will be the first change to the general vendor cap since 1979.

chapters.summary: Sanchez asks how DCWP will prioritize new general vendor licenses. Levine says they'll start with 9,000 people on the existing wait list, then create a new wait list, with rules coming in weeks.
chapters.title: Sanchez on wait list prioritization for new vendor licenses

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: So the questions are, when the new wait list opens, how will DCWP prioritize the new general vendor licenses?

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: Do you have a sense of the criteria the agency will be using?

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: And how can we ensure that licenses are getting to the hands of the street vendors who've been waiting for decades?

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: And the third one on street vending is outreach.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: The chair touched on this a bit regarding street vendor project and our partners on the ground.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: But I suppose my question is whether there are

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: any dedicated staff within the agency across the 189 new lines that you mentioned in your opening specifically for outreach.

chapters.summary: Sanchez asks for a status update on the click-to-cancel rule. Levine says it's currently a proposal, received many comments, and the Roosevelt Institute estimated it could save New Yorkers $160M annually.
chapters.title: Sanchez on click-to-cancel rule status and staffing

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: And then, as you know, Commissioner, I am ecstatic about the click to cancel rule and that the momentum the agency has on this policy.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: You know, I speak for myself and I know many New Yorkers who are just so freaking annoyed by companies that make it impossible to leave subscriptions,

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: that harass you even after you're gone, but really trap New Yorkers into subscriptions that they don't need.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: So just a status update on where we are with the proposed rules and is there any staffing associated with implementation of that new rule.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: Okay, thank you.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: Two seconds to spare.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: That was great.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: I'm going to run through each one and we'll see how I do, okay?

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: And Carlos can back me up.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: All right, licensing staffing.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: We are confident we have the capacity in our licensing division to hit the ground running this summer on getting the general vendor licenses out the door.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: We have an extremely efficient licensing division.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: And I'll just say, you know, average wait time of four minutes when you're in the office, four days for processing.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: Typically, most, about 90% of our licensees now apply online.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: We recognize many street vendors are going to prefer the in-person option, and that is why we asked for and received six new lines for FY27.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: We are confident we're going to be able to meet those needs and continue, you know, outstanding, I think, the best in the city for licensing service to our smallest small businesses and our newest licensees.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: With respect to the wait list and how to prioritize, we're going to be releasing rules on that in the next couple weeks.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: I could say as a general matter, Council Member, we're going to start with about the 9,000 folks who are already on the wait list, which was closed in 2016.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: Once we process those applications, we'll move on to creating a new wait list and we'll start processing those applications.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: So we're going to put out the rule soon, but my commitment is to make sure this process is predictable, people understand it, people know that even if they don't get a license in the first tranche, they know where they are in line.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: That's really important to us.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: With respect to outreach, we really want to go big this summer.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: And Carlos will be able to add to this, I think, after I get through the questions.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: I know we're meeting with you in a couple days about this.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: We've been working with street vendor project with the Office of Street Vending with Small Business Services.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: We know that this is a community not used to being helped by the city.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: Usually when they interact with city officials, it's police or other enforcement.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: We want to reach out to our street vendors with licenses and with information on how to obtain licenses.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: So we are dedicating staff to outreach on that, and Carlos can share more.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: Last, but definitely not least, we're incredibly excited about the clip-to-cancel rule, as I share your enthusiasm.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: It is a proposal right now.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: We are the first city.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: in America to propose a rule like this.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: We're very excited about it.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: We received a lot of comments.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: I don't know the exact number, but I think one of the most interesting comments is from the Roosevelt Institute, which estimated that this could save New Yorkers up to $160 million every year.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: That is the scale of tricks and traps we are facing.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: So we're extremely excited.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: The next step will be we're going to make a decision about whether to adopt the rule and make it final.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: Do you want to add a little bit to the outreach?

utterances.speaker: Carlos Ortiz
utterances.text: Yes, thank you, Commissioner.

utterances.speaker: Carlos Ortiz
utterances.text: I think we're already developing plans for how to meet street vendors where they are, including by identifying high-traffic areas where vendors are conducting their business activity.

utterances.speaker: Carlos Ortiz
utterances.text: Our community affairs team is six members right now, but they burn a lot of shoe leather.

utterances.speaker: Carlos Ortiz
utterances.text: I think they're, I personally believe they're the best outreach team in the city, and our overall external affairs division often contributes as well to helping out in outreach initiatives.

utterances.speaker: Carlos Ortiz
utterances.text: So I think we plan to be out there extensively over the course of the summer.

utterances.speaker: Carlos Ortiz
utterances.text: Also working with agency partners, too, to make sure that we have boots on the ground to accomplish our goals.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: There you go.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Thank you, Chair.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Cheers.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, next we have Councilmember Krishnan, followed by Councilmember Wong.

chapters.summary: Krishnan asks if DCWP has sufficient resources for the for-hire vehicle and delivery worker deactivation laws. Levine expresses confidence, and Wagoner describes rulemaking efforts and staffing plans using investigators and attorneys.
chapters.title: CM Krishnan on deactivation bill implementation readiness

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: Thank you, Chairs, Commissioner, Deputy Commissioner, and all the whole team.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: Thank you all so much for all your great work at DCWP and your leadership.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: Proud to see all that you're doing and to partner together.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: Deputy Commissioner, First Deputy Commissioner Carlos Ortiz, also a constituent in Jackson Heights, too.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: So I appreciate you all paying close attention to the issues there from street vendors.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: The taxi cab drivers, everything else.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: I just have a quick question.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: So one is, you know, we've talked a bit about having the resources that you all need for the new legislation that we've passed.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: And so I just want to make sure we've talked a lot about street vendor implementation.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: Very glad to hear that.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: I know, Commissioner, we've spoken a bunch about.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: Taxi driver, the deactivation bill that we've passed and how important that is.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: So I just want to make sure you all feel that you have what you need for the number of cases that will come in, the look back period, and how that will be raised.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: You feel good with what you've got to unveil that program?

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: I do, Councilmember.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: I really appreciate the question.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: I think it was probably apparent from the last hearing we had that my biggest concern actually going into the summer is how we're going to make sure we can meet the Council's mandate on

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: the outstanding bill we championed on for higher vehicle deactivation and then later in the year, at the end of the year, delivery worker deactivation.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: I do feel confident.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: Liz's team is working very hard to put an infrastructure in place.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: You know, we're going to see what the volume is.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: If it spikes much higher than we think, you know, we might have to revisit it.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: But at this point, based on our projections and staffing, we do have confidence we can meet those needs.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: Great.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: And can you say a bit more about, is it going to be a new staff, a set of staff attorneys hired that would be focused on this in particular?

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: What would that staffing look like?

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: Let me turn that over to our person in charge of that.

utterances.speaker: Elizabeth Wagoner
utterances.text: Sure.

utterances.speaker: Elizabeth Wagoner
utterances.text: Right now, we're very focused on rulemaking.

utterances.speaker: Elizabeth Wagoner
utterances.text: and being sure we can get a rule out that lays out some of the key details of how apps are required to handle their various obligations under this law.

utterances.speaker: Elizabeth Wagoner
utterances.text: We are looking to staff this law the way we have successfully enforced many of our other laws with investigators trained to do the initial intake work.

utterances.speaker: Elizabeth Wagoner
utterances.text: Who often are able to successfully resolve a case without attorney involvement and then moving to attorneys when that's not possible.

utterances.speaker: Elizabeth Wagoner
utterances.text: And, you know, we believe that we're going to be able to be able to start successfully handling those cases.

utterances.speaker: Elizabeth Wagoner
utterances.text: Great.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: Very glad to hear it.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: And, Commissioner, I would just say, too, any ways that we can partner together, you know, to get the word out to in districts where there's obviously high, you know, driver resident populations, you know, partner with us, my office, with NYCHA.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: Very happy to find any ways to get the word out there.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: We would love to do that.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: We're eager to get the word out.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: NYCHA has been a great partner on this, and we'd love to work with you further.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: Absolutely.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: Well, thanks for your leadership.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: And I know Carlos will follow up separately, too, on a couple quality of life issues, too, in the district that we wanted to address with you all, too.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: So appreciate your partnership there, too.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: Thank you, Councilman.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: Thanks.

utterances.speaker: Elizabeth Wagoner
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Elizabeth Wagoner
utterances.text: Councilmember Wong, followed by Councilmember Caban.

chapters.summary: Wong describes a major problem with unlicensed tow trucks using fake plates to extort accident victims in his district. Levine and Tiger describe enforcement actions including a case against Instant Recovery and partnership with NYPD.
chapters.title: CM Wong on unlicensed tow truck enforcement

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Thank you, Chair.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: In my district, there's a big problem of unlicensed tow trucks.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: And the problem is this.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Whenever there's an accident, the police is no longer required to show up at the scene.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: So we have these tow trucks that pop out of nowhere quite often with fake plates.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: And with a combination of coercion and threats, they were able to convince many of these drivers to let their cars be towed away with that.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: And they don't know, the drivers don't know that they were fake, unlicensed, or with fake licenses.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: This is only my fifth month at the job, and I'm hearing like over a dozen of these complaints

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: that their cars got towed away to some backyard shop, and they're being extorted on hundreds of thousands of dollars in storage fees and in repairs that they...

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: They did not expect.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: So can you talk about what is being done and how could your increase in headcount be able to step up the enforcement of checking their licenses?

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: I'm very happy to do that, Council Member.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: I'm going to frame it up and then turn these over to our General Counsel to talk about our, including our recent enforcement action against one of these unlicensed companies.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: Let me just start by saying this is a huge problem across the city, a big problem in Queens.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: And one thing I really want to stress is we always want to educate consumers about this.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: We want to educate New Yorkers to use.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: There's licensed tow providers.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: But one thing I always say to people is it is not on consumers to get this right.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: You've just been in a car accident.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: Your car just broke down.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: You can't realistically expect people to go to DCWP.gov and see if, .nyc.gov, and see if they're licensed.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: So my big focus is on making sure these unlicensed tow companies are held accountable.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: The onus has to be on the tow companies to stop operating illegally on our streets, not on consumers to do their own due diligence when they're in some of the hardest moments of their lives.

utterances.speaker: Michael Tiger
utterances.text: Yeah, we take this very seriously.

utterances.speaker: Michael Tiger
utterances.text: I mean, this is one of our core license categories that we get the most complaints about, not just about unlicensed activity.

utterances.speaker: Michael Tiger
utterances.text: In fact, often it's from licensed actors who are overcharging.

utterances.speaker: Michael Tiger
utterances.text: The council has very wisely set very strict limits on how much tow companies can charge consumers because it is such a precarious time for consumers, as the commissioner was alluding to and

utterances.speaker: Michael Tiger
utterances.text: as you were referencing.

utterances.speaker: Michael Tiger
utterances.text: So we look through those complaints.

utterances.speaker: Michael Tiger
utterances.text: We choose which ones are getting the most complaints, and we focus our enforcement in that direction.

utterances.speaker: Michael Tiger
utterances.text: We filed a case against instant recovery, as the commissioner alluded to in his opening testimony.

utterances.speaker: Michael Tiger
utterances.text: We always have several major open-toe investigations ongoing.

utterances.speaker: Michael Tiger
utterances.text: As far as unlicensed activity, that is a little trickier because it's out on the street in real time.

utterances.speaker: Michael Tiger
utterances.text: That's something that we're partnering with the New York Police Department to figure out what the best next steps are.

utterances.speaker: Michael Tiger
utterances.text: And I will say it's...

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: very important

utterances.speaker: Michael Tiger
utterances.text: If you are getting complaints from constituents, we're all ears.

utterances.speaker: Michael Tiger
utterances.text: And there's a lot of different avenues that you can approach.

utterances.speaker: Michael Tiger
utterances.text: Individuals, you know, despite what the commissioner said, can always go to DCWP.

utterances.speaker: Michael Tiger
utterances.text: But you and your staff, we're always, Carlos, my team, we always want to hear what are the companies.

utterances.speaker: Michael Tiger
utterances.text: Because actual individual names with individual facts on the ground will always help sharpen our investigations.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Yeah, I'll share the list with you based on the info I collected from my constituent complaints.

utterances.speaker: Michael Tiger
utterances.text: Great.

utterances.speaker: Michael Tiger
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Thank you, Councilmember.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Thank you, Chair.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Thank you, Councilmember Coban, followed by Councilmember Aldebal.

chapters.summary: Menin asks if DCWP is ready to enforce the Delivery Protection Act, which Levine supports but notes would need 19 additional lines not in the budget. She also asks about expanding fair work week laws to other industries.
chapters.title: Menin on Delivery Protection Act and fair work week expansion

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Hello.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: You guys are my favorite agency.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: I don't need to couch it, my colleague over there.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: You can tell everybody.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Also, thank you for...

utterances.speaker: Michael Tiger
utterances.text: I will tell everybody now.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: I stand by it, whatever.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Also glad to see that you had Amazon cough up, what, close to 10 million in unpaid tickets.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: So I was happy to see that.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Not going to be surprised that I want to ask about the Delivery Protection Act and sort of what that will cost moving forward.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: I know that I heard testimony that you feel good about your ability to...

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Enforce the many laws that we have already passed, the ones that are coming into effect, but we want to do more.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: I think that we share the same ambition in being able to do more.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: We also know that there's just so much that we get in return in terms of raising revenue and putting money back in people's pockets.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: So, you know, at the hearing on the Delivery Protection Act, you testified that you support the bill.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Do you feel ready to, should it pass, enforce the licensing process and requirements that would come along with that?

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Do you, with the numbers that you have, is that enough to...

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Enforce that bill, for example, or do you need more?

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: Well, thank you, Councilmember.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: We enthusiastically support the bill.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: I mean, just yesterday we were discussing before this hearing a Supreme Court case around a company that tried to force for hire, excuse me, last mile delivery workers into arbitration.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: This was in another state, but it just shows the scale of the worker exploitation we see all over the United States.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: Your bill would put a stop to that in New York City.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: With respect to the lines we would need to enforce it, I believe we estimated we would need 19 lines.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: That was not included in the executive budget.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: It still hasn't passed.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Because we want to pass that soon, so it sounds like we need some more money.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: Yes.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: That sounds good.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: I also want to ask, because again, we share the goal in being as ambitious as possible.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: You guys are very good at what you do.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: I know that there may be limits on what you can say about potential legislation or legislation that hasn't even come before you yet.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: What do you see as other potential areas of expanded regulation that you are currently concerned with and to give you a not so surprise?

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Or shocking example, would you support expanding what is already a successful fear work week law?

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: And would you have the resources to do that, or do you need more?

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: Well, without commenting on any specific bills, of course, since we're talking generally, look, I think it is absolutely a problem, especially with the advent of algorithmic scheduling, where

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: AI algorithms are deciding without any regard to the fact that workers have real lives and have families and have second jobs when people need to be on a shift.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: It is not fair to tell a parent the morning of that they have to show up to work.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: We have nation-leading protections for retail, for fast food.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: And what I love about the protections in New York City is that it doesn't matter what the algorithm spits out.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: At the end of the day, if you're a fast food worker, you get two weeks'notice.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: If you're a retail worker, you get 72 hours'notice.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: I'd be very interested in having conversations with you, conversations with the rest of the admin, about expanding those rights to other industries.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: There's no reason workers should have to toil at the mercy of an algorithm that takes no account for the full lives that they lead.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Yeah, you need more money.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: That's the takeaway.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: All right, thank you.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Councilmember Aldebar?

chapters.summary: Aldebol asks whether DCWP is expanding staffing enough to educate consumers, workers, and businesses. Ortiz describes aiming for 760 outreach events annually, up from 600, with 3 new community affairs positions.
chapters.title: CM Aldebol on community outreach capacity expansion

utterances.speaker: Shirley Aldebol
utterances.text: Thank you, Chairs.

utterances.speaker: Shirley Aldebol
utterances.text: And I just want to echo what everyone else has said.

utterances.speaker: Shirley Aldebol
utterances.text: You are certainly a lean, mean fighting machine.

utterances.speaker: Shirley Aldebol
utterances.text: I do have a question about community outreach and outreach to businesses and to workers.

utterances.speaker: Shirley Aldebol
utterances.text: And are you expanding?

utterances.speaker: Shirley Aldebol
utterances.text: Staffing and given all the worker protection laws and consumer laws,

utterances.speaker: Shirley Aldebol
utterances.text: are you expanding enough to have capacity to be able to educate consumers, workers, and businesses?

utterances.speaker: Shirley Aldebol
utterances.text: On what their obligations are and what their rights are.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: I appreciate the question.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: I'll turn it over to our Deputy Commissioner for External Affairs.

utterances.speaker: Carlos Ortiz
utterances.text: Thank you, Commissioner.

utterances.speaker: Carlos Ortiz
utterances.text: Traditionally, on average every year, we hit about 600 outreach events.

utterances.speaker: Carlos Ortiz
utterances.text: I think this year we're aiming to hit about 760, two per day.

utterances.speaker: Carlos Ortiz
utterances.text: We've been doing that through our expansions that we had toward the end of last year and our community affairs team, but also we're receiving an allocation of three additional positions under these new needs that we received from the administration.

utterances.speaker: Carlos Ortiz
utterances.text: We've also, I think, dramatically improved our ability to connect with New Yorkers over social media, digital media, press.

utterances.speaker: Carlos Ortiz
utterances.text: I think we've

utterances.speaker: Carlos Ortiz
utterances.text: We want to, following the direction of our commissioner, making sure that New York City workers, consumers, small businesses understand the role of this agency and how we can serve them.

utterances.speaker: Carlos Ortiz
utterances.text: And we only want to make that grow over the years to come.

chapters.summary: Aldebol asks about outreach for the new security guard wage law taking effect January 1. Levine describes partnering with 32BJ and plans for aggressive enforcement. Ortiz estimates about 70,000 workers in the industry.
chapters.title: Aldebol on security guard minimum wage law implementation

utterances.speaker: Shirley Aldebol
utterances.text: I have a question about security guard compensation.

utterances.speaker: Shirley Aldebol
utterances.text: The new local law requires private sector security guard employers to meet government contract wage standards.

utterances.speaker: Shirley Aldebol
utterances.text: What outreach is DCWP conducting to ensure that employers are aware of and prepared for these requirements before?

utterances.speaker: Shirley Aldebol
utterances.text: The effective date of the law?

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: Well, I appreciate the question, Council Member.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: We were very proud as an administration to support this bill, to support the council override.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: Not only is this going to be a critical milestone for security guards, but it's going to open the door to other potential efforts by the city to raise private sector minimum wages.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: New with respect to outreach, the minimum wage portion of the bill, the law, takes effect, I believe, January 1st.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: Our plan is to partner closely with, in particular, 32BJ to make sure that, and I know they're going to be very active in getting the word out, we want to work with them closely to get the word out.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: And we're going to be enforcing the law aggressively to make sure that even if workers might not know, we'll have partners out there who can make sure to bring violations to our attention so we can hold those employers accountable.

utterances.speaker: Shirley Aldebol
utterances.text: Do you have a sense of how many security guard employers will fall under the law?

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: I'm sorry, can you repeat that?

utterances.speaker: Shirley Aldebol
utterances.text: Do you have an idea how many security guard employers fall under the law?

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: I once knew that.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: I don't know if I have that number at hand.

utterances.speaker: Shirley Aldebol
utterances.text: I once knew it too.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: I knew it in January.

utterances.speaker: Shirley Aldebol
utterances.text: It's been a while.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: We don't have it, but we'll get back to you with that exact number.

utterances.speaker: Carlos Ortiz
utterances.text: I think approximately.

utterances.speaker: Shirley Aldebol
utterances.text: I'm sure 32BJ knows.

utterances.speaker: Carlos Ortiz
utterances.text: I think approximately the industry represented about 70,000 workers, the last number I checked, but I think this is something as we implement that will ensure that we have a full understanding of the universe of the industry.

utterances.speaker: Shirley Aldebol
utterances.text: Okay, thank you.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, great.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: I'm going to pass it back to Chair Epstein.

chapters.summary: Epstein confirms 20 new GC positions (17 lawyers, 3 investigators) and 29 OLPS positions (11 lawyers, 10 investigators, 8 data scientists), asking if this mix is appropriate for handling larger corporate defendants.
chapters.title: Epstein on detailed staffing breakdown for GC and OLPS

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: Thank you all.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: So just so I'm clear, so labor and policy standards getting 27 positions and general counsel is getting 25 positions.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: Is that right?

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: I believe it's 29 for labor and policy.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: And 20 for general counsel.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: 20 for general counsel.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: 20 for general.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: Do you want me to go through the division breakdown?

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: And what jobs are they?

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: Are they lawyers, paralegals, investigators, researchers?

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: Sure.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: So it's 20 for GC, I think.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: Lawyers.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: Only lawyers?

utterances.speaker: Michael Tiger
utterances.text: No, it's lawyers and investigators in the first fiscal year in our industry.

utterances.speaker: Michael Tiger
utterances.text: Fiscal years, there'll be additional clerical support staff.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: So how many lawyers, how many investigators?

utterances.speaker: Michael Tiger
utterances.text: The current plan is of those 2017 lawyers at different levels of seniority.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: 17 lawyers, some supervisors, I assume, in that mix, and then three investigators?

utterances.speaker: Michael Tiger
utterances.text: Yes.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: And you think that's the appropriate mix?

utterances.speaker: Michael Tiger
utterances.text: Yeah, I think this really gives us a chance to thrive as we move forward.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: What we're seeing, Councilmember, unsurprising, is we take on bigger firms like Extra Space, a publicly traded firm.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: These are serious companies with serious lawyers, and we need to be sure that in settlement negotiations, if they don't go well, we can take them to court.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: Right.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: For that, we just simply need...

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: You got a lawyer up.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: We got a lawyer up.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: And then you don't have out your projections because you just want to see how the staffing goes?

utterances.speaker: Michael Tiger
utterances.text: I'm sorry, can you say that again?

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: So you don't, because you have 20 for this year, but you don't have projections what you're going to do next year?

utterances.speaker: Michael Tiger
utterances.text: I mean, I think we do have a sense.

utterances.speaker: Michael Tiger
utterances.text: I'm a little hesitant because, obviously, as the year progresses, there may be decisions made to move things around.

utterances.speaker: Michael Tiger
utterances.text: So I think we do have a general sense.

utterances.speaker: Michael Tiger
utterances.text: I don't think that's necessarily ready for discussion because I don't want to, like, houseify it right now.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: And then Office of Labor Policy and Standards, you said, what's 29, 10 investigators, we said?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Right.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So I've written it down since you asked me the question.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So we've got 11 lawyers.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: There's 10 investigators and eight data scientists, and that includes the supervisory staff in each category.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: Great.

chapters.summary: Epstein worries about backlogs persisting after hiring, given 10,000 potential Uber/Lyft deactivations, 2,000 new street vendor licenses, and other new laws. Levine acknowledges the challenge of rising complaints but says they've accounted for it.
chapters.title: Epstein on backlog concerns with upcoming mandates

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: And you think both sections of the department, that is going to get you in a sufficient place to deal with any backlog and all these future issues that are coming forward?

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: We have 10,000 potential Uber lift deactivations coming down the pike.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: We are having 2,000 new potential street vendor licenses, not to mention laundry workers, not to mention security guards, not to mention every other thing that we're going to do.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: You know, I know Councilman come on talked about last mile, you know, there's like things that are coming that might be coming forward.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: I want to be clear that do you think there will not be a backlog when consumers and workers are coming to your office?

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: We're not going to tell them wait six months now when we're staffed up.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: That's our projection at this point, Chair.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: I'll say those numbers we just rattled off are, of course, for FY27.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: There are additional infusions for all of my colleagues here in FY28 and then further in FY29.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: But again, this is going to be...

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: We're going to be continuously evaluating this.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: If delivery protection passes, as I hope it does, we're going to have additional needs for lines.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: We're going to be monitoring the student loan marketplace.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: We may need additional OTPS for outreach there.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: So we're going to be monitoring the student loan marketplace.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: We're monitoring this.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: So we're going to be monitoring the student loan marketplace.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: We could see a big spike, an unexpected spike.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: So we're going to be monitoring the student loan marketplace.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: You know, we've already seen an increase in worker complaints in 2026.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: So we're going to be monitoring the student loan marketplace.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: But again, this is going to be...

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: That is going to make it harder to close the backlog.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: But again, we have accounted for that in this budget.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: We're going to be continuously evaluating this.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: But we have to be monitoring our complaints, as we will be, and we have to be monitoring our backlog, as we are, and of course monitoring the new laws that are coming online.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: If delivery protection passes, as I hope it does, we're going to have additional needs for lines.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: We're going to be monitoring the student loan marketplace.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: We may need additional OTPS for outreach there.

chapters.summary: Epstein asks about Instacart's temporary exemption ending in July 2026. Wagoner confirms they're already monitoring Instacart's wage payments and will require full on-call time payment, with no full exemption granted.
chapters.title: Epstein on Instacart grocery delivery compliance for July

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: Can you just update us?

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: You know, I mean, obviously I'm pushing to give you more money, so here you go.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: So we're going to be monitoring the student loan marketplace.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: But I would just be concerned that we get past the budget and then we see in the summer or in the fall that you've hired up and there still is a backlog.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: So we're going to be monitoring the student loan marketplace.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: You know, in January there's a backlog because you've hired up and you've trained people.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: But again, this is going to be...

utterances.text: We may need additional OTPS for outreach there.

utterances.text: And again, it's a little bit like going uphill because the complaints are rising.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine

utterances.text: So we need to hire faster than the complaints are rising and deal with the backlog.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: But again, this is going to be continuously evaluating this.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: And with respect to certain laws, like deactivation, as you mentioned, there's going to be a look-back period.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: If delivery protection passes, as I hope it does, we're going to have additional needs for lines.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: So the thousands of workers who were deactivated over the last six years, six years are going to be able to have those six or five, seven, seven years are going to be able to have those deactivations revisited.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: We're going to be monitoring the student loan marketplace.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: So we're going to be monitoring the student loan marketplace.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: So we're going to be monitoring the student loan marketplace.

utterances.speaker: Sam Levine
utterances.text: So we're going to be monitoring the student loan marketplace.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: So we want to understand the impact on affordable and rent-stabilized housing, and so we're looking forward to hearing your testimony in that regard.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: So once again, I want to thank the chairs for their leadership and the EDC team for being here.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: being here and we look forward to a very productive hearing.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: So let me pass it back to our finance chair.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Great, thank you.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: And I will pass it to Chair Maloney.

chapters.summary: Maloney highlights the $100M insurance investment, notes the 16.8% decrease in the five-year capital plan, and asks about the $30M for city-backed grocery stores including site selection and operating criteria.
chapters.title: Chair Maloney on insurance, capital plan, and grocery stores

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: I'm honored to chair the committee, Council's Committee on Economic Development and to be joined by our finance chair, Lee, as well as Speaker Menon for this important conversation.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: We're going to be hearing today from the interim president of NYC EDC on the fiscal 2027 executive budget, including the executive capital commitment plan.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: As we move towards budget adoption, it's important that EDC's investments reflect its core priorities of growing innovation sectors, delivering sustainable communities, and strengthening the city's future economy.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: This committee looks forward to discussing how the executive budget is going to advance those goals and how the investments will translate into tangible benefits for New Yorkers.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: The largest investment included in this plan is for the city-backed insurance program, supported by $20 million in fiscal year 2027, increasing to $40 million in 2028 and 2029, totaling $100 million over three years.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: For too long, affordable housing developers and providers have been constricted by rising insurance costs, with insurance premiums having more than doubled since 2017.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: So this investment targets one of the highest operating costs for developers and comes at a critical time.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: We look forward to hearing more details about the program.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: how it will be structured to drive premiums down.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: The executive capital commitment plan covers fiscals 2026 to 2030 and includes $3.5 billion for EDC.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: That's a 16.8% decrease from the fiscal year 2027 preliminary capital commitment plan we discussed in March.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: That plan has a 10-year commitment and includes $7.1 billion, only a small increase of $4.8 million from the preliminary plan.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: So this change reflects a stretching of funds that were originally allocated within the next five years out to the 10-year plan,

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: and we're interested in hearing why these timelines have changed, whether any critical projects that EDC funds like Hunts Point Produce Market or Inwood Greenway will be impacted by those changes.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: And third, the plan includes the $30 million allocated for city-backed grocery stores.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: Since our first hearing, two of the five sites have now been identified, and we'd like a lot more detail on how these stores will operate, the criteria for site selection, the timeline for getting these up and running,

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: and how they will balance minimizing taxpayer subsidy while delivering on the promise of making essential household groceries more affordable.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: I want to thank the interim president, Jenny Pack, for being here, as well as the chief of staff, Jen Montalvo, as well as the rest of EDC.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: staff for coming here and testifying.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: And I want to thank everyone who's been incredibly responsive to our many requests.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: We would not have been able to analyze the city's budget at such a detailed level without EDC's partnership.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: So I commend your leadership in this interim period.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: And I also want to thank the finance staff, Spencer Kuhn,

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: Hector German, Daniel Krupp, Emre Edev, Chima Obichere

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: for their work preparing for this hearing, and the committee staff, Alex Polonov, William Hong-Yank for their support, as well as my own staff that put in a lot of effort, Sophie Secor, Isabella Soldano,

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: and Kaveri Kutagiri for all their hard work to prepare, and I'll now defer back to the finance chair.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And before we begin the testimony, just wanted to remind everyone that for this year's budget hearings, we're doing all the public testimony on the same day on Wednesday, June 10th, beginning at 930.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So please make sure to sign up for the public testimony portion, which again is Wednesday, June 10th, beginning at 930.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And I will now turn it over to Brian Sarfo, our committee counsel, who I would also love to thank for all of his hard work sitting here and keeping me in check.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Take it away.

chapters.summary: Committee counsel swears in Interim President Jeanne Peck before testimony begins.
chapters.title: Swearing in of EDC witnesses

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Good afternoon.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Do you affirm to tell the truth, the whole truth, and nothing but the truth before this committee, and to respond honestly to council member questions, Commissioner Pock?

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: And, oh, you may begin.

chapters.summary: Peck describes EDC reimagining economic development to expand the economic pie for all New Yorkers, with a focus on elevating working people historically left behind.
chapters.title: Peck on EDC's economic development vision under Mamdani

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: Thank you very much.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: Good afternoon, Speaker Menon, Chair Lee, Chairman Loney, and members of the Economic Development and Finance Committees.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: Well, we are back here again, and I'm still here.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: My name is Jeanne Peck, and I continue to serve as the interim president and CEO of New York City Economic Development Corporation, as well as the chief financial officer.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: I am here with my Chief of Staff, Jennifer Montavo.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: And I'd just like to begin to say in the two months since I last testified before you at the preliminary budget hearing, EDC has continued advancing our work to make our city more affordable and livable.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: Together with the Mayor and Deputy Mayor Julie Hsu, EDC is reimagining economic development with a focus on economic growth

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: that both expands economic pie for all New Yorkers and ensures the pie includes and elevates working people who have previously and historically been left behind.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: Before I jump into our projects, I want to zoom out quickly and give a sense of where our economy is.

chapters.summary: Peck reports labor force participation at all-time high, commercial leases at strongest since 2019, VC investment strongest since 2021, and 2.1 million residents aged 18-34, while acknowledging affordability challenges.
chapters.title: Peck on NYC economy resilience despite headwinds

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: Despite continued national and international headwinds, New York City's economy remains resilient.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: Labor force participation is at an all-time high.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: Real estate and citywide vacancies continues to improve.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: Venture capital.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: investment in our city reached its strongest quarter in five years.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: And the city continues to attract more college graduates, with one in four New Yorkers, or 2.1 million residents, who live in New York City between the ages of 18 and 34.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: Yet, we know we are continuing

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: to face real challenges as well as the increasing pressure of affordability.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: We know that far too many New Yorkers feel the city they love is becoming more and more unaffordable.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: For this reason, EDC takes a forward-looking approach to economic growth and development.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: We will continue to use every tool in our toolbox to build a more economically just New York City.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: Today, I'll use my time to share a handful of updates on work that has seen significant progress since March.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: These initiatives are advancing mayoral priorities on affordability.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: And represent major wins for the city and working people.

chapters.summary: Peck announces La Marqueta in East Harlem as the first NYC groceries site, a 9,000 square foot store opening in 2029, built with community input to offer affordable food to working-class New Yorkers.
chapters.title: Peck on NYC public groceries initiative and La Marqueta site

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: Public groceries.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: Mayor Mumdani has been clear, the working people of this city should be able to afford the basic needs and services this city has to offer.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: And that includes healthy food and groceries.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: Today, as a result of rising costs of rent,

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: Gas, bills, and food, more and more New Yorkers can't afford everyday healthy groceries.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: Making sure working people can buy fresh, affordable groceries is a key part of the mayor's affordability agenda.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: March, I shared that EDC was working closely with the administration on the public groceries initiative to increase food access to fresh, healthy food at a price New Yorkers can afford.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: That work has continued and intensified and has led to two major announcements to date with concrete milestones toward realizing this vision.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: In April, I stood with the mayor in East Harlem as he announced La Marqueta as the first site for NYC groceries.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: As one of the city's six public markets operated by EDC, La Marqueta supports more than 20 small businesses and 120 workers, including restaurants, art vendors, and community organizations.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: Now as a future Manhattan site for NYC groceries, La Marqueta will further benefit the East Harlem community by offering affordable food to working class New Yorkers.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: The 9,000 square foot store will be constructed from the ground up with a significant community outreach and input to ensure it reflects the diversity of diverse cultures of El Barrio and is expected to open in 2029.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: And just last Monday, I joined the mayor again, this time in Hunts Point in the Bronx, to announce the second NYC grocery store.

chapters.summary: Peck announces the second NYC grocery store at the Peninsula in Hunts Point, a 20,000 square foot store opening by end of 2027, plus a new online portal for private property owners to recommend sites.
chapters.title: Peck on Peninsula grocery store and private site portal

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: This one will be at the Peninsula, the redeveloped site of the former Sfofford Juvenile Detention Center.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: EDC is leading the transformation of the site into a campus of dignified living.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: A place where working broad sites can afford to live

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: work, and play.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: The Peninsula development includes 740 units of 100% affordable housing, a child care center,

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: 30,000 square feet for commercial light industrial businesses providing good paying jobs, and over 50,000 square feet for community, cultural, and recreational open space.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: With this new NYC grocery store, they will now have access to healthy, affordable food right in their backyard.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: This 20,000 square foot store will be the first NYC groceries site to open, expected by the end of 2027.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: Along with the peninsula, the mayor also announced an online portal for private property owners to recommend sites for future NYC grocery stores in Brooklyn, Queens, and Staten Island.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: The portal, created by EDC, will support early identification and screening of potential sites and inform next steps and timeline for the three other NYC groceries locations.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: I'm incredibly proud of EDC's work on the NYC Groceries Initiative.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: This is what public investment should look like, making sure that benefits and the prosperity of our city reach working people who need it most.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: Beyond our long-term stewardship and advancement of the sites where these two stores will be located, our team has been working tirelessly to develop this initiative and ensure that the basic needs of working people and families are met.

chapters.summary: Peck describes the $4M modular restroom pilot program RFP and EDC's view of housing as essential economic infrastructure, with insurance costs tripling since 2017 driving the need for the city-backed insurance program.
chapters.title: Peck on public restrooms and housing affordability

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: Public restrooms.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: In March, I shared that EDC had released the RFP for modular restrooms across

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: The city to expand access to public bathrooms, one of the mayor's first 100 days commitments.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: This $4 million pilot program will make sure that more New Yorkers will have more access to public bathrooms.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: Bathrooms are basic public infrastructure and access to them should be easier.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: We anticipate an update on the RFP soon.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: Housing.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: EDC views housing production as an essential economic infrastructure.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: Affordable housing enables workers and New Yorkers to live near jobs, to raise their families, build roots in the community.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: It supports economic mobility and underpins the neighborhood vitality across the five boroughs.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: But today, New York City is facing an existential affordability and housing crisis.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: As the mayor has said, New York cannot remain a city of opportunity if the people who make this city run are priced out of it.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: At EDC, we're advancing affordable housing development across the city, and that includes going after the structural costs that are driving up the price of housing.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: Insurance is one of them, with the price of insurance tripling since 2017.

chapters.summary: Peck details the insurance program partnering with HTC and HPD, aiming to issue policies for 20,000 units next year and 100,000 by 2030, with expected 5-7x return in city capital subsidy savings.
chapters.title: Peck on city-backed insurance program details

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: This April, we announced the first city-backed insurance program to help New Yorkers stay in their homes, give building owners the support they need to make repairs, and building affordable housing more affordable.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: The insurance program will reduce the cost of property and liability insurance for affordable and rent-stabilized housing.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: EDC is partnering with HTC and HPD to develop and execute the insurance program, which aims to

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: aims to issue new insurance policies for 20,000 units of housing next year and 100,000 homes by 2030.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: Additionally, the city expects a five to seven times return measured in city capital subsidy savings.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: Lower insurance costs help to increase property level income, which supports larger property level loans and reduces the subsidy capital required in new construction and preservation deals.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: This program uses the city's purchasing power to lower insurance premiums for affordable housing and rent-stabilized apartments, helping our investments reach more New Yorkers and is a key part of the city's housing plan.

chapters.summary: Peck describes EDC's World Cup role including fan fests, watch parties at public markets, Dress the City campaign, and five borough-wrapped NYC ferries showcasing the city's energy and diversity.
chapters.title: Peck on World Cup preparations and NYC Ferry wrapping

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: World Cup.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: Finally, in just 13 days, our city will welcome the world for the 2016 FIFA World Cup.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: As I am sure you have all seen, the mayor has been making a number of announcements to activate our city during the 39-day tournament, ensuring working people, New Yorkers, and tourists alike enjoy all that the five boroughs have to offer.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: And EDC continues to play a big role from free fan fests, watch parties, and fan zones at our public markets on our biggest campuses, including the already announced Staten Island University

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: Hospital Community Park and more locations to be announced very soon, to the Dress the City campaign that will show off host cities'soccer jerseys, inspiring New Yorkers to get into the game.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: EDC is working tirelessly to ensure the World Cup

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: up Kix goals for working people this summer.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: I know some of you are ferry riders and I hope you all get the chance to see five wrapped NYC ferries, which we unveiled last week, each with a borough specific wrapping showcasing the energy, diversity, and pride of our city.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: They will be on our harbor this summer as part of the World Cup festivities.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: The World Cup is a tremendous opportunity for us to promote New York City as a place to live, visit, and conduct business.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: As I said in March, this is a rare moment to show a global audience of tourists, companies, investors, and delegations that there's no better place to do business than New York City.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: As I mentioned, these are just a couple of EDC's most notable highlights.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: Across all of our work, we are proud of our partnership with the City Council to improve the lives of our neighbors, increase affordability, expand opportunity, and ensure New York City remains a global hub of business, culture, and innovation.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: I want to thank you for the opportunity to speak with you today, and we welcome any questions you have.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Really quickly want to recognize we've been joined by Council Members Riley, Council Member Murano on Zoom, Council Member Farias, and Council Member Epstein.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And Speaker Minin, go ahead with your question.

chapters.summary: Menin expresses concern about job loss and asks for EDC's plan. Peck cites labor participation highs, commercial lease growth, and VC investment strength, noting NYC added 28,000 jobs year over year.
chapters.title: Menin presses on job creation and retention strategy

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Okay, thank you very much for the testimony.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: I mean, I really want to start with something that I referred to in my opening statement, which is I'm deeply concerned about the job loss in New York City from the past year.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: And in this testimony, I didn't hear any

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: about job creation at all?

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: What is EDC's plan to focus on job creation and job retention?

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: So it's not only about creating new jobs, it's also making sure that we're retaining the jobs that currently exist in the city.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
