utterances.text: Yeah, thank you for that question.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: We understand the headwinds that's happening nationally and globally, and New York City is affected as all the other major cities in the United States.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: But we do know that New York City is resilient.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: And from 9-11 to the financial crisis to coming out of COVID, it has proven to be an incredibly resilient city, primarily because this is New York City and we have the best talent in the world and the greatest infrastructure.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: We are comforted by a few statistics that I'll share with you.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: We have an economic group that puts out a monthly snapshot.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: It came out today, so hopefully you'll be able to see it.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: But a couple of things are labor participation is an all-time high.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: Our commercial leases have had the highest two consecutive quarters since 2019.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: VC investments are the highest since 2021.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: Our unemployment rate is fairly low at 5.6%, although BIPOC is higher at 7.3%, so that's something that we are committed to looking at and addressing.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: And tourism, transit ridership, all are doing well.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: Why is it?

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: I mean, we have the top talent of, top destination for talent.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: We have over a million of college student and recent college graduates, which are an incredible pool.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: Year over year, New York City actually has increased jobs by 28,000 and 5,000 since in 2026 year to date.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: So this is more than other comparable metros such as Miami, D.C., Boston that actually have had decreased jobs and New York City has increased jobs.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: This is on par with like Dallas.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: So we're not doing worse than Dallas.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: We're doing about similar Dallas, but we're doing a lot better in terms of jobs from our comparable cities.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: You know, what is EDC doing?

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: We believe New York City is an innovation economy.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: We have a dedicated business development group that works closely with business generators, the people who are interested in coming, as well as businesses that are thinking of growing here in New York City.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: We want to retain.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: Like you said, we want to retain companies, so Amex committing to 10,000 jobs in the World Trade Center, Anthropic doubling their size.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: These are all great commitments, and it's a proof to New York City is a great place to thrive.

chapters.summary: Menin warns about AI job displacement, media/entertainment losses to other states, and Texas surpassing NYC in financial sector jobs, urging EDC to develop retention plans. Peck describes dedicated business development and innovation teams.
chapters.title: Menin on business retention and media/entertainment job loss

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Okay, I just have a couple responses on that, because you referred to 9-11, and certainly after 9-11, and I served for many years on the board of the Lower Manhattan Redevelopment Corporation, we launched...

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: specific programs to retain businesses.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: I mean, that is why many businesses ended up staying in Lower Manhattan or moving to Lower Manhattan.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: My concern is that, and particularly with what is happening around AI, we're going to see increased job displacement.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: And if we do not put in plans in place right now to retain companies here and to also attract new companies, I mean, I'm very concerned about that.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: I'm concerned around media and entertainment.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: That you have job loss to other states.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: And we're seeing in the financial sector where there are now more jobs in the financial sector in the state of Texas than there are in New York.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: That's truly shocking.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: We used to have triple the number not that long ago.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: And so again, I'm just urging EDC

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: to start putting in place those plans to make sure that we are fully prepared to retain these businesses and to attract new businesses.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: And the last thing I will mention is, you know, you talked about tourism.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: The tourism numbers are not great.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: We know that Broadway year to year is down.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Hotel occupancy year to year is down.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: So I just...

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: We see all the warning signs, and it's incumbent upon us as government then to put in plans as place to deal with that.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: Yeah, we appreciate that.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: We appreciate your observations there.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: And we are working hard every day.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: Again, I think we have a dedicated business development team.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: We also have an innovations in it.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: industry group that focus on innovative

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: You know, as like New York City, while it has its stable businesses such as finance and tech, it is also an innovations economy.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: So we want to make sure that we're growing like the next generation of companies.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: And we're doing that especially with green economy, tech, AI, life science, et cetera.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: So we are committed to growing that.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: And also, I think what is really clear to us is that we want to make sure that these are good paying jobs and that they're accessing people who normally don't have access to these jobs.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: Part of that is through a lot of the programs that we have, like the Founder Fellowship.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: We also have a lot of sort of industrial-related construction programs that ensures that other

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: people who, small businesses who have not been part of these large construction jobs, such as Construct NYC, Waterfront Pathways is another program that we have that makes sure that our maritime work

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: addresses a lot of new companies coming into the industry.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: Because industries are always and constantly changing.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: So we are focused on that.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: And I guess the other piece of this is the affordability piece, that part of economic growth is ensuring that the people that live and work here can afford New York City and that they stay here and that they're not going to leave New York City because it's too expensive.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: And so we're very focused, as you may imagine, on the affordability part of it.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: And let me just be clear.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: We totally agree with that.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: We are all in on building more affordable housing.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: It's one of the reasons we announced our small lots program to build 35,000 new units of housing.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: And that sort of gets to this issue.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: I know there are several topics that we all want to ask about.

chapters.summary: Menin asks how the two grocery sites were selected and how many competing businesses are nearby. Peck explains sites were prioritized based on public ownership, food access metrics, and development readiness.
chapters.title: Menin on grocery site selection and impact on bodegas

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: On the affordable groceries plan, to be clear, as I said in my testimony, we completely agree that grocery prices are extremely high and that we have to figure out what we can do about that, which is why

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: we're very focused on funding food pantries.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: We're very focused on funding a number of organizations that are currently doing a lot of work in this space in New York.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: As I mentioned, we're focused on banning surveillance pricing, dynamic pricing, so we're getting sort of to the root causes across the city that are causing for rising prices.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: We're focused on creating an insurance accountability office to lower insurance costs for small businesses, grocery stores, and others that are, again, paying four to five times the national average on insurance.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: So I guess one of the questions I have about the...

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: The administration's plan is how were these various, the two sites, how were they selected and how many competing businesses, grocery stores, bodegas are in those surrounding areas of the two sites that have been selected?

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: And what will be the impact on them?

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: Thank you for that question and we appreciate all that you're doing for the, you know, helping the food affordability.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: It is a crisis here in New York City and, you know, the NYC Grocery is just one lever that we're using to reach the same goal, which is making food more affordable.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: NYC Groceries is focused on fresh, healthy foods and everyday groceries.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: groceries to be affordable.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: We're not going to, it's going to be a regular grocery store, but we're not going to discount everything.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: So we're focused on fresh, healthy foods and everyday groceries.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: How we selected the sites is we prioritized public sites.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: That was first and foremost.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: And so we did an inventory of all the public sites.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: We also wanted to make sure that from the city's measure that it was, did not have enough grocery stores per capita.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: There's a specific DCP measure.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: And we also wanted to make sure that it was going to be able to be constructed and online fairly quickly.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: It wasn't going to take, you know, a decade.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: So we were committed to opening all five stores by the end of 2029, with the first one opening by the end of 2027.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: So the first site that we announced, which will be opened by 2029, is La Maqueta.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: That is a city site.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: And if you've been up there, it has a public market there.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: It has other sort of, it's like an eight block.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: Site which has all different uses and one of it and it's being renovated and reimagined and one of it is an empty lot and that empty lot was going to be a farmers market so there was going to be city dollars are going into that to convert it to a farmers market and

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: through our discussions we

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: we realized that this would be a great place for NYC groceries.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: So this peninsula site is, and so that's a ground-up construction, so that's very specific, and that's why it's not going to be open until 2029.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: The peninsula, as you know, is the former spot for juvenile detention center.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: It's a development.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: that EDC and the city's been working on for some time.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: It's gonna have 740 units of housing.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: A lot of it's gonna be family housing.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: It's in three phases, and we're pretty much through phase one.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: And about to launch phase two, but phase three, contemplated, which is not gonna happen for several years, was gonna have a grocery store.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: It was required to have a grocery store.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: So there's a real shortage of grocery stores up there.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: There's not really any grocery store really close by.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: So we decided that we were gonna accelerate that grocery store that was gonna be

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: online for constructed in phase three and bring it to today and that's and it was also a space that was ready to be tended to fit out into a grocery store sort of like poised for a kind of a

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: perfect location and structure for a grocery store and so we selected that one as the second site that we announced but it will be the first one open by the end of 2027.

chapters.summary: Menin asks how groceries will be priced and what's in the core basket. Peck explains a third-party operator will run stores normally, with the city covering tenant fit-out, rent, and property tax to subsidize a core basket of fresh foods.
chapters.title: Menin on grocery pricing model and basket of goods

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: How much are the groceries going to cost?

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: Well, the grocery, you mean in terms of the discount?

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Yeah.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: Okay, so how we're doing this is that we're going to, in the summer, we're going to put out an RFI for a third-party operator, city grocery operator.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: And through that structure, they're going to be operating the grocery store like a normal grocery store.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: And we're going to have in that RFI.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: A core basket of goods that will be discounted.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: What we're offering the operator is the city paying the tenant fit out.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: So the substantial cost of the grocery operator would normally have to pay.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: They are not going to have to pay rent and then

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: Property tax.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: So we're going to ensure that that subsidy directly goes to the food affordability of those core baskets.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: And then we're going to work out all the details.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: Once the RFE comes out, you'll see some of the details.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: And then ongoing negotiations with the city operator.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: It might be one.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: It might be multiple.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: A couple questions.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: So what are the basket of goods that will be covered?

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: What's included in that?

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: So we're still working that out, but the focus is fresh, healthy foods and everyday groceries.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: So think bread, milk, meat, chicken, eggs.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: And how...

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: Produce.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Given that...

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: I understand the concept of they're going to not have to pay rent and there'll be reduced costs, but that given that they're not going to be able to volume purchase, which other grocery stores are able to, how will these

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: government-run stores be able to drive the cost down in that regard, on the purchasing side?

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: So these are going to be third-party operators that already have grocery stores that they run.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: So whatever model that they have to operate, they will still be operating through that model.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: It's not going to be like a separate model.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Understood.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: And then how many New Yorkers will they be able to reach?

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Like, in other words, the Hunts Point and the La Maricata site, what does EDC's projections say in terms of the reach of each of the respective stores?

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: I'm sorry, can you say that, Council?

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: Yes, yeah.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: So for the Hunts Point and the La Maricata site, what is the...

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: the reach that EDC is estimating.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: I mean, how many New Yorkers are going to be able to shop there, purchase a basket of good, like what is the actual reach?

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: Yeah, we're not gonna limit it.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: It's going to be like a regular grocery store so people can come and shop.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: And so it'll be people for the neighborhood, but people who are coming from other places as well.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: We want to increase the foot traffic because as you said, you know, we want the neighborhood to thrive through the,

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: New York City grocery store being located there.

chapters.summary: Menin asks about EDC's plans to support pre-existing bodegas. Peck describes outreach to bodega associations, meetings with local owners, and notes the core basket won't overlap with high-profit bodega items like lottery tickets or alcohol.
chapters.title: Menin on supporting existing bodegas near new grocery stores

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: And what is EDC's plans to support pre-existing bodegas that are in the area, small grocery stores that have been there for years?

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: How is EDC going to support those businesses so they're not driven out of business?

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: Yeah, so as I mentioned that the

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: that we did select neighborhoods that really had a shortage of grocery by cities measures.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: So, because part of it is food, part of the goal is food affordability as well as food accessibility.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: So before we announced the grocery store initiative,

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: A few months ago, we did reach out to bodega associations and the National Supermarket Association to inform them of our initial plan.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: Once each site has been announced, La Marqueta and then now Peninsula, we have met with the bodega owners in the area.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: And we'll continue, and same thing is happening with Peninsula.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: And we will continue to do that.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: During the

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: of the grocery store initiative when it opens and throughout the operation.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: So we really want to work with the bodega owners to make sure.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: I would say that, again, this is a regular grocery store, but the core basket of goods is going to be set and limited.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: It's not really, some of the stuff that the bodega owners offer, we're not going to have.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: And some of the largest profiting items we're not going to have discounted.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: So I think that there's going to be, we're hoping that we can even like direct traffic to bodega owners with more foot traffic because there's not going to be as much of an overlap as people might think.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: But we are going to find ways to try to support them, and we're going to work through that.

chapters.summary: Menin asks why EDC administers the contract if HPD manages the program. Peck explains the three-way partnership: HPD has housing stock, HTC handles bond finance, and EDC brings transactional expertise.
chapters.title: Menin on city-backed insurance program structure and benefits

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Okay, I know other members have a bunch of questions on that, so I'm going to move topics to city-backed insurance.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: So I just want to better understand, because we're very interested in the issue of insurance and lowering insurance costs.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: This is incredibly important.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: As I mentioned in the opening statement, we're creating an insurance accountability office to try to drive down the price of insurance, whether it be for homeowners, renters, small businesses.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: So my understanding is that the insurance program that was announced will be run and managed by HPD.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Can you explain what the benefit is in having EDC administer the contract, but you're not managing the program?

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: Yeah, thank you for that question.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: This has been...

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: A program that we've been working with HPD and HTC for actually a few years now, and so we're really excited that this is now happening and funded.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: So the relationship, it's a three-way effort, and we're working very close with HPD and HTC.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: HPD as because they have the housing stock and they are building affordable housing.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: HTC because they're the bond finance arm for the affordable housing.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: And EDC is involved because we have a lot of investment and transactional expertise through our strategic investments group.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: And so this is kind of what they do every day.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: And so it was a really good synergy.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: So we're actually going to be doing the sort of transacting and executing of the insurance program.

chapters.summary: Menin asks how 20,000 homes by 2027 and 100,000 by 2030 were calculated, and what happens after FY29 funding ends. Peck explains the investment continues as a captive fund and hopes to attract additional investors.
chapters.title: Menin on insurance funding methodology and post-2029 continuity

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: And what methodology is being utilized to determine the appropriate funding level for the program?

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: So 100 million has been allocated.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: It's going to be rolled out and scaled up, starting in 20 million and then 40 and 40 in the subsequent years.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: And so we think that that is going to provide

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: about 20,000 units in the first year and over 100, about 100,000 by 2030.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: We are also hoping, again, the city is going to be like an investor and we're hoping it might attract other investors and so we can scale that up as time goes on.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: So it's going to be an insurance provider, but that doesn't

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: I mean, it's like a closed investment.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: We're hoping other investments come in so we can scale it up.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Okay, and two last questions, and I'm going to pass it over to Chair Lee.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: So for the program, how is it determined that the program will be able to provide assistance for 20,000 homes by 2027 and 100,000 by 2030?

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: So I can get back to the actual calculation, but the idea is that insurance costs have ballooned like 20 to 30% over the last few years, and

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: we analyzed it, and it's largely due to overhead and profit, and also

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: you know, EDC in the city has the lowest cost of capital.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: So all those things combined allow us to offer lower premiums.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: So we estimated that it can do 20,000 units and then 100,000.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: What that will also do is provide savings, so to speak, for the HPD subsidy for five to seven times because it's going to be less costly to bring this affordable housing

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: to build affordable housing, you'll need less city subsidy for those HPD sites.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Okay, and then lastly, the program is only funded through fiscal 2029.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: So what happens to homes that are covered under the insurance program after that time?

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: So this is just, this is like an investment.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: So it'll continue on.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: And again, we're hoping that through this investment, we'll attract other residents.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: It'll scale.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: But it's not like it's going to be depleted.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: It's serving there as like a captive, you know?

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: So it's there.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: The principle is not being used.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: And in fact, you know, we're hoping there's going to be some return.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Okay, thank you.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: I'll pass it over to Chair Lee.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Great.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Thank you, Speaker.

chapters.summary: Lee asks about $2.1M savings from terminating a marketing contract. Peck explains they brought expertise in-house using existing marketing and business development teams, with targeted LinkedIn outreach.
chapters.title: Lee on marketing contract savings and in-house staffing

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Really quickly, just going into some of the savings, because I know that in FY27's executive plan, it included savings of about $2.1 million over the next four years.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And this is largely, I think, from the termination of a marketing contract in favor of doing the work in-house versus external.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And so a lot of the marketing contract, I believe, was focused on business attraction and development through programs such as REAP, which is Relocation and Employment Assistance, and RACE, which is the

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Relocation Assistance Credit for Employees.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And these businesses, you know, the programs can help attract businesses and employees through the relocation incentives.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And so just wanted to know.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Who was it contracted with and if in-house are you hiring additional staff to do that and why did EDC determine that the marketing work could be done in-house versus externally?

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: Yeah, thanks for that question.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: I think, you know, we have a whole marketing team and external affairs team as well as a business development team.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: And so I think we thought that the expertise from the marketing contract.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: that we had, we sort of understood what we needed to do and we could bring it in-house.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: We also have like a lot of, as everyone is relying more on social media, so there's a lot of activity related to LinkedIn especially and other avenues to get the word out there.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: But in terms of those specific incentives, we have very targeted outreach through our biz dev team.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: For companies that have shown interest or companies that we have partnerships with these generators that generate interest in bringing businesses to New York City.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, so I'm assuming most of that is going to be towards staffing.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Is any ad buys included?

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: Say it again.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Most of it's probably going to staffing, I'm assuming.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And are there any ad buys?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And also, I'm assuming multiple languages, hopefully, because we know our businesses in New York are very diverse in terms of the languages spoken.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: Yeah, a lot of it's going in-house.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: We do still have a marketing budget, and we do buy specific ads through LinkedIn or paid LinkedIn posts, I guess, to reach a larger audience.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: And I think specifically, I'll have to get back to you about the multiple languages.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: But we appreciate that.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: I think that's a great idea.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: No, only because just from my former work in the nonprofit sector, the language is very important and key.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And I know that there's a lot of, you know, for example, even local media.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: that a lot of folks pay attention to and may spread the word through their friends and family networks that way to say, hey, there's this program out there.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So just wanted to put that out there.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: Yeah, that's great.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: Thank you.

chapters.summary: Lee asks if EDC tracks small business numbers and analyzes REAP and RACE incentive efficacy. Peck says they track usage and analyze results through their strategy group, offering to share data.
chapters.title: Lee on tracking small businesses and incentive program efficacy

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: May 2024 report titled New York City Small Business Recovery, Patterns of Growth in a Changing Economy, showed that at the time, New York State had over 183

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: thousand small businesses more than ever before and showed substantial recovery from the drop seen during COVID.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And over the last two years, economies have changed drastically on the local, national, and global levels.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And so do you track the number of small businesses located in the city?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: If not, is that something that the corporation is willing to track?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And if yes, what is the current number of small businesses and what are the trends that you're seeing?

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: I'll have to get back to you if we track those small businesses.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: And if we don't, we can look into tracking them.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: We do work very closely with SBS as well as we are very focused on small business.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: They're sort of the backbone of the economy here.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: And they are good work, you know, paying jobs for people.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: And what we do is we have programs to promote small business and support small businesses, but we also use our assets to tenant out for small businesses specifically.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: So, for example, for Brooklyn Army Terminal, many of those small business are manufacturing and industrial companies.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: And we...

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: are very focused on ensuring that we provide them tenant space.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, great.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And just my last questions around revenue.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Do you conduct any analysis on the efficacy of incentive programs like race and rebond tax revenue for the city?

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: We do track, I think, the usage.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: I have to go back to you exactly, but we do track on sort of, like, we look backwards and see, like, how many companies use Raise a Reap,

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: where they, what those companies were, to see if people are using it and what they're using it for and where it's, like, successful or not.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: Yes, we have a strategy group that does all of that analysis.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, and have you considered expanding existing marketing, business attraction for these programs?

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: So we were involved in sort of advising City Hall on sort of potential incentive programs, and so we'll continue to play that role.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: We haven't done it, like, recently, but we continue to play that role with City Hall.

chapters.summary: Lee raises concerns about REAP being an as-of-right program that could cost billions for large companies like Amazon. Peck notes EDC provides analysis but doesn't make policy decisions on as-of-right incentives.
chapters.title: Lee on REAP open-ended tax breaks and Amazon HQ2 concerns

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, and then do the businesses tend to be smaller, larger in size, that benefit from REAP and RACE?

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: I think it's smaller to medium.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Smaller to medium.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: I think it's on a case-by-case basis.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And the main city-funded subsidy, for example, for Amazon HQ2 was the REAP program, and it would have cost the city up to $1.4 billion in tax subsidy.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Yeah, that's the exception.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Yeah.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Since REAP is an as-of-right program, the city would not have had the authority to limit or condition the use of...

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: of this tax break by Amazon or any other large company.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So does EDC think an open-ended tax break like that is wise use?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Are you looking into that or any of the other programs?

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: Again, I think we provide the statistics and look at it.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: And when we offer that up, it's not in our sort of decision-making power to decide on that, but we do analyze it and provide.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: As you know, we have IDA, which is a discretionary tax incentive program, and so we really look at those very specifically and analyze those to see that it's making sense for the communities in the city.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: No, that's great.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: I love data.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So anything you guys have to inform that would be wonderful.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: We'd be happy to share.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: And I'll get back to you on the size of the companies because I might have.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: I'm not really sure.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: I think it varies.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, perfect.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Chair Maloney, I'm passing it over to you.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: Excellent.

chapters.summary: Maloney asks about new programs for economic uncertainty and raises concerns about the lack of a permanent EDC president six months after the former CEO's departure. Peck says operations continue uninterrupted under her interim leadership.
chapters.title: Maloney on market uncertainty and permanent EDC president vacancy

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: Thank you so much, Chair Lee.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: Thank you for your testimony and for the work that you have been carrying forward over the past several months.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: We spoke about some of the economic trends and the import of investment in programs

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: like race and REAP that are helping to bring businesses and keep employers here in New York.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: How are you?

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: Looking at market uncertainty and thinking about new programs in this economic environment to address some of the concerns that Speaker Menon raised about

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: creating jobs here in New York?

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: Yeah, I think, you know, as I said, we have a group that looks at it and see how effective they are and like, again, who's using it, like where they're going, et cetera, what types of industries,

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: And so we can provide as a resource.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: We don't make the policy decisions on these as-of-right incentives.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: And we focus on the IDA benefits that we provide for the discretionary benefits that are significant and meaningful.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: So with the savings of $2.1 million, that's directed just at the marketing.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: The actual programs are not expected to be decreased?

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: Correct.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: Great.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: And just having been an EDC alum can't overstate how important it is to have those intentional strategy investments and programs that are trying to create jobs here in New York.

chapters.summary: Maloney asks about timeline, direction from Deputy Mayor Hsu, and impact on morale. Peck reports no timeline received, works closely with Deputy Mayor Hsu multiple times weekly, and staff remain focused and passionate.
chapters.title: Maloney presses on timeline for filling permanent EDC president

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: I appreciate hearing about everything you've been doing, and especially over the past couple of months as the interim president, but wanted to at least touch on the fact that we don't have a permanent president in EDC.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: In December last year, the former CEO and President Kimball announced he was going to be departing.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: We're now almost at June, six months, and I'm wondering, I know I have full confidence in

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: the abilities of the acting leadership, but as we're talking about long-term economic growth, how are you to plan for those multi-year conversations without the formal authority of being deemed the president?

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: Yeah, thank you.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: We appreciate that question.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: Yes, it's been a few months now, and it's really been a privilege of a lifetime.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: I did not expect to serve this role, and it's been a tremendous experience.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: I've had a real, it's been a real pleasure, and it's been a great experience for me to work with all the EDC teams.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: As you know, it's an incredibly talented group of individuals that have such enormous specialties in such a diverse number of areas.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: I've also really been able to work closely with Deputy Mayor Julie Hsu and her team as well as the mayor.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: And so we really have not stopped.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: We have completely moved forward, not only in the sort of our everyday work of managing 224 assets and 60 million square feet and all of the programs that we do have in terms of innovation industries and workforce development

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: and our whole capital team, NYC Ferries.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: I mean, I could go on and on and on.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: The economic monthly snapshot.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: But we have also done a tremendous amount for the mayor's new initiatives, such as the modular bathrooms and NYC groceries, which is an incredible, incredible initiative that really has...

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: Pulled together everything great about EDC, between real estate transactions to strategy to asset management.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: And yeah, so we haven't stopped.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: We continue to move forward and we continue to, and ultimately the president is the mayor's decision.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: And so we look forward to his decision.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: Have you received a timeline or an update on where we are with filling the vacant role?

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: No.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: It's all business.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: We just do business.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: And how regularly are you receiving direction from Deputy Mayor Hsu or from the administration?

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: Oh, we work very closely with Deputy Mayor Hsu.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: We're constantly working with her multiple times a week.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: We have meetings and ongoing conversations.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: And then the mayor has also been very involved in the NYC Grocery Source Initiative, so we work with him directly as well.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: Do you have any sense of the cause for delays?

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: No.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: And is it impacting day-to-day morale at all in EDC's offices?

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: No, I think, again, it's an incredibly special group of people that are really committed to the mission of what they're doing.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: And so everyone's staying focused and really trying to move.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: In fact, everyone's so passionate about what they're working on that they just continue moving forward at the speed that they have normally done.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: Well, we're all eager to see this position filled, but you also seem great.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: So I want to move on to.

chapters.summary: Maloney asks about criteria for selecting homes for city-backed insurance. Peck explains they target the middle 60% of affordable housing stock, avoiding both best performers and worst risks, to avoid taking on extra risk.
chapters.title: Maloney on insurance selection criteria and risk pool

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: What was already mentioned, but the city-backed insurance.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: On the expense side, this is the biggest shift from the prelim budget to now.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: And I'm encouraged by, I've got it actually printed out, the block-by-block plan.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: And, you know, housing affordability is a top priority of the council as well.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: So I appreciate the effort to address one of the highest costs, which is in insurance.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: When we're talking about the criteria for which homes are going to receive insurance, city-backed insurance through the program, what's the selection criteria?

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: Yeah, thank you.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: That's an important question.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: We thought about this a lot.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: It's sort of, if you look at the pool of affordable homes, whether they're new affordable homes or existing stock, we didn't want to have the best performing, so to speak, and we didn't want to be the insurer of last resort.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: We want to sort of take the middle block, so let's call it the middle 60%.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: That is where we're, that's the stock that we will be insuring.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: And so HPD will be looking at their portfolio and sort of their pipeline for bringing affordable housing, and they'll direct us in terms of what, but that's the criteria.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: It's sort of the middle group.

chapters.summary: Maloney asks how the program reverses cost trends and about city liability for catastrophic events like Hurricane Sandy. Peck says they'll tap the reinsurance market for catastrophic risks and hope to set competitive standards.
chapters.title: Maloney on reversing cost trends and catastrophic liability

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: And so how will limiting participation to that kind of subset of owners impact the overall risk pool?

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: So that's why we're picking that sort of middle selection, because we're not taking on any extra risk.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: What we're doing is we're offering this lower cost because we're not having the overhead and the profit and the cost of capital that the insurance companies have.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: So it's not about taking on more risk or liability.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: It's just about doing it more efficiently because the city doesn't have the same situation as these private insurers.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: The report, and you mentioned this before, how we hope that this is going to reverse cost trends and then ultimately subsidize the insurance program.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: I'm just wondering if you could speak a little bit more to how this will reverse cost trends.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: So we're hoping that this sort of sets a standard and becomes competitive.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: But what we're really hoping is that it's going to be able to leverage other investors to be part of this insurance solution.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: And also, we expect that it's going to help reduce the subsidy needed to bring on HPD housing, which will allow us to bring more housing.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: So, for example, about 500 and 700 million savings brings about an additional 2,000 units of affordable housing.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: So in the case that there were something like a Hurricane Sandy, which caused billions of dollars in damage, would the city be liable for claims relating to those types of damages?

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: Or if it's any damage related to the distress of the building, just to understand the operations?

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: Yeah, thank you for that.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: That's an important question.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: We are going to look for the reinsurance market.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: This is typically what insurer providers do.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: They reinsure sort of certain...

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: Like certain risks.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: And so we're going to tap into the reinsurance market for those like catastrophic sort of, we haven't made the decision exactly, but that's an option for us for that very reason.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: The question comes at trying to secure the city from potentially, as you said, having higher risk for a budget deficit down the line if an event were to happen in the city's life.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: Exactly, and that's where the reinsurance market, I think, can be really effective, and so we're going to be looking into that.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: That's part of the whole plan for the insurance program.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: My last question on the insurance is the executive plan includes $20 million in FY27 and then $40 million following.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: Does that include the costs for staffing and operating the programs?

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: Yes, we're going to have a third-party operator, so there'll be...

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: A minimal cost to, for that operator.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: Yeah, so that'll be included in the $100 million.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: And would that operator be responsible for conducting inspections or determining risk at each of the sites?

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: sites that were?

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: Yeah, there's going to be an actuary who's going to help us sort of assess the risk, so that has been procured.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: And then we'll have to work out the actual agreement.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: But it's not like the city or EDC is going to be assessing the risks for these housing.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: It's going to be experts.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: Great, thank you.

chapters.summary: Maloney asks why $700M was shifted from the five-year to ten-year capital plan. Peck explains this is a normal OMB exercise to smooth capital costs, with no project delays and flexibility to pull funds forward.
chapters.title: Maloney on capital plan timeline changes and project impacts

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: I had one question on the five-year versus the 10-year capital plan that I alluded to in my opening remarks, and then I'm gonna turn to Shanel Thomas-Henry.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: But the five-year capital plan decreased over 700 million in the executive budget, while the 10-year plan remained unchanged.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: I'm wondering if you can speak to why a substantial amount of funding was extended into the out years and

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: if at all there are any impacts of sequencing of some of the projects that are really important that EDC manages.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: Yes, thank you.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: So this is a normal, regular exercise that OMB has each agency do for their capital, 10-year capital plan, and they need to smooth out or stretch out the capital costs each of the years.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: And so those projects that were selected either that they didn't need to be in the timeline that they were, so they were able to stretch it out, or it's a very...

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: It's a very fluid capital plan, so if we need it, we can always pull that money forward.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: It's not like set in stone, so it's very flexible.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: But in terms of just putting out the plan, they needed to smooth it out.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: And they do this with all the agencies.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: So there's no impact on the timeline of completion of those major projects?

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: No, it's not like it's going to delay it in any way.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: Great.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: And also no projects are being prioritized over others or no shifts on that front?

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: No.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: If it's approved, then we're...

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: We can sort of move it as it needs to be moved.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: And now turning to Councilmember Shanel-Thomas-Henry.

chapters.summary: Thomas-Henry asks about $7.1M rollover for the Cannabis Impact Fund and why no loans were distributed this fiscal year. Peck explains the two-phase structure, with phase two expanding to 540 eligible businesses and loans starting fall 2026.
chapters.title: CM Thomas-Henry on Cannabis Impact Fund delays

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: Thank you, Chair.

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: So I've been doing a series of business tours, and I had the privilege of visiting a few cannabis businesses.

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: So in the executive budget, it rolls over $7.1 million from fiscal year 2026 to fiscal 27 for the Cannabis Impact Fund

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: because no loans were distributed this fiscal year as the program transitioned.

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: While I appreciate the expansion of eligibility, what specific challenges caused the delay and what assurances can you provide that these funds will reach businesses expeditiously in 27?

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: Yes, thank you.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: So we structured the cannabis loan fund into two phases.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: The first phase was about $1.4 million.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: There was about like 200 or so businesses that had these licenses.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: They were sort of just as involved individuals were the owners.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: And that we provided 14 loans, about 80 applied.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: Phase two is sort of expanding, like you said, and the cap on that loan was about 100.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: We've expanded in phase two to 540 businesses that are eligible.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: And so far we have had about 60 apply.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: Again, it's a rolling sort of application.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: And we're estimating, and the cap on the size of those loans are 500,000.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: So they're higher loan amounts.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: We're expecting that we're going to be providing loans starting in the fall of this year and through like 2028.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: So it's going to be sort of an ongoing program.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: And about 26, we're estimating about, projecting about 26 loans will be given out on that.

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: Okay, and how is EDC tracking where the dollars go geographically?

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: Like, can you provide data on whether communities that were most impacted by cannabis enforcement, including communities of color, specifically in Queens, Brooklyn, and the Bronx, are receiving a proportionate share of those dollars?

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: Yeah, we can provide that information for you.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: Yeah.

chapters.summary: Thomas-Henry asks about RISE program outreach to small businesses in climate-vulnerable neighborhoods and asks Epstein's question about Innovation East. Peck confirms Innovation East is moving forward with no alterations to the plan.
chapters.title: Thomas-Henry on RISE program and Innovation East (for Epstein)

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: The RISE program.

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: So the executive budget includes additional funding for the RISE program, which helps businesses adopt innovative climate resilient technology.

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: How is EDC ensuring that small businesses in climate vulnerable neighborhoods like Corona and East Elmhurst in my district are aware of and able to access these resources, particularly those that

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: may lack the capacity to navigate complex application processes and kind of piggybacking on what Councilmember

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: Lisa said the language barrier often times.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: Yes.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: So this is a federally funded program.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: And in the implementation of how the RISE program was, I'll have to get back to you on that.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: Because I'm not familiar exactly how it's implemented for small businesses.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: But I know it's for small businesses to promote climate resilience.

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: Chair, if it's okay, I do want to ask before my time expires.

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: Councilmember Epstein had to leave but had a question.

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: Do you mind if I ask his question before I go?

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: Sure, go ahead.

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: Innovative East, Innovation East was approved in 2025.

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: Is EDC looking at this project to do this project at all?

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: Okay, I'm sorry, say it.

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: Innovation East.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: Yes.

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: Was approved in 2025.

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: Again, this is Councilmember Epstein.

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: Is EDC looking at that project?

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: And if you are looking at it, is it the same plan or have any alterations been made of the plan?

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: There's no alterations to the plan.

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: But you are still looking forward to moving it forward?

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: Yes.

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: Councilmember Brewer?

chapters.summary: Brewer asks why the city isn't using the existing Milford insurance program and raises concerns about AI training for workers, citing David Greenfield's mandatory Friday AI training program at Met Council.
chapters.title: CM Brewer on insurance versus Milford program and AI training

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Thank you very much.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: On the insurance, I mean, may he rest in peace, Dick Ravitch and now John Crotty, they have the Milford program.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Why are we not just using that insurance, which is supposed to be for affordable housing?

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: I think we believe that

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: Having it be a city-backed creates efficiencies that the private sector can't benefit from, which is the overhead and the profit margin as well as the cost of capital.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: I think Milford did have, but it was not successful.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: So we came in and came up with a solution with city capital.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Okay, I'd like to hear more about that.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: Oh, see, tax levy, sorry, not capital.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: I want to hear more about that from John, but okay.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: The other question I have is just in terms of AI.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: I know you heard about it from the speaker, but what, I mean, I'm supportive generally, but I know that the person who's doing most is David Greenfield, who has a large nonprofit.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Working with people who are trying to get food, and he trains every single Friday.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: He has a massive training for his employees on AI mandated.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: So my question is, what are we doing as a city to try to do something similar?

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: so that people are part of the solution and not left behind.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: Yeah, I think this is something, AI is something that we are keeping a close look at.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: We do have at EDC some programs, probably not on the scale that would be incredibly impactful, but we do have some library programs that are educating people to get to communities through the libraries.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: We have AI Nexus, which is helping some small businesses.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: Can learn about AI and how it impacts their businesses.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: So we are doing some of that, but it's something that we need to keep a close watch on and dynamically.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: So you don't have any training money to do what David Greenfield's doing for Met Council?

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: We don't have that right now.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: In terms of specific issues,

chapters.summary: Brewer raises issues about the John Street lot for South Street Seaport, opposition to Pier 19, and asks why the Bloomingdale Library project can't be 100% affordable housing instead of mixed-income.
chapters.title: Brewer on South Street Seaport and Bloomingdale Library

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: South Street Seaport, they need John Street lots.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: What are you doing about that?

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: Yeah, so we have a close partnership with South Street Seaport, and we're always looking to help them, you know, with any opportunities or issues that they have.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: So we'll continue to work with them.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: They need the lot.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: Yeah, we'll be talking to them about it.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: We haven't worked with that in particular, but we have a very good relationship with them, and we constantly help them with the opportunities that they have.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Okay, and same thing with Pier 15 and Pier 19.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Why do we need a Pier 19?

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: What's going on with the piers in the South Street Seaport?

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: I'll have to get back to you about Pier 19.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Okay, we don't like Pier 19.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: We want to keep Pier 15 and Pier 16 because

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: That helps the South Street Seaport Museum.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: That's a historic area.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: It's not to be something else.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: Understood.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: I'm not going to give up on the South Street Seaport Museum.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: It wouldn't be there if I hadn't bust.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: And I will continue.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: The Bloomingdale Library, that's your project.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: Yes.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: But we would like it to be, it's on city-owned land.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Why can't it be 100% affordable?

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: So if it's 100% affordable, it would be in the HPD pipeline.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: We'd like it to go to HPD and get it away from EDC.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: So part of the strategy to bring housing online faster is using the mixed income model so we can cross-subsidize.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: I'm familiar with it.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: I'm from Manhattan.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: So, and as you know, there are only certain areas where this kind of works, and this is one of those areas.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: And it's important for us to bring housing stock on fast.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: As you know, there's a real housing shortage.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: The decision was made that this was going to be the sort of mix.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: It's not going to, there may be other issues that come up, so it may not happen as fast as you think.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: And then at some point get back to me about my beach.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: Yes, there was a study that was done in 2015, and we're continuing to look at it.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: I think the study came back that we weren't ready for it, but we'll continue to look at it.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: I wanted to be able to go to the water.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: You know that?

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: Mm-hmm.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Okay, thank you.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: I could go on and on, but I'll stop.

chapters.summary: Lee asks Palladino's questions about grocery prices, whether name brands will be carried, and product variety. Peck confirms it will operate like a regular grocery store with only the core basket discounted.
chapters.title: Lee relays CM Palladino questions on grocery store prices and brands

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, I just had a really quick question on behalf of Councilmember Palladino, who was watching online.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Just in terms of the grocery stores, can you just, I'll ask them all at once.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And I think you actually answered some of these, but for the record, I just wanted to note her questions.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: She was asking about the prices and what they're going to be, which I know you said were certain percentages.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And then are you going to be carrying name brands?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: If not, what can we expect to see on the shelves?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Are people going to have the vast amount of options that we have in our grocery stores now?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And I think you sort of answered that question, but if you could again, just for the record.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: Yeah, a lot of that's going to be worked out when we have the third-party grocery opportunity, but basically it is how we're envisioning that it's going to be like a regular grocery store with this core basket being discounted.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And mostly focused on fresh foods, healthy items, those types of things.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: And everyday groceries.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And not going to overlap.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: There's going to be items with bodegas at bodegas that are not going to be there and grocery store items that are not going to be in those, but they can still get elsewhere in the local supermarkets, right?

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: Say that again.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So in other words, there's going to be some overlap in items, but a lot of them are,

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: because they're mostly focused on fresh items, healthy foods, a lot of it is, you know, in other words, the local stores and bodegas are still going to be carrying things at that supermarket.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Will not.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Yes.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: So, yes, there will be things that will not be offering in the NYC groceries that are big in brothels, like lottery tickets to pick up products, alcohols, things like that.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: But we will only be discounting a core basket.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: And sort of, as we understand, the sort of the highly profitable items for bodegas are not going to be discounted.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, perfect.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: I just was, I used to buy live chickens at that location.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: I don't want to tell you what happened to them after I purchased them, but at that location.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Thank you very much.

chapters.summary: Lee asks how prices stay down with name brands and relays concerns from local grocery store operators about competition. Peck explains the subsidy structure of no rent, no property tax, and city-funded fit-out directs savings to core basket.
chapters.title: Lee on keeping grocery prices down and bodega impact

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And then just really quickly, how will you be keeping prices down if they're name brand?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And I'm assuming it's because of the tax breaks and incentives that are going to go back into the...

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Lowering the cost, but if you could clarify that question.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: Yeah, so again, the tenant fit out, the grocery store wouldn't have to pay for that.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: They're not going to have to pay for rent or for property tax.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: And we're going to, because it's city controlled, we're going to ensure that that subsidy, so to speak, for lack of a better word, is going to go directly into the discounted basket.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, great.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And her last question was just, and I think the speaker actually asked a very similar question about whether you're taking into consideration the mom and pop stores around the neighborhoods and partnering with them.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: Yes, so we've had extensive outreach once a site has been announced and we'll continue to do so.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: Both, again, it doesn't stop.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: Once we open the

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: The grocery store will still have engagement as well as throughout the operations.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: It's going to be an ongoing relationship.
