utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, perfect.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And with that, actually, Gail, did you want to ask more questions before we...

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: I just want to talk about life sciences.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: I'm really impressed with your program that works with young people who are getting into the business.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: That's a great program.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: I know Westside Strategies runs it.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: So my question is, is there a cut to life sciences in terms of what you're doing?

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: How do you see that as a growing industry and bringing, hopefully, people who are from CUNY or other backgrounds that wouldn't necessarily get in?

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: How are you looking at that?

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: Yeah, so I think you're referring to the Life Science Internship Program, which is a very successful, it's like we're going in our 10th year, so we're super excited about that, and so we have it this summer.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: And we continue to have life science programs as well, both in terms of capital that we've

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: had, but also in these workforce development, including the life science internship, but we also have IDA benefits that we've allocated specifically for life science companies, and so our IDA team is open for business for life science companies to come.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: So was there a cut to the budget for life sciences, or is it maintained the same?

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: There hasn't been as, well, we had a life science expansion fund that was part of our chief savings officer fiscal 26, but we still have $500,000.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: That was specific, again, as you know, that...

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: DC does not get a lot of city tax levy, so for us to find something was really hard.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: But we were sort of re-strategizing about that fund anyway because it had been an investment, and then we did a forgivable loan, and we only had like three we had given and only two now.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: And so we're trying to rethink that.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: Again, we still have $500,000, but we thought that that would be sort of where we could cut, not cutting these internship programs.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: And also we have SPARC coming.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: So that's a huge life science campus, as you know.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: And so we are going to be putting on a par fee for E73rd this summer.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: So I hope you'll be looking forward to that.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: And then phase two of SPARC, which will have a lot of commercial life science after E73rd.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Okay, and then just quickly, how do you work with CUNY, with Ken Adams or anybody else?

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: In other words, we all are saying CUNY should be the bedrock to help get, as the speaker said, workforce, et cetera, et cetera.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: So how do you work?

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Obviously, SBS works, other agencies work, but how does EDC work, if at all, with CUNY?

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: Yeah, we have a lot of partnerships with CUNY, including these life science internships program, because that's a feeder.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: There's a lot of other type programs like that where we access CUNY students.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: But for example, SPARC, we're working very, very closely with CUNY because that's going to be their campus.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: So our team meets with them like every single week to make sure that that campus is meeting their needs.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Okay, so that's the main program that you work with with CUNY.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: Yeah, we have other programs, but that's the largest one right now.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: All right, thank you.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, great.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Sorry, just for the record, we were joined earlier by Councilmember Banks and Justin Sanchez, and I'm passing it back to you, Chair Maloney.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: Thank you.

chapters.summary: Maloney questions whether La Marqueta was the best first site, noting multiple full-service supermarkets within one mile. Peck says the site qualified based on DCP's per-capita grocery measure and development readiness.
chapters.title: Maloney on La Marqueta site selection and food desert criteria

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: I want to turn back to the questions about grocery stores that were alluded to earlier by a few of my colleagues and

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: reiterate how important addressing the food insecurity in our city is, especially with snap cuts.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: And through this budget process, I've seen so many organizations asking for funding to fill in that gap.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: I wanted to go back to the site selection, particularly for La Marqueta, where

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: it sounds like the two criteria, one is whether or not it's a public site that you already have access to, and then if it's a food desert, are those the two primary criteria for deciding the locations?

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: Another part of it is that it can be...

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: Built and online before 2029.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: So it's sort of like development ready.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: So La Marqueta, I believe, has multiple full-service supermarkets that are within one mile from the location.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: I have the USDA definition of a food desert is 500 people living more than one mile away from a supermarket.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: So just wanted to speak to if there could have been a higher need area to prioritize as the first market.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: Yeah, thank you.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: And why are we choosing that location?

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: Yeah, we had to balance a bunch of things.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: From the DCP measure of enough grocery stores for a neighborhood, it did rank quite low.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: So for the per capita that lives up there, it's a very dense neighborhood.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: That it did qualify.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: And the construction timeline for that site will be 2029?

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: Yes.

chapters.summary: Maloney asks about alternatives like FRESH, Groceries to Go, and food pantries, and whether the $30M could go further through other avenues. Peck says all programs are complementary, with NYC groceries offering city-controlled subsidies.
chapters.title: Maloney on alternative food programs and grocery store economics

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: I want to also talk about some of the alternative programs to address the issue of food security.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: So there's Fresh, which provides tax and zoning incentives for private supermarkets.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: Groceries to Go, which gives a 50% discount for fresh produce.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: Of course, the traditional food pantry.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: And if we looked at, as a city government, at these alternative options and also how far $30 million would go through these other...

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: avenues.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: Yes, so these are all complementary programs.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: They do serve different purposes and you know I can go through the fresh program if you if you want but because that's that's run by IDEA

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: but the idea of the NYC grocery stores is that it's city controlled and so that the subsidy that we're providing through the tenant fit out no rent and no property tax by the third party operator is going to go

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: directly because we control it we're sure that it's going to go directly to the food affordability of the core basket that's you know and also we want to create

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: these like pleasant dignified places to shop and good paying jobs for the workers there so um all that combined uh

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: It's one sort of effort to create food affordability and accessibility throughout the city.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: That's not like the only solution.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: All the other ones that you have mentioned are also, we're all working together to sort of meet those goals, having different priorities or levers.

chapters.summary: Maloney cites examples of public grocery stores operating at deficits nationally and asks about ongoing costs and self-sustainability. Peck says the goal is self-sustaining operations with the subsidy structure, with details to be worked out in the RFP.
chapters.title: Maloney on grocery store operating costs and sustainability

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: And the $30 million for the La Marquette build-out, that's just the construction, not the actual operation.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: Is that right?

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: Correct.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: But again, the operation is going to be done by the third-party operator.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: So we're giving the sort of subsidy for that ground-up construction.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: To the operator to pass on the subsidy for the core basket.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: Most models, if not all models, for public grocery stores in the United States operate at a deficit.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: So there's, of course, the capital expenditure to get the grocery store up and running.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: But then what is the projection of the ongoing costs?

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: Are we expecting these will turn a profit, that the rent and tax abatements will...

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: Allow it to continue without needing further capital assistance.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: If you could just speak to the economic

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: Once the grocery store is up and running.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: Yeah.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: Again, we're putting in an RFI this summer, so these things are going to be worked out.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: But the goal is for this to be self-sustaining operations with, again, with the subsidies that I spoke about.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: And we're going to look at exactly how the subsidies are going to be structured for these core basket goods.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: The key thing that we really care about, one of the things that is priority to care about is that they're meaningful savings.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: And they're not only meaningful savings for these core baskets, but they're stable.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: Because as you know, food prices can fluctuate, and so we want people to be able to budget.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: They're going to know when they go to an NYC grocery store how much the eggs are, how much the milk is, how much the bread is.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: And so they can budget each week and manage their finances that way.

chapters.summary: Maloney asks what prevents higher-income shoppers from clearing out subsidized groceries. Peck confirms stores are open to all, with the RFP including performance benchmarks for affordability and good-paying jobs.
chapters.title: Maloney on access for all New Yorkers and preventing hoarding

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: And it sounds like the stores will be open to all New Yorkers.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: Yes.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: So what is to prevent a higher income shopper from clearing out all the subsidized groceries?

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: Everyone is welcome to NYC groceries.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: And will, I suppose, is there a pro forma about how these grocery stores will remain profitable or

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: Revenue neutral, not require additional subsidies.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: Again, we're still working all of those details out.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: And, you know, when we put out the operator RFP this summer, you'll see some of that information.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: And will the RFP include a performance benchmark for these grocery stores?

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: Yeah, I mean, the key to that is that they need to make it, that they're requiring them to ensure that the food is affordable for the core basket, as well as that they have good paying jobs, and it's a pleasant, good experience.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: And will it be a requirement that they're self-sustaining?

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: We're going to still work out exactly how the subsidy is going to work because we have to ensure that these are sort of long-term viable and that the subsidy is meaningful for New Yorkers.

chapters.summary: Maloney cites commissary models costing $1.4B annually with zero revenue and questions the $30M cost for 9,000 square feet at La Marqueta, roughly four times standard NYC retail construction. Peck explains it's ground-up construction under railway on a complex site.
chapters.title: Maloney on other public grocery models and capital cost per square foot

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: One of the other things I want to look at is just the history of other public grocery stores in the United States.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: So there's, and I guess have you all looked at those examples?

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: What are we doing differently here to make this pilot more successful?

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: I don't know all the models, but I know the team has looked at it.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: I think one of the key things is that we're looking at a core basket.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: We're not offering everything.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: Although it's going to be a regular grocery store, we're having a third-party operator who has experience in running grocery stores to manage all the things that are important to run a grocery store, including inventory.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: purchasing, et cetera.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: So those are the things that we made sure that we were incorporating so that they were successful.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: The examples I saw show that it costs, for 250 grocery stores globally through the Defense Commissionary Agency, it was $1.4 billion per year in federal appropriations for zero revenue.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: Another one in St.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: Paul had...

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: Zero competition nearby, but it was still a $400,000 federal subsidy.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: Yeah.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: And so I guess I'm getting at the long-term operations of this, particularly if we're putting forward a really large capital investment, we want it to be a sustainable solution that's going to...

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: Yeah, a sustainable solution.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: Yeah, yes.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: That's the commissary model, so we did look at that model.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: We're not basing our NYC groceries on the commissary model.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: Again, everything in that store is discounted.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: So this is a smaller subset of groceries.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: That will be core basket.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: On the $30 million for the 9,000 square foot build out, that's roughly four times the cost of standard New York City retail construction.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: So can you speak a bit more about why we expect it to be so expensive?

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: So this is a ground-up construction on an empty lot under sort of the railway there.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: And this is part of an entire reimagination and reimagining of La Maqueta, which spans eight blocks.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: The team looked at it very closely and budgeted that amount for city capital.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: So this is a more complex site than others.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: Correct.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: For a pilot, did we look at taking on a less complex site that would cost less?

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: We inventoried, you know,

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: So many sites, it's hard to even explain how many sites we looked at.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: And again, the criteria was, we had multi-pronged criteria, which was all the things we talked about, making sure it didn't, like for the city's measure, it didn't have enough food accessibility, that it was dense, that it had transportation,

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: and that it could be brought online by 2029, which a lot of the city sites, the developments, are not going to be available by 2029.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: So that was really important because we want to get these up and running.

chapters.summary: Maloney asks if EDC considered leasing existing grocery stores and how $70M will cover five stores when La Marqueta alone costs $30M. Peck says other sites like Peninsula are tenant fit-outs, and the private site portal is open for Brooklyn, Queens, and Staten Island.
chapters.title: Maloney on leasing existing stores and budget for five stores

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: Did you look at leasing existing grocery stores?

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: So we have a private site portal that's open right now for Brooklyn, Queens, and Staten Island.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: And so we encourage private owners to fill out the form to submit their property.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: And it can be a former grocery store or a grocery store.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: Like, you know, we're open to it.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: But again, it's going to, NYC grocery stores are going to have its own look and feel.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: So we want to make sure it's uniform across the five stores.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: So we will still have some input.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: And on the...

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: The budget, the original plan was 30, or sorry, 70 million for all five boroughs, 30 million going to La Marqueta.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: How are we going to get to five with that budget allocation?

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: So the peninsula, for example, is a tenant fit out, so it's not a ground-up construction, and we anticipate with this private site portal that we will also have

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: sites that are sort of ready to go that are already built out in terms of the base infrastructure.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: I think those are all my questions on grocery stores.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: Back to the chair.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Can I, sorry, can I just ask a follow up?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So when you say third party vendors are gonna be bidding on the RFIs and the RFPs, which type of groups are you talking about?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Like who would be viable?

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: Current grocery store operators.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Current grocery store operators, okay.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So like food bazaars or whoever it is could also bid on those to operate?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Because I do have a couple friends that are in that sort of industry, and so they were saying that the build-out compared to what they would pay is more, and obviously there's different reasons for that.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So that's why they were just wondering, you know, how this is going to sort of compete with some of their stores that are in the communities.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: I don't know if they're necessarily right next to La Marquette or some of the other ones, but I think that there were some concerns that they had expressed about that in terms of how this would impact the local grocery store company.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So I just wanted to reiterate that point that I think there is that.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Sort of concern out there, especially when you look at the cost.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And so, yeah, just wanted to uplift that point that you had brought up, Chair.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: Understood.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Yeah.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Sorry, before we go into more questions, I think Councilmember Shanel Thomas-Henry, you had a few more questions, right?

chapters.summary: Thomas-Henry asks about ferry pricing during World Cup and whether Willets Point will get ferry service. Peck confirms no price changes, notes the Rockaway Rocket costs more, and says Willets Point is being studied but has no landing currently.
chapters.title: Thomas-Henry on NYC Ferry pricing and Willets Point service

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: Yes.

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: Thank you, Chair.

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: New York City Ferry.

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: So the mayor recently announced that this summer the ferry would be increasing weekend trip frequency.

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: And opening additional summer routes and offering reservable seats.

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: Will ticket prices be changing at all during the World Cup or over the summer to account for costs associated with the increased frequency?

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: No.

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: And does the reserved seat option come with additional costs to passengers?

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: That's the Rockaway Rocket, but that's always been more than the regular.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: Ferry pass.

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: It's a specific service.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: And I would say, I would add to that, that it's increased service, but also we're going to be using larger vessels.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: So we want to make sure that we're really accommodating the greater excitement for NYC ferries over the summer.

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: And is there any funding for increased frequency or new routes?

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: I'm just going to plug Willits Point, for instance.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: So, Willits Point, we have committed to studying Willits Point as a ferry stop for our ferry forward vision plan report.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: So, and we made that commitment to the borough president as well as some other community members.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: So, we'll get back to you on sort of the outcome of that.

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: Will any service be available during this increased frequency?

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: Since there is a fan fest in that vicinity.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: There's nowhere to land or dock.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: So we can offer service where we don't already have a landing.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: We can just increase the services to places where we have a landing already.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: Got it.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: Do you have any idea of like cost to actually increase the land, put a landing there?

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: I think it's site by site.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: And so that's what we're going to study.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: Got it.

chapters.summary: Thomas-Henry asks how fan fest locations were determined and about developing the additional 40 acres at Willets Point. Peck says locations were chosen for maximum benefit and cost, and phase three discussions are open.
chapters.title: Thomas-Henry on World Cup fan fests and Willets Point housing

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: And World Cup, how were the FanFest activities and locations determined?

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: So we worked closely with the World Cup czar and City Hall through the World Cup czar to determine those.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: I think it was sort of maximum benefit.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: Also, you know, cost was a consideration.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: But we are also having some activities on our own sites, and those are the public markets as well as our largest campuses.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: So we'll be having watch parties and fan fests and cultural and community programs.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: Housing.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: So we know housing is a priority across the board.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: We had a wonderful tour of the brand new Willits Point today.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: And so on 20 acres, we were able to plan out for 2,500 units of affordable housing, a school, a soccer stadium, the like.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: There are an additional 40 acres on that site.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: Yes.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: What do you think it would take to get the city to take a closer look at that immediately for future development?

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: Yeah, thank you for that.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: We're really excited about everything that's going on in Willens Point related to the housing, 2,400 units, the soccer stadium, the school, the public realm, et cetera.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: So,

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: Phase three has not been budgeted for, as you know, or contemplated.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: There are a bunch, there's a lot of private owners on that site.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: So we would have to figure that out, but we're happy to open that discussion with you.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: It's a sort of a long term, as you know, phase one, phase two was for, it took decades.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: So we're, oof.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: Phase three will not take that long.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: Yeah, we're happy to, I mean, it's a phenomenal project, phenomenal development.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: We're happy to have more continued conversations for phase three.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: And Chair, if I may have one more question.

chapters.summary: Thomas-Henry asks about EDC's process for creating additional public markets. Peck clarifies EDC inherited existing markets and didn't create them, but is open to conversations about exploring options.
chapters.title: Thomas-Henry on creating new public markets in other districts

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: Your public markets, La Marqueta, for example, what is the process for EDC to look at of creating additional public markets in other districts?

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: Yeah, just so you understand, these markets existed for a very long time, and EDC has taken over the management for it.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: We didn't, like, create these markets on our own.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: So, but I think, you know, again, we are happy to have any conversations if you're interested in exploring that option for your district.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: Definitely am, but thank you.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And of course, Chair Maloney.

chapters.summary: Lee asks about differences between public markets operating at a loss and the new grocery stores. Peck explains public markets support small businesses with discounted rent, while grocery stores focus on food affordability through a different model.
chapters.title: Lee on public markets operating at $3.6M loss versus grocery model

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: I had one more question on retail markets.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: EDC has three existing retail markets that you operate, is that right?

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: Retail markets or public markets?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Public.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: We actually operate six, but three we directly manage.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And those operate at a loss right now, I believe, of $3.6 million in FY25.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: Correct.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So that included no direct subsidy to the food in those markets and was still operating at a loss?

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: Yes.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So can you speak to the differences in design between those retail markets and the public grocery stores and how EDC will minimize the operating subsidies in these new grocery stores?

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: Yeah, these are very different, although they're both food related, these are very different.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: These are for small businesses, sort of diverse businesses, culturally relevant businesses for that neighborhood and sort of bringing these foods and these vendors there.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: It's, you know, it has, it's a kind of a community place for people in the neighborhood to go.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: And so.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: A lot of the projects and tenants that we have in EDC assets are not market rate.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: We want to support these small businesses.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: We want to support these communities.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: And so that's what the public markets are.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: NYC Groceries is not about supporting small businesses.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: It's about running a grocery store and bringing food affordability.

chapters.summary: Lee asks for details on the $20M already spent and additional $15M for the host committee. Peck explains $20M went to planning, transportation, and logistics, while $15M funds fan fests, marketing, and cultural programming.
chapters.title: Lee on World Cup host committee contract spending breakdown

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: It's a very different model.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And can you speak to the affordability of the products in the existing?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: retail markets if there's any subsidy and how those are going to compare to the prices in the New York City grocery stores.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: So how we operate the public markets is that we give discounted rent for the tenants, for the people who have their food markets in the public markets.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: And that's how we support the small businesses and what they're doing.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: We do not monitor their pricing.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: We just provide

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: discounted rates, tenant rates for them.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So it sounds like those are more about creating jobs for small businesses and less about food security.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: Exactly.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: Well, it's also about bringing sort of those small businesses that are culturally relevant for the neighborhood.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: In La Maqueta, you'll see a lot of different kinds of stalls there, and so it's important for the neighborhood, and it's like a nice gathering area.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So switching gears, I want to talk about the World Cup, since my colleague brought it up, and acknowledge a lot of the great work that's been done since our prior hearing.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: I was on one of the ferries with the raft of the burros and loved that experience and the cultural map and a lot of the efforts to get small businesses included

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: in tourist activities while they're here.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And I did want to circle back on some of the questions we had about the contract with the host committee.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And my understanding from our prior hearings is this is a

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: on a reimbursement basis and EDC can see how those funds are being spent.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And at the time we didn't have a lot of details, so I'm wondering if you could give us a little bit more of a breakdown on how the 20 million is distributed to how it's being spent by the host committee

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: and then the additional 15 million that had been added in the 2027 preliminary plan.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: Yeah, so the $20 million has already been spent, and that was for a lot of the planning, transportation, logistics, the sort of cultural and to plan for all the activity that's happening right now.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: The $15 million is...

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: Being spent now and the scope of services there is really for these fan fests and these villages as well as for marketing like the dress the city just you'll see you know see so much going on

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: dressing the city for the world's cup and also for these for some community and cultural programming across the five boroughs

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: I also want to commend the administration for making it more affordable to go to the cup.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So the work to reduce the cost of NJ Transit and also including the bus service at a lower cost.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And part of that was securing 1,000 tickets at $50.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: The news outlets say that this did not come at any cost to the taxpayers and was released directly from the host committee.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So I just wanted to get your comment on the record on that.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: Yes, that's right.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: That is not part of the $15 million that's coming through EDC.

chapters.summary: Lee asks about $38M for reconstructing the 34th Street ferry landing. Peck confirms it's critical for safety and capacity, with construction staged to minimize disruption and the stop remaining open.
chapters.title: Lee on 34th Street ferry landing reconstruction

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And onto the ferries.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: We see there's an additional $38 million across FY29 to 2031 to support reconstruction of the 34th Street ferry landing.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Is that considered critical to improving safety for passenger operators?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Is that correct?

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: Yes, the 34th Street landing has always been a popular landing, and it's been very crowded in terms of queuing and sort of safety and accommodation.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: Also, our recent ferry optimization has increased the way we've optimized the way the boats go from station to station,

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: has increased the need for greater capacity at that site.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: So that's what we'll be addressing.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Do you know the scope of the work and the timeline for the reconstruction?

chapters.summary: Lee asks about ridership and subsidy trends. Peck reports ridership at near all-time highs despite winter disruptions, with per-passenger subsidy at $8.33, down 30% since the ferry's start, lower than other ferry systems nationwide.
chapters.title: Lee on ferry ridership trends and per-passenger subsidy

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: The timeline?

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: Not yet.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: We will get back to you.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And will the work be conducted during lowest ridership months to reduce disruption?

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: We're going to stage it so that it does have the least disruption.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: But we'll get back to you the specifics on that.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Sure.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And a follow-up, which we can cover then, is will riders be able to continue utilizing the stop while the construction is underway?

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: Yes, and we're not going to close the stop.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, great.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And will there be any impact on ferry ridership or revenue levels?

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: We're hoping, during construction, we hope not because we're going to try to stage it accordingly.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: We don't want it to impact ridership.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: Ridership is incredibly important for us, but we're also hoping that with the completion of the construction, that'll increase ridership because there'll be more people that'll be able to queue there.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And on the cost side, do you have any information you can share on ridership trends?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: I know ridership's up, I believe, but that as well as the per passenger subsidy that we expect for this year.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: Yeah, so ridership was at an all-time high when we've had a little bit of a hit because of

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: the terrible cold winter that we had.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: I don't know if you had, we had a stop service a couple of days because there was ice.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: But ridership is still very high.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: It's a really beloved, very popular way of people going from place to place and we wanted to continue to be that way.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: The Rockaway Rocket has become more and more popular as well.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: So we're really excited about how the ferries is done.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: The price per subsidy is about, price per subsidy per passenger is about $8.33, and it's lower than any other ferry passenger across the country, as well as the Long Island Railroad or Metro North.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: So we're really proud of that.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: It's down 30% since the start of the ferries, so we've worked really hard to make sure that that

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: that the price for subsidy has gone down and we're running the ferries financially efficient.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And do we expect the shifting cost of fuel to have an impact on that per passenger subsidy or an increase in costs this coming summer in particular?

chapters.summary: Lee asks about fuel hedging, the $15M operating deficit, and corporate sponsorship efforts. Peck describes a fuel hedging strategy, confirms the ferry is subsidized by EDC rental revenue, and says sponsorship submissions didn't offer sufficient value.
chapters.title: Lee on fuel costs, ferry deficit, and corporate sponsorship

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: Yeah, we do have a fuel hedging strategy.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: So some of that we've...

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: Hedged and mitigated, but I will have to get back to you on exactly the impact.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: One of the questions I have is, I believe in fiscal year 2025, there was an operating deficit of roughly 15 million.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Is that correct?

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: An operating?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Or the operating deficit of the ferry.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: So the ferry,

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: technically runs at a deficit and it's subsidized by other revenue and that is some of our rental revenue that we get for some of the assets that we manage at EDC directly goes to subsidize that ferry.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: I don't know if that's what you're referring to.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: There's not a deficit because we subsidize it.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: The deficit prior to tapping into EDC's cash reserves.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: No, I'm not sure what you're referring to, but again, this subsidy is there as a...

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: As a staple source of revenue.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: And again, it's part of our maritime assets and our 42nd Street assets that subsidize the revenue that we get from those rental properties, subsidize the ferry.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: So the deficit is basically the subsidy, but we don't have an additional deficit over that.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And is there an effort to look at increasing New York City ferry revenues to try to help that decrease that subsidy?

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: We're always looking to increase the financial efficiency of NYC Ferry.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: I think the increased ridership, the other...

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: Ways of like Wi-Fi sponsorship, et cetera, that other ways for us to increase the ferry revenue.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: Ultimately, it is going to be all transit is subsidized.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: And so we are realistic in understanding that it's such a beloved form of transit for New Yorkers that it will require a subsidy.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Earlier this year, I know there were efforts to acquire a corporate sponsor.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Did EDC receive any submissions or proposals for private entities to be a corporate sponsor and sort of help with that revenue?

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: Yeah, so we are always looking for partnerships to help NYC Ferry.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: We did receive some...

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: Submissions that did not like have a maximum benefit for for what they were offering and so we decided not to go with

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: with this with any of the submissions and have you received revenue from ferry sponsors in the past and if so how much

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: We've read some, like, inside the ferries, et cetera, and it's about 300,000.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: And in 2025, it was about 300,000.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And is that continuing?

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: Yes.

chapters.summary: Lee asks for updated figures on NYC's AI readiness and what steps EDC has taken on priorities from the AI Advantage Report. Peck says she'll need to follow up with details.
chapters.title: Lee on AI readiness and workforce development

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: My last question is around AI and tech.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: In January 2025, there was the Artificial Intelligence Advantage Report that EDC recognized New York City as the applied AI capital of the world with 40,000 AI-ready workers,

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: active investors, over 2,000 AI startups.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Do you have an updated figure on how we're performing in terms of AI readiness?

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: We will have to get back to you on that.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: I don't have that right now.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Great.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And as a follow-up, we'd love to hear what steps EDC took to accomplish some of the priorities that were outlined in that report

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: around fostering new business creation and partnership to build this AI ecosystem, developing a diverse AI-ready workforce to power the future economy, and

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: position New York as a global leader for applied AI.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So we'd love to follow up with the key steps that EDC has taken to achieve the priorities outlined in your report,

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: any programming to train workforces on the development of AI-ready skills, and any city funds being deployed to bolster the AI workforce or help develop AI-related businesses.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: So we'll get back to you on that.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Back to the chair.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And a hearing would not be complete without Councilmember Ressler.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So I'm going to toss it to him.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And then I just have a couple last questions after.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: No, no, go, go, go.

chapters.summary: Restler thanks EDC for helping manage tourism in Dumbo and expresses deep concern about the weak office market in downtown Brooklyn and Dumbo, with buildings 80% vacant, calling for new economic development strategies.
chapters.title: CM Restler on tourism management and Brooklyn office market

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Thank you, Chair Lee, for making me laugh and for doing such a great job leading these hearings.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: It's great to join you and Chair Maloney.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Thank you to the EDC team for being here as well.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: I'll say...

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: One nice thing first, which is not what I, I don't do that at every hearing.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: I am very grateful to Jennifer Sun and some of her colleagues for joining us in Dumbo recently.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: to try to help us better manage tourism in the neighborhood and EDC's willingness to

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: kind of be a creative partner and go outside of what might be its traditional roles to try and help address needs and issues in our community is appreciated.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Some other city agencies that were asked to help declined.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: So we appreciate EDC for stepping up and working with us.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: And I really want to thank you for that.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: We love Jennifer Sun as well.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: She's great, and she's really done, I've been really appreciative.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Other thing I will say, just to put a marker down, I am deeply concerned about jobs in New York City, and as I look across my district

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: on the kind of waterfront in Brooklyn, downtown Brooklyn, the office market is just incredibly weak, and we need to be developing new economic development strategies to revitalize downtown Brooklyn and Dumbo,

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: where we have buildings that are 80% vacant right now.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: So we're working on a white paper ourselves with some expert help, but really want to make this a priority partnership opportunity with EDC in the months to come,

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: where we try to ensure that our communities are really dynamic 24-7 live work communities.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: They're just filling out the live part right now.

chapters.summary: Restler strongly advocates banning non-essential helicopter travel from city helipads, citing extreme noise disruption and pollution equivalent to 40 idling cars. Peck notes EDC is looking at EV tolls as a quieter alternative pending FAA approval.
chapters.title: Restler on helicopter noise and stop the chop campaign

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: On to my topic of choice, maybe my favorite issue to raise with my friends at EDC is stop the chop.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: I know you're surprised.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: You know, we have a socialist...

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Mayor, who has committed his support for stopping the chop.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: And we'd love to start to see the elimination of non-essential helicopter travel from the city's helipads.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Last year, the Ryder Cup was in New Jersey, I think, and we had helicopters going, or no, it must have been Long Island.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: The Ryder Cup was in Long Island, excuse me, I don't follow golf.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: But I did experience it because there were helicopters leaving every five minutes from the city's helipads going over, very low, over our community and making it impossible for people to have a conversation outside on the street, on their stoop, in their backyard.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Are we expecting similar amounts of extremely disruptive helicopter travel for the World Cup?

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: So the World Cup is eight games here.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: We have, EDC runs two heliports in Midtown and then on downtown Skyport.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: The Midtown one has special permits so it can be only open for certain hours.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: The downtown, we've worked very closely with the community members to reduce those hours.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: And so it's a public.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: I'm just aware of the changes that have been made, and they have really not helped in my community, where we continue to get an enormous amount of incoming, where people struggle in Brooklyn Bridge Park.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: They struggle just to be in their own property because of the extreme noise that's created by helicopters.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: So I have— It's a public—it's a public—the downtown Skyport is a public heliport, so—

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: We cannot.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: not allow people to land.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Not allow people to land.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: You could regulate the type of travel that's happening from the heliport.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: You don't need to allow folks to go to the Hamptons or JFK on helicopter rides.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: You don't need to allow tourism trips from there.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: I certainly have no problem with people taking a helicopter if they need to, for health reasons or if there's some sort of public benefit like news helicopters or NYPD helicopters.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: But the idea that we pollute our environment, it's the equivalent of 40 cars idling on the street, one helicopter above ground, and then create such extreme noise that people can't have conversations.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: You know, so that the top one-tenth of one percent can get to the Hamptons faster or get to JFK faster, I find that deeply offensive.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: So I strongly support the banning of all non-essential helicopter travel from the heliports that you all control.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: And I'm trying to understand, can we do anything to limit the helicopter activity out for the World Cup?

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: And are there other ways that EDC is looking at limiting these types of really egregious and disruptive helicopter activity from your helipads?

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: Yeah, this isn't your FAA preview, but I would say that just looking ahead, we hear your concerns.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: And just looking ahead, we are looking to EV tolls.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: And so we have hopefully the pilot we'll get by the end of this year, and then FAA will approve like EV tolls.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: And I don't know if you came to our demonstration, but they're very, very quiet.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: I mean, I haven't flown in one, but I saw them on the east side not too long ago with some of my friends, you know, Botcher and Powers and others were out there.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: What?

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Should I not be?

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Were you out there too?

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: No?

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: No.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: She couldn't make it.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: The chair missed it.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: But I'm glad to hear that they're quieter and they're better for the environment.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: If that all works, God bless.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: I don't have any objection.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: But what I do have an objection to is the status quo and our insistence on maintaining it and allowing for these incredibly disruptive helicopters to pollute and disrupt our communities all day, every day, I think is really bad policy.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: And I think we're favoring the extreme elite at the expense of everybody else.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: And I think we should stop it.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: I think we should discontinue this helicopter activity immediately.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: And once the EV tolls are ready, bring them in and let the rich people travel around that way.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Understood.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Really quickly, a couple follow-ups.

chapters.summary: Lee asks how EDC is incentivizing private owners to submit sites for grocery stores, then raises concerns about multi-year delays in nonprofit capital project funding through EDC, with costs rising while funding stays fixed. Peck acknowledges the burden and commits to working on improvements.
chapters.title: Lee on grocery site submission portal and nonprofit capital delays

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Earlier you mentioned the grocery store site submission form is open for applications.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So how are you planning on incentivizing the private owners to possibly use their sites or their, you know, if they have properties or if they have land, how would you incentivize them to apply?

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: Yeah, so we are getting the word out there, so please help us get the word out there.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: We are doing a big marketing campaign.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: So we've had quite a lot of, we can see how many people have gone on and looked at it.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: And so it just opened.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: So we're still gathering information, but that's how we're getting the word out, just by marketing and also direct emails, because we know a lot of these property owners and brokers,

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: to ensure that the word is getting out there.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Perfect.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And just my last question, because I'd be remiss coming from the nonprofit sector without bringing up the capital projects that run through EDC from the nonprofit perspective.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Because I think we got awarded city funds in...

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: I say we because I still feel like I'm part of the nonprofit community, but got awarded funding in 2018, and they literally, I think, just got their CP like a month ago, right?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And so when I think about the fact that these organizations...

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Need the space and the renovation and the funding to provide services to the community, not for a for-profit benefit or reason.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Plus, you know, I think with costs, like if we're talking about savings and just costs, the fact that all that time has passed, you know, the costs have gone up, the funding hasn't, right?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And so what ends up happening is the scope of work has to continually decrease.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And it just, you know, is a drain on the nonprofit organizations where they have to take out high interest loans.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So the total cost of the projects end up costing the city more.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And so I'm just wondering from your perspective.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: I get that there's regulations and that there's processes that need to happen and checks and balances with OMB.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: But back then when I was still at my organization, I have to say EDC was great in the sense that the staffers we were working with at EDC felt really bad for us because a lot of it was not on us.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: We were submitting all the paperwork.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: It's just that the process itself is so cumbersome a lot of times.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And so I'm just wondering from your perspective if there are ways that we can look at perhaps

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: not foregoing regulation, but also streamlining some of it so that it doesn't take as long for these groups to get their funding.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So I just wanted to get your thoughts on that.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: Yeah, that's through our funding agreements, which is a fantastic group, and they work really closely, as you said, with the nonprofits and applicants.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: And I think...

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: You know, people really, nonprofits want to go through EDC because we take that very tailored approach and we try to move things on very quickly.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: There's very strict rules with these grants.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: It's on a reimbursement basis.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: There's so much paperwork, as you can imagine, and it has to go through OMB and OMB Council.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: So there is a lot of process.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: But yes, you know, we recognize that it can be a hardship for nonprofits.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: I mean, EDC has a lot of capital projects that we do on behalf of our assets as well as DOT parks and libraries.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: And so we're not in a not dissimilar situation.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: And so we really work hard to building a very close relationship with OMB so we can move these.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: CPs as much as we, as fast as we possibly can, but as you can imagine, it is a process with limited staff on their side.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: So, but we will continue to look at that and try to make sure that we understand the burden it has on nonprofits.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Yeah, and if there's any sort of

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: task force or group internally that can look at this periodically, I think that would be great because it's just,

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: I just, I cannot tell you how badly I feel because these organizations are just trying to do good work on the ground and then they get stuck with all this paperwork and then can't do things.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So I ended up having to be a professional fundraiser or event planner because I had so many

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: you know, like I had, you know, and it's like, okay, we just did a capital campaign and then we're doing another one and the community's like, didn't we just submit, you know, didn't we just give you guys donations for a capital campaign?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: I'm like, yeah, but you know, there's all these extra costs.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And so would love to have that feedback.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: Yeah, we'd be happy to work on that.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, perfect.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And I think, does anyone have any other questions?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Yeah.

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: Do you have more?

utterances.speaker: Jeanne Peck
utterances.text: Oh, okay.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Well, if you think of more, we can always submit after and then ask for follow-up.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So, and I think with that, we are done with, what are we on, day four of hearings?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So I can gavel out, but thank you so much.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Thank you.

summary: Joint FY27 Executive Budget Hearing covering the Department of Transportation, Department of Consumer and Worker Protection, and NYC Economic Development Corporation, chaired by Finance Chair Linda Lee with Transportation Chair Shaun Abreu, Consumer Protection Chair Harvey Epstein, and Economic Development Chair Virginia Maloney.

Key topics included DOT's $1.6B budget with investments in bus lanes, bike infrastructure, street lighting, and automated enforcement; DCWP's historic headcount expansion of 181 new positions for worker protection enforcement; and EDC's city-backed insurance program, public grocery stores, and World Cup preparations.

Council members pressed agencies on daylighting costs, camera equity data, enforcement backlogs, grocery store impacts on bodegas, ferry subsidies, helicopter noise, and nonprofit capital project delays.
title: Committee on Consumer and Worker Protection
topic: Executive Budget Hearings - Consumer and Worker Protection
